topics.title: Tree Like Reasoning, Probabilistic Reasoning and Bayes Theorem, Weighted Sum Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT862 - HEALTHCARE DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tree Like Reasoning, Probabilistic Reasoning and Bayes Theorem, Weighted Sum Approach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Tree-Like Reasoning

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Tree-Like Reasoning is a structured form of deductive and inductive reasoning in which knowledge is represented as a hierarchical tree of nodes, where each internal node represents a test on an attribute, each branch represents an outcome of the test, and each leaf node represents a class label or decision outcome. In healthcare analytics, this maps to clinical decision trees used for diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment selection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Tree-like reasoning is the foundation of expert systems in medicine (e.g., MYCIN, Internist-I, QMR). It is deterministic in its pure form but is enhanced by probabilistic methods when uncertainty is introduced.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a doctor asking a patient a series of **yes/no questions** to narrow down a disease. The first question might be "Do you have a fever?" — if YES, the doctor follows one branch; if NO, another branch. Each question splits the possibilities, much like branches on a tree, until a final diagnosis (the leaf) is reached. This is exactly how tree-like reasoning works in healthcare AI.

---

## 1.2 Probabilistic Reasoning

**Formal Definition:**
Probabilistic reasoning is the process of drawing conclusions under conditions of uncertainty using the principles of probability theory. It uses random variables, conditional dependencies, and joint probability distributions to model and infer outcomes when the information is incomplete or noisy — a situation that is **universal** in clinical practice.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 context, probabilistic reasoning forms the mathematical backbone of Clinical Decision Support Systems (CDSS), Bayesian networks, and risk-prediction models.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a weather forecast that says *"70% chance of rain"*. You are not certain it will rain, but you make a probabilistic judgment. Similarly, a probabilistic medical system might say *"85% probability of pneumonia given cough, fever, and elevated WBC count"*. The system reasons with uncertainty rather than absolute truth.

**Standard Metrics in Bold:**
- **Prior Probability = P(H)**
- **Posterior Probability = P(H/E)**
- **Likelihood = P(E/H)**
- **Evidence / Marginal Probability = P(E)**
- **Sensitivity (True Positive Rate)**
- **Specificity (True Negative Rate)**

---

## 1.3 Bayes Theorem

**Formal Definition:**
Bayes Theorem is a fundamental result in probability theory that describes how to update the probability of a hypothesis (H) when new evidence (E) becomes available. It establishes a rigorous mathematical relationship between prior beliefs and observed data, making it the cornerstone of probabilistic reasoning in healthcare.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor:** Bayes Theorem is the most heavily tested concept from Module 1 in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE). Expect either a 3-mark direct definition OR a full 14-mark numerical application.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Suppose a rare disease affects **1 in 10,000** people. A diagnostic test for this disease is **99% accurate**. If you test positive, intuition says you almost certainly have the disease — but Bayes Theorem reveals the shocking truth that your actual probability of having the disease is only about **1%**! This counter-intuitive result is why Bayesian reasoning is revolutionary in medical diagnostics.

---

## 1.4 Weighted Sum Approach

**Formal Definition:**
The Weighted Sum Approach (also called the Linear Scoring Model or Additive Weighting Method) is a multi-criteria decision-making technique in which each relevant factor (criterion) is assigned a numerical value and a weight reflecting its relative importance. The final decision score is computed as the **arithmetic sum of the products** of the weights and the factor values.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine choosing the best hospital for a surgery. You consider factors like doctor experience, cost, distance, success rate, and patient reviews. You don't treat all factors equally — **cost might be 30% important, doctor's experience 40% important, distance 10% important**, etc. The weighted sum approach formalizes this intuitive process into a single, comparable numerical score for each option.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024, the Weighted Sum Approach is treated as a **scoring function** in clinical decision support, often combined with Bayesian outputs to produce a final risk score.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tree-Like Reasoning — Operational Logic

Tree-like reasoning follows a structured decision flow:

- **Step 1 (Root Node):** Start with the entire population or problem space (e.g., all patients with chest pain).
- **Step 2 (Splitting Criterion):** Apply an information-theoretic measure (e.g., **Gini Index**, **Entropy**, or **Information Gain**) to select the most discriminative attribute.
- **Step 3 (Branching):** Partition the data based on the outcome of the chosen test.
- **Step 4 (Recursion):** Recursively apply the splitting criterion to each subset until a stopping condition is met (e.g., pure node, max depth).
- **Step 5 (Leaf Annotation):** Assign a class label, probability, or predicted outcome to each leaf.
- **Step 6 (Pruning — Optional):** Remove branches that do not provide statistical significance to avoid overfitting.

**Why Tree-Like Reasoning?**
- It is **highly interpretable** — clinicians can follow the path of decisions.
- It requires **little data preprocessing** (no normalization needed).
- It handles **both numerical and categorical** clinical data.
- It mimics the natural **differential diagnosis** process used by doctors.

---

## 2.2 Probabilistic Reasoning — Foundational Logic

The reasoning chain operates in three layers:

1. **Prior Knowledge Layer:** Encodes existing beliefs (e.g., disease prevalence in the population).
2. **Evidence / Observation Layer:** Incorporates observed data (e.g., test results, symptoms).
3. **Inference Layer:** Combines the prior and the evidence using probability calculus to update beliefs.

The core operation is the computation of **joint probability distributions** over a set of random variables, often expressed using the chain rule:

$$P(X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid Parents(X_i))$$

This factorization (used in Bayesian Networks) allows tractable inference in high-dimensional healthcare datasets.

---

## 2.3 Bayes Theorem — The Heart of Probabilistic Reasoning

### 2.3.1 Conditional Probability Foundation

The probability of event A given that B has occurred:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}, \quad \text{where } P(B) > 0$$

### 2.3.2 Bayes Theorem (Simple Form)

$$P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$$

### 2.3.3 Bayes Theorem (Extended Form with Marginalization)

When the evidence E can arise from multiple mutually exclusive causes (H₁, H₂, …, Hₙ):

$$P(H_i \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H_i) \cdot P(H_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(E \mid H_j) \cdot P(H_j)}$$

---

## 2.4 Weighted Sum Approach — Operational Logic

The Weighted Sum Approach computes a composite score as the **linear combination** of multiple weighted criteria.

**Core Formulation:**

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot x_i$$

where:
- $S$ is the composite score (decision metric)
- $w_i$ is the weight of the $i$-th factor
- $x_i$ is the normalized value of the $i$-th factor
- $n$ is the total number of factors

**Normalization Constraint (commonly applied in KTU problems):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1, \quad 0 \leq w_i \leq 1$$

**Normalization of Factor Values (Min-Max Scaling):**

$$x_i^{norm} = \frac{x_i - x_i^{min}}{x_i^{max} - x_i^{min}}$$

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Meaning | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bayes Theorem** | $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$ | $H$ = Hypothesis, $E$ = Evidence | Diagnostic test interpretation |
| **Extended Bayes** | $P(H_i \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H_i) \cdot P(H_i)}{\sum_j P(E \mid H_j) \cdot P(H_j)}$ | Multi-hypothesis posterior | Multi-disease screening |
| **Sensitivity** | $Sensitivity = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $TP$ = True Positives, $FN$ = False Negatives | Test reliability metric |
| **Specificity** | $Specificity = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | $TN$ = True Negatives, $FP$ = False Positives | Test reliability metric |
| **PPV** | $PPV = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Positive Predictive Value | Post-test probability |
| **NPV** | $NPV = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FN}$ | Negative Predictive Value | Post-test probability |
| **Weighted Sum** | $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot x_i$ | $w_i$ = weight, $x_i$ = factor | Clinical risk scoring |
| **Entropy (Decision Tree)** | $H(S) = -\sum_{c} p_c \log_2 p_c$ | $p_c$ = class probability | Best-attribute selection |
| **Information Gain** | $IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | $A$ = attribute, $S_v$ = subset | Tree split quality |
| **Odds Ratio Form** | $O(H \mid E) = O(H) \cdot \dfrac{P(E \mid H)}{P(E \mid \neg H)}$ | Odds formulation of Bayes | Risk factor analysis |
| **Chain Rule** | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$ | Joint probability | Bayesian Networks |
| **Min-Max Norm** | $x_i^{norm} = \dfrac{x_i - x_i^{min}}{x_i^{max} - x_i^{min}}$ | Scaling to [0,1] | Preprocessing for WSA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Valuation Note:** When writing Bayes Theorem in the exam, always label which term is **Prior**, **Likelihood**, **Evidence**, and **Posterior**. Examiners award 1 mark specifically for correct identification of these four components.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility in Healthcare

| Application | Technique Used | Clinical Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Cancer Screening** | Bayes Theorem | Accurate interpretation of mammography/PSA tests |
| **ICU Patient Triage** | Weighted Sum Score | Prioritizing critical care based on vital signs |
| **Differential Diagnosis** | Tree-Like Reasoning | Systematic narrowing of disease possibilities |
| **Drug Response Prediction** | Bayesian Networks | Personalized pharmacogenomic modeling |
| **Hospital Readmission Risk** | Weighted Sum + Bayes | Reducing 30-day readmission rates |
| **Epidemic Outbreak Detection** | Probabilistic Reasoning | Real-time pandemic surveillance systems |
---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Bayes Theorem from First Principles

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **guaranteed 14-mark question** in KTU ESE Module 1. Master each step precisely.

### Step 1: Starting Point — Definition of Conditional Probability

By definition, the conditional probability of event A given event B is:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}, \quad \text{provided } P(B) > 0$$

This equation (1) is the **axiomatic definition** of conditional probability. It says: the probability of A given B is the joint probability of both occurring divided by the probability of B occurring at all.

### Step 2: Symmetry of Joint Probability

The joint probability $P(A \cap B)$ can be expressed in **two equivalent symmetric forms** by applying the conditional probability definition twice:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$$

$$P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

These two expressions (2a and 2b) are equal because they both describe the same joint event — the simultaneous occurrence of A and B. The difference lies only in **which event we condition upon first**.

### Step 3: Equating the Two Expressions

Since equations (2a) and (2b) describe the same quantity $P(A \cap B)$, we can set them equal:

$$P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

### Step 4: Isolating the Posterior Probability

Our goal is to express $P(A \mid B)$ — the probability of A given B (i.e., the **posterior**). We divide both sides by $P(B)$:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

This is the **Bayes Theorem in its simplest two-event form**.

### Step 5: Mapping to the Healthcare Notation

In the clinical/medical context, the variables are relabeled as follows:
- Event A = Hypothesis H (e.g., "Patient has disease D")
- Event B = Evidence E (e.g., "Test result is positive")

After relabeling, Bayes Theorem becomes:

$$P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$$

**Interpretation of each term:**

$$P(H \mid E) = \text{Posterior Probability} = \frac{\text{Likelihood} \times \text{Prior Probability}}{\text{Evidence}}$$

- **$P(H)$ — Prior Probability:** The probability of the hypothesis *before* observing the evidence (e.g., disease prevalence).
- **$P(E \mid H)$ — Likelihood:** The probability of observing the evidence *if* the hypothesis is true (e.g., test sensitivity).
- **$P(E)$ — Evidence:** The total probability of observing the evidence, regardless of the hypothesis.
- **$P(H \mid E)$ — Posterior Probability:** The *updated* probability of the hypothesis *after* observing the evidence.

### Step 6: Extending to Multiple Hypotheses (Marginalization)

When the evidence E can be caused by **multiple mutually exclusive and exhaustive hypotheses** $H_1, H_2, \dots, H_n$, the evidence $P(E)$ must account for all possible causes. Using the **Law of Total Probability**:

$$P(E) = P(E \mid H_1) \cdot P(H_1) + P(E \mid H_2) \cdot P(H_2) + \dots + P(E \mid H_n) \cdot P(H_n)$$

$$P(E) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(E \mid H_j) \cdot P(H_j)$$

Substituting this expanded denominator into Bayes Theorem:

$$P(H_i \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H_i) \cdot P(H_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(E \mid H_j) \cdot P(H_j)}$$

This is the **most general form** of Bayes Theorem and is the version most commonly tested in KTU 2024 numerical problems.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Bayes Theorem in Medical Diagnosis

**Problem (Modeled on KTU Dec 2023 Pattern):**
A new screening test for **Diabetes Mellitus** has the following characteristics in a given population:
- Prevalence of diabetes: $P(D) = 0.05$
- Sensitivity: $P(+\mid D) = 0.92$
- False positive rate: $P(+\mid \neg D) = 0.08$

If a randomly selected patient tests **positive**, calculate:
1. $P(+\mid \neg D)$ clearly (specificity relationship)
2. The total probability of a positive test, $P(+)$
3. The posterior probability $P(D \mid +)$
4. Comment on the result.

### Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):

**Given Information (Step 0):**
- $P(D) = 0.05$ (Prior probability of diabetes)
- $P(\neg D) = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95$ (Prior probability of no diabetes)
- $P(+\mid D) = 0.92$ (Sensitivity / True Positive Rate)
- $P(+\mid \neg D) = 0.08$ (False Positive Rate)

**Part 1: Specificity**

Specificity is the True Negative Rate — the probability of a negative test given no disease:

$$P(-\mid \neg D) = 1 - P(+\mid \neg D) = 1 - 0.08 = 0.92$$

**Part 2: Total Probability of a Positive Test (Evidence)**

By the **Law of Total Probability**, a positive test can arise from two mutually exclusive paths — the patient either has diabetes or does not:

$$P(+) = P(+\mid D) \cdot P(D) + P(+\mid \neg D) \cdot P(\neg D)$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$P(+) = (0.92 \times 0.05) + (0.08 \times 0.95)$$

$$P(+) = 0.0460 + 0.0760 = 0.1220$$

**Part 3: Posterior Probability via Bayes Theorem**

Now applying Bayes Theorem:

$$P(D \mid +) = \frac{P(+\mid D) \cdot P(D)}{P(+)}$$

$$P(D \mid +) = \frac{0.92 \times 0.05}{0.1220}$$

$$P(D \mid +) = \frac{0.0460}{0.1220} = 0.3770$$

**Part 4: Interpretation**

Even with a test that is **92% accurate**, a positive result only confers a **37.7% probability** of actually having diabetes. This is because the disease is rare (only 5% prevalence), so the **false positives from the large healthy population (95%) outnumber the true positives from the small diseased population (5%)**. This is the famous **base-rate fallacy** that Bayes Theorem corrects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Examiners frequently award a 1-mark bonus comment for explicitly stating the *base-rate fallacy* insight. Always conclude your Bayes Theorem numerical with a one-sentence clinical interpretation.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Weighted Sum Approach in Clinical Risk Scoring

**Problem:**
A hospital uses a **Cardiac Risk Scoring System** with five factors. The weights and a specific patient's normalized scores are:

| Factor | Weight ($w_i$) | Patient Score ($x_i$) |
|---|---|---|
| Age | 0.15 | 0.70 |
| Blood Pressure | 0.25 | 0.85 |
| Cholesterol | 0.25 | 0.60 |
| Smoking Status | 0.20 | 0.90 |
| Family History | 0.15 | 0.50 |

Calculate the **composite cardiac risk score** for this patient.

### Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):

**Step 1: Verify the Weights Sum to Unity**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i = 0.15 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$

This validates that the weights are properly normalized — a common KTU checkpoint.

**Step 2: Apply the Weighted Sum Formula**

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot x_i$$

**Step 3: Compute Each Term Individually**

$$w_1 \cdot x_1 = 0.15 \times 0.70 = 0.1050$$

$$w_2 \cdot x_2 = 0.25 \times 0.85 = 0.2125$$

$$w_3 \cdot x_3 = 0.25 \times 0.60 = 0.1500$$

$$w_4 \cdot x_4 = 0.20 \times 0.90 = 0.1800$$

$$w_5 \cdot x_5 = 0.15 \times 0.50 = 0.0750$$

**Step 4: Sum All Weighted Contributions**

$$S = 0.1050 + 0.2125 + 0.1500 + 0.1800 + 0.0750$$

$$S = 0.7225$$

**Step 5: Clinical Interpretation**

A composite score of **0.7225 (or 72.25%)** indicates a **HIGH cardiac risk**. The patient is in the top quartile of risk and should be referred for further invasive diagnostic testing (e.g., coronary angiography).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Recognition:** Weighted Sum problems in KTU ESE always include a verification step (Step 1) and an interpretation step (Step 5). Do not skip either.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of the Three Reasoning Paradigms

| Dimension | Tree-Like Reasoning | Probabilistic Reasoning | Weighted Sum Approach |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Foundation** | Logic / Information Theory | Probability Theory | Linear Algebra / Multi-Criteria Decision |
| **Uncertainty Handling** | Limited (deterministic splits) | Native (probability distributions) | Limited (uses deterministic scores) |
| **Output Type** | Class label / Decision | Probability distribution | Single composite score |
| **Interpretability** | Very High (visual tree) | Moderate (requires math) | High (transparent formula) |
| **Data Requirement** | Moderate | Large (for joint distributions) | Moderate |
| **Scalability** | Poor (exponential growth) | Moderate (factorization helps) | Excellent (linear in factors) |
| **Clinical Use Case** | Differential Diagnosis | Test Result Interpretation | Risk Stratification |
| **KTU 2024 Weight** | 3 marks (definition) | 8–10 marks (deep) | 4–6 marks (numerical) |
---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Tree-Like Reasoning for Chest Pain Diagnosis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Patient Presents with Chest Pain"]
    nodeB{"Is Pain Cardiac in Origin?"}
    nodeC{"Does ECG Show ST Elevation?"}
    nodeD{"Are Cardiac Enzymes Elevated?"}
    nodeE["STEMI Diagnosis - Immediate Intervention"]
    nodeF["NSTEMI / Unstable Angina Pathway"]
    nodeG["Non-Cardiac Chest Pain Pathway"]
    nodeH{"Is Troponin Level High?"}
    nodeI["Myocardial Infarction Confirmed"]
    nodeJ["Monitor and Stress Test Ordered"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeC
    nodeB -- No --> nodeG
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeE
    nodeC -- No --> nodeD
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeF
    nodeD -- No --> nodeH
    nodeH -- Yes --> nodeI
    nodeH -- No --> nodeJ
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **root node** is the patient presentation.
- Each **diamond** is a decision (test/observation).
- Each **rectangle** is a diagnostic outcome or action.
- The **branches** represent the logical flow of clinical reasoning.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Probabilistic Reasoning with Bayes Theorem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    prior["Prior P H - Disease Prevalence"]
    likelihood["Likelihood P E given H - Test Sensitivity"]
    evidence["Evidence P E - Marginal Probability"]
    posterior["Posterior P H given E - Updated Belief"]

    prior --> combine1
    likelihood --> combine1
    combine1[/"Numerator P E given H times P H"/]
    combine1 --> division
    evidence --> division
    division[/"Division by P E"/]
    division --> posterior
    posterior --> clinicalAction["Clinical Decision - Treat or No Treat"]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **Prior** and **Likelihood** combine multiplicatively in the numerator.
- The **Evidence** acts as a normalizing constant in the denominator.
- The result is the **Posterior**, which drives clinical action.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Weighted Sum Approach Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    f1["Factor 1 - Age"] --> n1["Weight w1"]
    f2["Factor 2 - Blood Pressure"] --> n2["Weight w2"]
    f3["Factor 3 - Cholesterol"] --> n3["Weight w3"]
    f4["Factor 4 - Smoking"] --> n4["Weight w4"]
    f5["Factor 5 - Family History"] --> n5["Weight w5"]

    n1 --> mult1[/"Multiply"/]
    n2 --> mult2[/"Multiply"/]
    n3 --> mult3[/"Multiply"/]
    n4 --> mult4[/"Multiply"/]
    n5 --> mult5[/"Multiply"/]

    mult1 --> sum["Weighted Sum Aggregator"]
    mult2 --> sum
    mult3 --> sum
    mult4 --> sum
    mult5 --> sum

    sum --> out["Composite Risk Score S"]
    out --> decision{"S exceeds Threshold?"}
    decision -- Yes --> action1["High Risk - Refer to Specialist"]
    decision -- No --> action2["Low Risk - Routine Follow Up"]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Each clinical factor is **scaled by its weight** through a multiplication block.
- All weighted products are **summed** in the aggregator.
- The final score drives a **binary clinical decision** based on a threshold.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Integrated Healthcare Reasoning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Patient Data Input"]
    start --> tree["Tree-Like Differential Diagnosis"]
    tree --> probs["Probabilistic Belief Updating via Bayes"]
    probs --> score["Weighted Sum Risk Score"]
    score --> output["Final Clinical Recommendation"]

    tree -.-> feedback["Feedback Loop - Update Priors"]
    feedback -.-> probs
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- This represents the **integrated workflow** combining all three reasoning paradigms.
- Tree-like reasoning narrows down possibilities first.
- Probabilistic reasoning then quantifies uncertainty.
- Weighted sum converts the final belief into an actionable score.
---
