<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Bayes Theorem. Identify and explain each of its four components in the context of a medical diagnostic test.**

**Model Answer:**

Bayes Theorem is a mathematical formula in probability theory that computes the posterior probability of a hypothesis H given observed evidence E. The formula is:

$$P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$$

**Four Components:**

| Component | Notation | Clinical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Prior Probability** | $P(H)$ | Probability of disease *before* the test, usually the disease prevalence |
| **Likelihood** | $P(E \mid H)$ | Probability of observing the evidence (positive test) *if* the disease is present — i.e., **sensitivity** |
| **Evidence** | $P(E)$ | Total probability of the evidence across all possible patient states |
| **Posterior Probability** | $P(H \mid E)$ | Updated probability of disease *after* observing the evidence |

Bayes Theorem is fundamental in healthcare analytics because it allows clinicians to **rationally update diagnostic beliefs** in light of new test results, replacing intuitive guessing with mathematically grounded inference.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is the Weighted Sum Approach? Write its general formula and state the constraint that the weights must satisfy.**

**Model Answer:**

The Weighted Sum Approach (WSA) is a multi-criteria decision-making technique where each relevant factor is multiplied by an importance weight, and the results are summed to produce a single composite decision score.

**General Formula:**

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot x_i$$

where $S$ is the composite score, $w_i$ is the weight assigned to the $i$-th factor, and $x_i$ is the normalized value of the $i$-th factor.

**Weight Constraint:**

The weights must satisfy the **normalization condition**:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1, \quad 0 \leq w_i \leq 1$$

This ensures that the composite score remains within the same [0, 1] range as the individual factor values, allowing consistent clinical interpretation.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose ONE)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. In Bayes Theorem numericals, students often confuse **sensitivity** ($P(+\mid D)$) with **PPV** ($P(D\mid +)$). These are NOT the same.
> 2. In Weighted Sum problems, students frequently forget to **verify the weight normalization** constraint — losing 1 mark.
> 3. In Tree-Like Reasoning diagrams, students forget to **label the leaf nodes** with their class — losing 1 mark.
> 4. Always include the **clinical interpretation** in your final answer — it carries 1–2 marks even in numerical problems.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive Bayes Theorem from the definition of conditional probability. State the general form when there are $n$ mutually exclusive hypotheses.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hospital is evaluating a new **AI-based diagnostic tool for detecting Lung Cancer** from CT scans. The following statistics are known for a target population:
- Prevalence of lung cancer: $P(LC) = 0.02$
- True Positive Rate: $P(+\mid LC) = 0.96$
- False Positive Rate: $P(+\mid \neg LC) = 0.04$

A patient undergoes this AI test and gets a **positive** result. Calculate:
1. The probability of a negative test given no cancer, $P(-\mid \neg LC)$.
2. The total probability of a positive test, $P(+)$.
3. The posterior probability of lung cancer given a positive test, $P(LC \mid +)$.
4. Provide a clinical interpretation of your result.

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Bayes Theorem [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Definition of Conditional Probability**

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}, \quad P(B) > 0 \quad \text{...(1)}$$

[1 mark for the correct definition]

**Step 2: Two Symmetric Forms of Joint Probability**

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) \quad \text{...(2a)}$$

$$P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) \quad \text{...(2b)}$$

[1 mark for expressing joint probability both ways]

**Step 3: Equating (2a) and (2b)**

$$P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) \quad \text{...(3)}$$

[1 mark for the equality]

**Step 4: Solving for the Posterior**

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)} \quad \text{...(4)}$$

[1 mark for isolating the posterior]

**Step 5: Healthcare Notation Mapping**

$$P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)} \quad \text{...(5)}$$

[1 mark for correct relabeling]

**Step 6: Extension to n Hypotheses (Marginalization)**

Using the Law of Total Probability:

$$P(E) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(E \mid H_j) \cdot P(H_j)$$

Therefore:

$$P(H_i \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H_i) \cdot P(H_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(E \mid H_j) \cdot P(H_j)} \quad \text{...(6)}$$

[2 marks for the full generalization]

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $P(LC) = 0.02$, $P(\neg LC) = 0.98$
- $P(+\mid LC) = 0.96$, $P(+\mid \neg LC) = 0.04$

**(1) Specificity Calculation [1 Mark]**

$$P(-\mid \neg LC) = 1 - P(+\mid \neg LC) = 1 - 0.04 = 0.96$$

**(2) Total Probability of Positive Test [2 Marks]**

$$P(+) = P(+\mid LC) \cdot P(LC) + P(+\mid \neg LC) \cdot P(\neg LC)$$

$$P(+) = (0.96 \times 0.02) + (0.04 \times 0.98)$$

$$P(+) = 0.0192 + 0.0392 = 0.0584$$

**(3) Posterior Probability [3 Marks]**

$$P(LC \mid +) = \frac{P(+\mid LC) \cdot P(LC)}{P(+)}$$

$$P(LC \mid +) = \frac{0.96 \times 0.02}{0.0584}$$

$$P(LC \mid +) = \frac{0.0192}{0.0584} = 0.3288$$

**(4) Clinical Interpretation [1 Mark]**

Despite the high test accuracy (96%), a positive AI-CT result confers only a **~32.9% probability** of actually having lung cancer. This is because lung cancer is **rare** (2% prevalence), so false positives from the 98% healthy population **dwarf** the true positives. A confirmatory biopsy is essential before initiating treatment.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain Tree-Like Reasoning with a suitable medical example. Draw a labeled decision tree for diagnosing **Type 2 Diabetes** based on the following attributes: Fasting Blood Sugar (FBS), HbA1c Level, and Body Mass Index (BMI). Show all leaf nodes with their class labels.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hospital uses a **Weighted Sum Approach** to predict the 30-day readmission risk of discharged patients. The model considers four factors with the following weights and a specific patient's normalized scores:

| Factor | Weight ($w_i$) | Patient Score ($x_i$) |
|---|---|---|
| Age | 0.20 | 0.80 |
| Number of Comorbidities | 0.30 | 0.65 |
| Length of Stay | 0.25 | 0.50 |
| Medication Adherence | 0.25 | 0.90 |

Compute the **composite readmission risk score** and interpret the result (threshold for "high risk" is 0.70).

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Tree-Like Reasoning for Type 2 Diabetes Diagnosis [7 Marks]

**Explanation [3 Marks]:**
Tree-Like Reasoning is a hierarchical, top-down decision-making process where a problem is decomposed into a series of attribute-based tests. Each internal node tests an attribute, each branch represents the outcome, and each leaf represents a final decision or class label. In medicine, it mimics the natural process of **differential diagnosis** performed by clinicians.

**Decision Tree Diagram [4 Marks]:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    a1["Patient with Suspected Diabetes"]
    a2{"FBS greater than 126 mg per dL?"}
    a3{"HbA1c greater than 6.5 percent?"}
    a4{"BMI greater than 30?"}
    a5["DIAGNOSIS - Type 2 Diabetes Confirmed"]
    a6["Probable Type 2 Diabetes - Order OGTT"]
    a7["At Risk - Lifestyle Intervention"]
    a8["No Diabetes - Routine Screening"]

    a1 --> a2
    a2 -- Yes --> a3
    a2 -- No --> a4
    a3 -- Yes --> a5
    a3 -- No --> a6
    a4 -- Yes --> a7
    a4 -- No --> a8
```

**Labeling Requirements Met:**
- Root node: Suspected patient
- Internal nodes: FBS, HbA1c, BMI tests
- Leaf nodes: Four clearly labeled outcomes

#### Part (b) — Weighted Sum Computation [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Verify Weight Normalization [1 Mark]**

$$\sum w_i = 0.20 + 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.25 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2: Apply the Weighted Sum Formula [1 Mark]**

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i \cdot x_i$$

**Step 3: Individual Product Calculations [3 Marks]**

$$w_1 \cdot x_1 = 0.20 \times 0.80 = 0.1600$$

$$w_2 \cdot x_2 = 0.30 \times 0.65 = 0.1950$$

$$w_3 \cdot x_3 = 0.25 \times 0.50 = 0.1250$$

$$w_4 \cdot x_4 = 0.25 \times 0.90 = 0.2250$$

**Step 4: Sum and Compare to Threshold [1 Mark]**

$$S = 0.1600 + 0.1950 + 0.1250 + 0.2250 = 0.7050$$

**Step 5: Clinical Interpretation [1 Mark]**

Since $S = 0.7050 > 0.70$ (threshold), the patient is classified as **HIGH RISK** for 30-day readmission. The hospital should assign a **case manager**, schedule a **follow-up within 7 days** of discharge, and ensure **medication reconciliation**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Use this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Tree-Like Reasoning** = Hierarchical decision structure with root, internal (decision) nodes, and leaf (outcome) nodes. Uses Entropy, Gini Index, Information Gain for splitting.
- **Probabilistic Reasoning** = Reasoning under uncertainty using random variables and joint probability distributions. Backbone of Bayesian Networks.
- **Bayes Theorem (Simple):** $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$
- **Bayes Theorem (General):** $P(H_i \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H_i) \cdot P(H_i)}{\sum_{j} P(E \mid H_j) \cdot P(H_j)}$
- **Prior = $P(H)$** | **Likelihood = $P(E \mid H)$** | **Evidence = $P(E)$** | **Posterior = $P(H \mid E)$**
- **Sensitivity** = $P(+\mid D)$ (True Positive Rate) | **Specificity** = $P(-\mid \neg D)$ (True Negative Rate)
- **PPV** = $P(D \mid +)$ ≠ Sensitivity — do not confuse.
- **Weighted Sum Formula:** $S = \sum w_i \cdot x_i$ with constraint $\sum w_i = 1$
- **Min-Max Normalization:** $x^{norm} = (x - x_{min}) / (x_{max} - x_{min})$
- **Base-Rate Fallacy:** Even highly accurate tests give low PPV for rare diseases — this is the key insight of Bayes Theorem.
- **Law of Total Probability:** $P(E) = \sum_j P(E \mid H_j) \cdot P(H_j)$ — used in the denominator of extended Bayes.
- **Odds Form of Bayes:** $O(H \mid E) = O(H) \cdot \dfrac{P(E \mid H)}{P(E \mid \neg H)}$ — used in epidemiological studies.
- **Chain Rule:** $P(A, B, C) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) \cdot P(C \mid A, B)$ — basis of Bayesian Network factorization.
- **Always include clinical interpretation** in your final answer — it's worth 1–2 marks.
- **Always verify weight normalization** in Weighted Sum problems — it's worth 1 mark.
- **Always label the four components** of Bayes Theorem explicitly — it's worth 1 mark.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensitivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT862 - HEALTHCARE DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensitivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Sensitivity

In **Healthcare Data Analytics**, **Sensitivity** is a fundamental **diagnostic accuracy metric** that quantifies the proportion of *actual positive cases* (patients who truly have a disease) that a screening or diagnostic test correctly identifies as positive. Formally, it is the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** or **Recall** of a binary classification system.

$$
\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{\text{True Positives (TP)}}{\text{True Positives (TP)} + \text{False Negatives (FN)}}
$$

It answers the critical clinical question: *"Of all the patients who actually have the disease, how many did our test successfully catch?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Sensitivity is studied under the broader umbrella of **Confusion Matrix Metrics** and **Diagnostic Test Evaluation**. It is one of the four cardinal performance indicators, alongside **Specificity**, **Precision (PPV)**, and **Negative Predictive Value (NPV)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sniper & Net" Intuition

Imagine a hospital lab is testing blood samples for a rare virus. The lab uses a "diagnostic net" to catch infected samples:

- **True Positives (TP)** = Sick patients the net *correctly catches*. ✅
- **False Negatives (FN)** = Sick patients the net *misses* (they slip through). ❌
- **False Positives (FP)** = Healthy patients the net *accidentally catches* (false alarm).
- **True Negatives (TN)** = Healthy patients the net *correctly ignores*.

> **Sensitivity** asks: *"How good is the net at catching every sick patient?"*
> If the net has **high sensitivity**, it rarely lets a sick patient slip through — minimizing dangerous **False Negatives** (missed diagnoses).

A test with **100% sensitivity** is a *perfect net* — it catches **every** sick patient. In medical screening (e.g., for HIV, Cancer, COVID-19), high sensitivity is essential because a *missed diagnosis* (False Negative) can be life-threatening.

## 1.3 Physical Range and Standard Metrics

- **Range:** $\text{Sensitivity} \in [0, 1]$ (or equivalently $0\%$ to $100\%$).
- **Optimal value:** **1.0 (100%)** — the test detects all diseased individuals.
- **Worst value:** **0.0 (0%)** — the test detects no diseased individuals (worthless for ruling in disease).
- **Unit:** Dimensionless ratio (probability/proportion).

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Rule of Thumb:** *A highly sensitive test is best used to **rule OUT** a disease* (because a negative result reliably means "no disease"). This is the famous **SnNout** mnemonic: **S**e**N**sitive test, **N**egative result, rules **OUT**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Confusion Matrix Foundation

Sensitivity is derived from the **2×2 Confusion Matrix**, which cross-tabulates *predicted* versus *actual* class labels in any binary classification problem.

| Dimension | Description |
|---|---|
| **TP (True Positive)** | Test = Positive, Reality = Diseased (Correct Hit) |
| **FN (False Negative)** | Test = Negative, Reality = Diseased (Missed Case — Type II Error) |
| **FP (False Positive)** | Test = Positive, Reality = Healthy (False Alarm — Type I Error) |
| **TN (True Negative)** | Test = Negative, Reality = Healthy (Correct Rejection) |

Sensitivity is fundamentally a **row-wise** metric — it conditions on the **actual positive (diseased) population** and measures how well the test recovers them.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step by Step

1. **Define the Disease Ground Truth:** Identify all patients with a confirmed diagnosis (the *reference standard*, e.g., biopsy for cancer).
2. **Apply the Diagnostic Test:** Run the new screening tool on every patient.
3. **Tabulate Outcomes:** Place each patient into one of the four cells of the confusion matrix.
4. **Sum the Disease Row:** Total diseased = $\text{TP} + \text{FN}$.
5. **Compute Sensitivity:** Divide correctly identified positives (TP) by the total diseased.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Metric** | **Formula** | **Clinical Meaning** | **Range** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensitivity (TPR / Recall)** | $\dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$ | Of all diseased, how many caught? | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $\dfrac{\text{TN}}{\text{TN} + \text{FP}}$ | Of all healthy, how many correctly cleared? | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Precision (PPV)** | $\dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}}$ | Of all flagged positive, how many truly sick? | $[0, 1]$ |
| **NPV** | $\dfrac{\text{TN}}{\text{TN} + \text{FN}}$ | Of all flagged negative, how many truly healthy? | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Accuracy** | $\dfrac{\text{TP} + \text{TN}}{\text{TP} + \text{TN} + \text{FP} + \text{FN}}$ | Overall correct classifications | $[0, 1]$ |
| **F1-Score** | $\dfrac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Harmonic mean of Precision & Recall | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Miss Rate (FNR)** | $1 - \text{Sensitivity} = \dfrac{\text{FN}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$ | Proportion of diseased missed | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Likelihood Ratio (+ve)** | $\dfrac{\text{Sensitivity}}{1 - \text{Specificity}}$ | Diagnostic power of a positive test | $> 1$ ideal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensitivity and Specificity are inversely related** through the **ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curve**. As the decision threshold lowers, sensitivity increases but specificity falls. The **AUC (Area Under Curve)** summarizes overall discriminative ability.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Healthcare

- **Screening Programs:** Mass screening for **TB, HIV, Diabetes, Breast Cancer (Mammography)** demands high sensitivity to avoid missing cases in the general population.
- **ML Model Validation:** Any healthcare AI/ML classifier (e.g., predicting heart failure from EHR data) is reported using sensitivity in clinical journals.
- **Medical Device Regulation:** The **FDA** and **CE marking** require sensitivity benchmarks for diagnostic device approval.
- **Epidemic Surveillance:** During the COVID-19 pandemic, **RT-PCR test sensitivity** determined outbreak control effectiveness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Derivation of Sensitivity from Probabilistic Foundations

Let $D$ denote the event *"patient has the disease"* and $+T$ denote *"test result is positive"*.

- **Sensitivity** is the conditional probability:
$$
\text{Sensitivity} = P(+T \mid D) = \frac{P(+T \cap D)}{P(D)}
$$

Applying Bayes' decomposition and using the law of total probability:

$$
P(D) = P(+T \cap D) + P(-T \cap D)
$$

Therefore:

$$
P(+T \mid D) = \frac{P(+T \cap D)}{P(+T \cap D) + P(-T \cap D)}
$$

In counting terms, $P(+T \cap D) \equiv \text{TP}$, $P(-T \cap D) \equiv \text{FN}$, so:

$$
\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}
$$

This is the **frequentist estimator** of the true sensitivity of the test.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Standard Sensitivity Calculation

> **[KTU Typical Numerical Pattern]**
> A new rapid antigen test for **Dengue NS1** is evaluated on **500 patients**. Of these, **200** are confirmed positive by the gold-standard ELISA test. The rapid test correctly identifies **180** of the confirmed cases but misses **20**. Additionally, it falsely flags **30** healthy individuals as positive.

**Step 1:** Construct the confusion matrix.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TP} &= 180 \\
\text{FN} &= 20 \\
\text{FP} &= 30 \\
\text{TN} &= 500 - 200 - 30 = 270
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the sensitivity formula.

$$
\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} = \frac{180}{180 + 20}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute.

$$
\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 = 90\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The rapid Dengue test catches **90%** of truly infected patients. The miss rate is **10%** (Type II error probability).

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Sensitivity vs. Prevalence Comparison

> A hospital deploys a **diabetes screening HbA1c test** with $\text{Sensitivity} = 0.85$ and $\text{Specificity} = 0.95$. Compute the confusion matrix in two populations of **1000** each — one with **low prevalence (5%)** and one with **high prevalence (30%)**.

### Case A: Low Prevalence Population (5%)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Diseased} &= 0.05 \times 1000 = 50 \\
\text{Healthy} &= 0.95 \times 1000 = 950 \\
\text{TP} &= 0.85 \times 50 = 42.5 \approx 43 \\
\text{FN} &= 50 - 43 = 7 \\
\text{FP} &= 0.05 \times 950 = 47.5 \approx 48 \\
\text{TN} &= 950 - 48 = 902
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{43}{50} = 0.86 \;(86\%)
$$

### Case B: High Prevalence Population (30%)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Diseased} &= 0.30 \times 1000 = 300 \\
\text{Healthy} &= 0.70 \times 1000 = 700 \\
\text{TP} &= 0.85 \times 300 = 255 \\
\text{FN} &= 300 - 255 = 45 \\
\text{FP} &= 0.05 \times 700 = 35 \\
\text{TN} &= 700 - 35 = 665
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{255}{300} = 0.85 \;(85\%)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Sensitivity is an *intrinsic property* of the test and **does not depend on disease prevalence**. However, the *absolute number* of true positives and false negatives does scale with prevalence — critical for hospital resource planning.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Sensitivity in ML Healthcare Models

> A logistic regression model predicts **30-day hospital readmission** for cardiac patients. On a validation set of **800** patients, **120** were actually readmitted. The model flagged **100** of them correctly but missed **20**. It also raised false alarms for **90** patients who were not readmitted.

**Step 1:** Extract counts.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TP} &= 100,\quad \text{FN} = 20 \\
\text{FP} &= 90,\quad \text{TN} = 800 - 120 - 90 = 590
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Sensitivity (Recall) computation.

$$
\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{100}{100 + 20} = \frac{100}{120} = 0.8333 = 83.33\%
$$

**Step 3:** Companion metrics.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Specificity} &= \frac{590}{590 + 90} = \frac{590}{680} = 0.8676 = 86.76\% \\
\text{Precision (PPV)} &= \frac{100}{100 + 90} = \frac{100}{190} = 0.5263 = 52.63\% \\
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{100 + 590}{800} = \frac{690}{800} = 0.8625 = 86.25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that even with **86% accuracy**, the **Precision is only 52%** — many alerts are false alarms. In contrast, **Sensitivity is robust (83%)**, meaning the model catches most real readmissions. In clinical practice, **high sensitivity is prioritized over precision** when the cost of a missed case is high.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Confusion Matrix as a Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Patient Population N"] --> B{"Actual Disease Status"}
    B -- Diseased Pos --> C["Disease Positives TP plus FN"]
    B -- Healthy Neg --> D["Healthy Negatives TN plus FP"]
    C --> E{"Test Result"}
    E -- Test Pos --> F["TP True Positives"]
    E -- Test Neg --> G["FN False Negatives Type II Error"]
    D --> H{"Test Result"}
    H -- Test Pos --> I["FP False Positives Type I Error"]
    H -- Test Neg --> J["TN True Negatives"]
    F --> K["Sensitivity equals TP divided by TP plus FN"]
    J --> L["Specificity equals TN divided by TN plus FP"]
    F --> M["Precision PPV equals TP divided by TP plus FP"]
    J --> N["NPV equals TN divided by TN plus FN"]
    K --> O["High Sensitivity minimizes Missed Diagnoses"]
    L --> P["High Specificity minimizes False Alarms"]
```

## 4.2 Diagnostic Test Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Administer Diagnostic Test"] --> B{"Test Outcome"}
    B -- Positive --> C{"Verify with Gold Standard"}
    B -- Negative --> D{"Patient Cleared or Follow-up"}
    C -- Confirmed Sick --> E["TP Sensitivity Hits"]
    C -- Actually Healthy --> F["FP False Alarm Specificity Loss"]
    D -- Later Confirmed Sick --> G["FN Missed Case Sensitivity Loss"]
    D -- Confirmed Healthy --> H["TN True Negative Specificity Hits"]
    E --> I["Sensitivity Maximized"]
    H --> J["Specificity Maximized"]
```

## 4.3 Sensitivity–Specificity Trade-off (ROC Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Lower Decision Threshold"] --> B["Sensitivity Rises FN Drops"]
    A --> C["Specificity Falls FP Rises"]
    D["Raise Decision Threshold"] --> E["Specificity Rises FP Drops"]
    D --> F["Sensitivity Falls FN Rises"]
    B --> G["ROC Curve Point Moves Up and Left"]
    C --> G
    E --> H["ROC Curve Point Moves Down and Right"]
    F --> H
    G --> I["AUC Ideal equals 1.0 Worst equals 0.5"]
    H --> I
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Sensitivity Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Patient Data EHR Lab Reports"] --> B["Data Preprocessing Cleaning Normalization"]
    B --> C["Apply Diagnostic Test or ML Model"]
    C --> D["Generate Predicted Labels Pos or Neg"]
    D --> E["Compare with Gold Standard Labels"]
    E --> F["Construct Confusion Matrix"]
    F --> G["Compute TP FN FP TN Counts"]
    G --> H["Sensitivity equals TP over TP plus FN"]
    G --> I["Specificity equals TN over TN plus FP"]
    G --> J["Precision equals TP over TP plus FP"]
    G --> K["F1 equals 2 Precision Recall over Precision plus Recall"]
    H --> L["Clinical Interpretation Report"]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M["Decision Rule SnNout for High Sensitivity Tests"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define Sensitivity in the context of healthcare diagnostic testing. Why is it clinically important?**

**Model Answer:**

Sensitivity is a performance metric that measures the proportion of truly diseased patients correctly identified as positive by a diagnostic test. It is also called the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** or **Recall**, given by:

$$
\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}
$$

**Clinical importance:**
- A **high-sensitivity test minimizes False Negatives** (missed diagnoses), which is critical in serious illnesses like cancer, HIV, and tuberculosis.
- It follows the **SnNout** rule: a **S**e**N**sitive test with a **N**egative result reliably rules **OUT** disease.
- It is essential for **mass screening programs** where missing a case is costlier than over-diagnosing.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Distinguish between Sensitivity and Specificity with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Sensitivity | Specificity |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Ability to detect *diseased* patients | Ability to detect *healthy* patients |
| **Formula** | $\dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$ | $\dfrac{\text{TN}}{\text{TN} + \text{FP}}$ |
| **Focus** | Minimize False Negatives | Minimize False Positives |
| **Mnemonic** | SnNout (rule out) | SpPin (rule in) |

**Example:** For an **HIV ELISA screening test**, high *sensitivity* ensures that no HIV-positive patient is wrongly told they are negative. High *specificity* ensures that no HIV-negative patient is wrongly told they are positive.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [Choice Option 1]**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Adapted]**
> A hospital introduces a new **cardiac risk prediction AI model** to detect patients at high risk of myocardial infarction. The model is tested on **1000 patients**, of whom **250** actually had a cardiac event. The model correctly predicted **200** of them as high-risk but missed **50**. It also incorrectly classified **80** healthy patients as high-risk.

#### **(a) Construct the Confusion Matrix and compute Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, and Accuracy. (7 Marks)**

**Step 1:** Identify the four counts.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TP} &= 200 \\
\text{FN} &= 50 \\
\text{FP} &= 80 \\
\text{TN} &= 1000 - 250 - 80 = 670
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Construct the matrix.

| | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** | **Total** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Actually Diseased** | TP = 200 | FN = 50 | 250 |
| **Actually Healthy** | FP = 80 | TN = 670 | 750 |
| **Total** | 280 | 720 | 1000 |

**Step 3:** Compute Sensitivity.

$$
\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{200}{200 + 50} = \frac{200}{250} = 0.80 = 80\%
$$

**Step 4:** Compute Specificity.

$$
\text{Specificity} = \frac{670}{670 + 80} = \frac{670}{750} = 0.8933 = 89.33\%
$$

**Step 5:** Compute Precision (PPV).

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{200}{200 + 80} = \frac{200}{280} = 0.7143 = 71.43\%
$$

**Step 6:** Compute Accuracy.

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{200 + 670}{1000} = \frac{870}{1000} = 0.87 = 87\%
$$

#### **(b) Explain the clinical significance of Sensitivity in this scenario. If the hospital wants to reduce missed cardiac events, what trade-off must be accepted? (7 Marks)**

**Significance:**
- A Sensitivity of **80%** means the AI model catches **4 out of 5** true cardiac events. The remaining **20%** (50 patients) would be *missed* — a clinically dangerous scenario in cardiology.
- For life-threatening conditions, the acceptable sensitivity threshold in literature is typically **≥ 90%**.

**Trade-off via Threshold Tuning:**
- To increase sensitivity, the model's decision threshold must be **lowered**, making it more permissive in flagging patients as "high-risk."
- Consequence: **Specificity drops** (more healthy patients are falsely alarmed), and **Precision falls** (more false positives per true positive).
- This is the classic **Sensitivity–Specificity trade-off**, visualized on the **ROC curve**.

**Recommendation:** For cardiac screening, the hospital should prioritize sensitivity and tolerate more false positives, because:
- A *false alarm* triggers a confirmatory test (low marginal harm).
- A *missed cardiac event* can be fatal (catastrophic harm).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Choice Option 2]**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Adapted]**
> A new **mammography AI tool** for early breast cancer detection is deployed across **5 hospitals**. The pooled results show:
> - **Diseased patients:** 400
> - **Healthy patients:** 1600
> - **Correctly identified diseased:** 360
> - **Missed diseased:** 40
> - **False alarms:** 100

#### **(a) Compute the Sensitivity, Miss Rate, Specificity, and Precision. (7 Marks)**

**Step 1:** Tabulate counts.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TP} &= 360,\quad \text{FN} = 40 \\
\text{FP} &= 100,\quad \text{TN} = 1600 - 100 = 1500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Sensitivity.

$$
\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{360}{360 + 40} = \frac{360}{400} = 0.90 = 90\%
$$

**Step 3:** Miss Rate (False Negative Rate).

$$
\text{Miss Rate} = \frac{\text{FN}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} = \frac{40}{400} = 0.10 = 10\%
$$

Equivalently: $\text{Miss Rate} = 1 - \text{Sensitivity} = 1 - 0.90 = 0.10$ ✓

**Step 4:** Specificity.

$$
\text{Specificity} = \frac{1500}{1500 + 100} = \frac{1500}{1600} = 0.9375 = 93.75\%
$$

**Step 5:** Precision (PPV).

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{360}{360 + 100} = \frac{360}{460} = 0.7826 = 78.26\%
$$

#### **(b) Justify why mammography prioritizes high sensitivity. Discuss the SnNout rule and the role of prevalence. (7 Marks)**

**Justification for High Sensitivity in Mammography:**
- Breast cancer is **life-threatening** and **treatable if caught early**. A missed case (False Negative) delays treatment and reduces survival.
- Mammography is a **first-line screening tool** applied to a large asymptomatic population — the *gatekeeping* role demands near-zero missed cases.

**SnNout Rule Application:**
- SnNout = **S**e**N**sitive test + **N**egative result = rules **OUT** disease.
- With Sensitivity = 90%, a *negative* mammogram strongly reassures the patient that no cancer is present (low FN).
- Therefore, mammography functions as a *rule-out* screening test.

**Role of Prevalence:**
- Breast cancer prevalence in screened populations is low (~0.5%–1%). With high sensitivity, the **Negative Predictive Value (NPV)** approaches ~100%, making negative results highly trustworthy.
- NPV computation:
$$
\text{NPV} = \frac{\text{TN}}{\text{TN} + \text{FN}} = \frac{1500}{1500 + 40} = 0.9740 = 97.40\%
$$
- This high NPV reinforces mammography's role as an effective *rule-out* tool.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Confusing the denominator:** Students often compute Sensitivity as $\dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{Total Patients}}$ instead of $\dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$ — *this is wrong* and loses 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Mixing up Sensitivity with Precision:** Sensitivity uses *actual positives* in the denominator (column of truth), while Precision uses *predicted positives* (row of prediction). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting the unit:** Always state answers in **% or [0,1]**, not raw numbers. Skipping units is a **0.5 mark deduction**.
> 4. **Ignoring the prevalence caveat:** Sensitivity is *intrinsic to the test*, but the *clinical utility* (e.g., NPV) depends on prevalence. Examiners reward students who mention this nuance.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sensitivity (Module 2)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Sensitivity is the **True Positive Rate** = proportion of *actual diseased* correctly flagged.
- ✅ **Formula:** $\text{Sensitivity} = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$ — *denominator is the disease row*.
- ✅ **Range:** $[0, 1]$ or $[0\%, 100\%]$; ideal = **1.0**.
- ✅ **Mnemonic:** **SnNout** — *Sensitive test, Negative result, rules OUT disease.*
- ✅ **Complement:** Miss Rate = $1 - \text{Sensitivity}$ (False Negative Rate).
- ✅ **Trade-off:** Increasing Sensitivity → Decreasing Specificity (via threshold lowering).
- ✅ **Independence from Prevalence:** Sensitivity is an *intrinsic* test property; PPV/NPV depend on prevalence.
- ✅ **Clinical Priority:** Use **high-sensitivity tests for screening** (rule out) and **high-specificity tests for confirmation** (rule in / SpPin).
- ✅ **ROC–AUC Connection:** Sensitivity and $(1 - \text{Specificity})$ are the axes of the **ROC curve**; AUC summarizes overall model performance.
- ✅ **F1-Score Link:** Sensitivity = Recall; F1 harmonizes Recall with Precision — preferred for imbalanced healthcare datasets.
- ✅ **Common Confusion:** Sensitivity ≠ Accuracy ≠ Precision — know the *denominator* of each formula cold.
- ✅ **Engineering Application:** In healthcare AI/ML pipelines, Sensitivity is *the* primary metric when the cost of False Negatives is high (oncology, cardiology, infectious disease screening).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specificity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT862 - HEALTHCARE DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Specificity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Healthcare Data Analytics**, **Specificity** is formally defined as a fundamental **diagnostic performance metric** that quantifies the ability of a medical test, screening tool, or classification model to correctly identify individuals who do **NOT** have a particular disease or condition.

Mathematically, Specificity represents the proportion of **actual negatives** (healthy patients) that are correctly classified as **negatives** by the diagnostic system. It is a conditional probability: given that the true condition is absent, what is the probability that the test result will also be negative?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official Definition (KTU Standard):**
> *Specificity (also called the True Negative Rate, TNR) is the ratio of True Negatives (TN) to the total number of patients who actually do not have the disease (TN + FP). It measures a test's ability to avoid producing false positive errors.*

Formally expressed as:

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$$

where:
- $TN$ = True Negatives (patients without disease AND test result negative)
- $FP$ = False Positives (patients without disease BUT test result positive — Type I Error)

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The Airport Security Scanner**
> 
> Imagine a security scanner at a Kerala international airport. The scanner's job is to detect passengers carrying prohibited items. 
> 
> - **Passengers WITHOUT prohibited items** who are correctly allowed through = **True Negatives** ✅
> - **Passengers WITHOUT prohibited items** who are falsely flagged and stopped = **False Positives** ❌ (innocent people delayed/frisked)
> 
> **Specificity** measures the scanner's ability to let innocent passengers pass without unnecessary interruption. A **HIGH Specificity** means: *"If you are clean, the scanner will NOT bother you."*

In healthcare terms: A test with **High Specificity** is excellent at ruling OUT disease in healthy people. This is why clinicians say: **"A highly specific test, when negative, rules OUT disease"** (the **"Sn-Nout"** mnemonic — Sensitivity-negative-rules-out, while SpIN = Specificity-positive-rules-IN).

## 1.3 Key Conceptual Properties

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary & Range Properties of Specificity:**
> - Range: $[0, 1]$ or equivalently $[0\%, 100\%]$
> - **Ideal Value:** $1.0$ ($100\%$) — every healthy patient correctly identified
> - **Worst Value:** $0.0$ ($0\%$) — every healthy patient wrongly flagged as diseased
> - Closely related complement: $1 - \text{Specificity} = \text{False Positive Rate (FPR)}$
> - In binary classification, Specificity is the **True Negative Rate (TNR)**

## 1.4 Why Specificity Matters in Healthcare

Specificity is critically important in scenarios where **false positives carry significant consequences**:

| Clinical Scenario | Why Specificity Matters |
|-------------------|------------------------|
| **Cancer Screening** (initial tests) | Avoids unnecessary biopsies in healthy patients |
| **HIV Initial Screening** | Prevents psychological trauma and stigma of false alarms |
| **Rare Disease Diagnosis** | Reduces costly follow-up tests on healthy individuals |
| **Drug Approval Trials** | Ensures side effects are not falsely attributed |
| **AI Radiology Models** | Prevents flagging normal scans as malignant |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Confusion Matrix Foundation

Specificity is derived from the **Confusion Matrix** (also called the Error Matrix or Contingency Table), which is the cornerstone of all diagnostic and classification performance evaluation in healthcare analytics.

$$\text{Confusion Matrix Structure} = \begin{bmatrix} TN & FP \\ FN & TP \end{bmatrix}$$

The matrix is built on:
- **Ground Truth (Actual Condition):** Disease status confirmed by gold-standard test
- **Predicted Outcome:** Result from the test/model under evaluation

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition

**Step 1: Identify the Denominator — The "Actually Negative" Population**
The denominator $TN + FP$ represents the **column sum** of the confusion matrix for actual negatives. These are patients proven (by gold standard) to be disease-free.

**Step 2: Identify the Numerator — The "Correctly Identified" Count**
The numerator $TN$ is the count of healthy patients whose test correctly returned a negative result.

**Step 3: Compute the Ratio**
Divide the correctly identified healthy patients by the total healthy patients.

**Step 4: Interpret**
- Closer to 1.0 → Test rarely produces false alarms
- Closer to 0.5 → Test is as good as a coin flip for healthy patients
- Closer to 0.0 → Test wrongly flags almost all healthy patients

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE FOR KTU EXAMS**

| Formula | Expression | Description | KTU Frequency |
|---------|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $\frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | True Negative Rate | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $1 - \text{Specificity} = \frac{FP}{TN + FP}$ | Complement of Specificity | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Sensitivity (TPR)** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | True Positive Rate (Recall) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **False Negative Rate (FNR)** | $1 - \text{Sensitivity} = \frac{FN}{TP + FN}$ | Complement of Sensitivity | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Precision (PPV)** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Positive Predictive Value | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **NPV** | $\frac{TN}{TN + FN}$ | Negative Predictive Value | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Accuracy** | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Youden's J Index** | $\text{Sensitivity} + \text{Specificity} - 1$ | Diagnostic discrimination | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Likelihood Ratio ($-$)** | $\frac{1 - \text{Sensitivity}}{\text{Specificity}}$ | Negative LR | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Likelihood Ratio ($+$)** | $\frac{\text{Sensitivity}}{1 - \text{Specificity}}$ | Positive LR | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Odds Ratio (Diagnostic)** | $\frac{TP \cdot TN}{FP \cdot FN}$ | Diagnostic OR | ⭐⭐ |
| **F1-Score** | $\frac{2 \cdot \text{PPV} \cdot \text{Sensitivity}}{\text{PPV} + \text{Sensitivity}}$ | Harmonic mean | ⭐⭐ |
| **Prevalence** | $\frac{TP + FN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Disease prevalence in cohort | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention:** All proportions are dimensionless. They may be expressed as decimals (0.95) or percentages (95%). Always declare the form in your answer.

## 2.4 The Trade-off: Specificity vs. Sensitivity

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Concept:**
> In most diagnostic tests, **Specificity and Sensitivity are inversely related**. Lowering the decision threshold (e.g., lowering the cutoff for "positive" diagnosis) **increases Sensitivity** but **decreases Specificity**, and vice versa. The **ROC Curve** plots Sensitivity (y-axis) against $1 - \text{Specificity}$ = FPR (x-axis).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In healthcare AI engineering pipelines, Specificity is used for:

1. **Threshold Tuning:** Selecting the optimal classification threshold for medical AI models (e.g., tumor classifiers in CNNs).
2. **Cost-Sensitive Learning:** Weighing false positives (cost of unnecessary treatment) more heavily in loss functions.
3. **Regulatory Compliance:** FDA/medical device approval requires reporting Specificity across demographic subgroups.
4. **Clinical Decision Support Systems (CDSS):** Selecting confirmatory tests with high Specificity when initial tests are positive.

## 2.6 Specificity in Multi-Class Healthcare Settings

For multi-class problems (e.g., classifying 5 types of skin lesions), Specificity is computed per class using **One-vs-Rest (OvR)** strategy:

$$\text{Specificity}_i = \frac{\sum_{j \neq i} TN_{ij}}{\sum_{j \neq i} TN_{ij} + \sum_{j \neq i} FP_{ij}}$$

This becomes essential in differential diagnosis systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Specificity from First Principles (Probability)

**Starting Premise:** We have a binary diagnostic test that produces one of two outcomes: $+$ (positive/disease predicted) or $-$ (negative/no disease predicted). The true disease status is $D$ (disease present) or $\bar{D}$ (disease absent).

**Step 1: Express Specificity as a conditional probability.**

$$\text{Specificity} = P(\text{Test} = - \mid \bar{D})$$

**Step 2: Apply the definition of conditional probability.**

$$P(\text{Test} = - \mid \bar{D}) = \frac{P(\text{Test} = - \ \cap \ \bar{D})}{P(\bar{D})}$$

**Step 3: Recognize the joint probability in the numerator as the True Negative rate (in terms of counts).**

$$P(\text{Test} = - \ \cap \ \bar{D}) = \frac{TN}{N}$$

where $N$ is the total population size.

**Step 4: Recognize the marginal probability in the denominator.**

$$P(\bar{D}) = \frac{TN + FP}{N}$$

**Step 5: Substitute and simplify.**

$$P(\text{Test} = - \mid \bar{D}) = \frac{TN/N}{(TN + FP)/N} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$$

**Step 6: Final Result.**

$$\boxed{\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}}$$

This derivation confirms Specificity as a **Bayesian conditional probability**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between Specificity, Prevalence, and NPV

Specificity directly influences the **Negative Predictive Value (NPV)** through Bayes' theorem.

**Step 1: Recall the formula for NPV.**

$$\text{NPV} = \frac{TN}{TN + FN}$$

**Step 2: Express TN and FN in terms of Specificity and Sensitivity.**

We know:
- $TN = \text{Specificity} \times (TN + FP)$
- $FN = (1 - \text{Sensitivity}) \times (TP + FN)$

**Step 3: Introduce prevalence $\pi$ where $\pi = P(D)$.**

The total disease-positive count is $\pi N$ and disease-negative count is $(1 - \pi)N$.

**Step 4: Rewrite NPV using prevalence.**

$$\text{NPV} = \frac{\text{Specificity} \times (1 - \pi)}{\text{Specificity} \times (1 - \pi) + (1 - \text{Sensitivity}) \times \pi}$$

**Step 5: Key Insight (KTU High-Yield!).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When **prevalence is LOW** (rare disease), NPV remains **HIGH** even with moderate Specificity. This is why screening tests for rare diseases (e.g., spinal muscular atrophy in newborns) can have excellent NPV — almost everyone truly doesn't have the disease, and Specificity ensures most are correctly identified as negative.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Youden's J-Statistic Using Specificity

**Step 1: Define Youden's J statistic.**

$$J = \text{Sensitivity} + \text{Specificity} - 1$$

**Step 2: Show that $J$ equals the difference between TPR and FPR.**

$$J = \text{Sensitivity} - (1 - \text{Specificity}) = \text{TPR} - \text{FPR}$$

**Step 3: Geometric meaning on the ROC curve.**

The maximum $J$ corresponds to the point on the ROC curve that is **vertically farthest from the diagonal line** (random classifier line). This point represents the **optimal decision threshold**.

**Step 4: At $J = 1$ (perfect test):** Sensitivity = 1 and Specificity = 1.
**Step 5: At $J = 0$ (worthless test):** Test performs no better than chance.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A new rapid antigen test for dengue fever is evaluated on 1,000 patients. Gold-standard RT-PCR confirms 200 have dengue (actual positives) and 800 do not (actual negatives). The rapid test yields the following results:

- Of the 200 dengue-positive patients, 180 test positive (TP) and 20 test negative (FN).
- Of the 800 dengue-negative patients, 760 test negative (TN) and 40 test positive (FP).

**Task:** Compute Specificity, FPR, NPV, and Youden's J index.

**Step 1: Build the confusion matrix.**

| | Actual Positive | Actual Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Predicted Positive** | TP = 180 | FP = 40 |
| **Predicted Negative** | FN = 20 | TN = 760 |

**Step 2: Calculate Specificity.**

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{760}{760 + 40} = \frac{760}{800} = 0.95$$

**Step 3: Calculate FPR (complement).**

$$\text{FPR} = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05 = 5\%$$

**Step 4: Calculate Sensitivity (needed for Youden's J).**

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90$$

**Step 5: Calculate NPV.**

$$\text{NPV} = \frac{TN}{TN + FN} = \frac{760}{760 + 20} = \frac{760}{780} \approx 0.9744$$

**Step 6: Calculate Youden's J.**

$$J = 0.90 + 0.95 - 1 = 0.85$$

**Step 7: Calculate Prevalence.**

$$\pi = \frac{200}{1000} = 0.20 = 20\%$$

**Step 8: Interpretation.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Interpretation:**
> - The test has **95% Specificity** — only 5% of healthy patients will be wrongly told they have dengue.
> - The test has **97.44% NPV** — a negative dengue result can be trusted $97.44\%$ of the time.
> - Youden's $J = 0.85$ indicates strong overall diagnostic discrimination.
> - Given Kerala's dengue endemicity, this test is suitable for screening in primary health centers.

## 3.5 Verification via Bayes' Theorem

Cross-check: If a patient tests **negative**, probability they are actually disease-free:

$$P(\bar{D} \mid \text{Test} = -) = \frac{P(\text{Test} = - \mid \bar{D}) \cdot P(\bar{D})}{P(\text{Test} = -)}$$

$$= \frac{0.95 \times 0.80}{(0.95 \times 0.80) + (0.10 \times 0.20)} = \frac{0.76}{0.78} \approx 0.9744 \ \checkmark$$

This matches the NPV computed earlier — a consistency check that confirms the derivation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Specificity Concept Map (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Total Patient Cohort (N)"] --> B{"True Disease Status?"}
    B -->|Positive D| C["Diseased Patients TP + FN"]
    B -->|Negative NotD| D["Healthy Patients TN + FP"]
    C --> C1["TP: Correctly Identified"]
    C --> C2["FN: Missed Cases Type II Error"]
    D --> D1["TN: Correctly Cleared"]
    D --> D2["FP: False Alarms Type I Error"]
    D1 --> E["Numerator of Specificity"]
    D --> F["Denominator of Specificity"]
    D2 --> F
    E --> G["Specificity equals TN divided by TN plus FP"]
    F --> G
    G --> H{"Interpretation"}
    H -->|Close to 1.0| I["Excellent Test Rare False Alarms"]
    H -->|Close to 0.5| J["Poor Test Coin Flip Quality"]
    H -->|Close to 0.0| K["Worst Test All Healthy Flagged"]
    G --> L["Complement gives False Positive Rate"]
    L --> M["FPR equals 1 minus Specificity"]
    G --> N["Used in ROC Curve x axis"]
```

## 4.2 Confusion Matrix Visual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Actual["ACTUAL STATUS (Gold Standard)"]
        AP["Actual Positive\nDisease Present"]
        AN["Actual Negative\nDisease Absent"]
    end
    subgraph Predicted["PREDICTED STATUS (Test Output)"]
        PP["Predicted Positive"]
        PN["Predicted Negative"]
    end
    AP -->|Correct| TPnode["TP Block\nSensitivity Numerator"]
    AP -->|Wrong| FNnode["FN Block\nType II Error"]
    AN -->|Wrong| FPnode["FP Block\nType I Error"]
    AN -->|Correct| TNnode["TN Block\nSpecificity Numerator"]
    TPnode --> Sens["Sensitivity Metric"]
    TNnode --> Spec["Specificity Metric"]
    FPnode --> FPR["FPR equals 1 minus Specificity"]
    FNnode --> FNR["FNR equals 1 minus Sensitivity"]
    Spec --> ROC1["ROC Curve x axis input"]
    Sens --> ROC2["ROC Curve y axis input"]
```

## 4.3 Diagnostic Decision Threshold Trade-off Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Continuous Diagnostic Score e.g. Biomarker Level"] --> B{"Decision Threshold Cutoff"}
    B -->|Score Above Threshold| C["Classify as POSITIVE"]
    B -->|Score Below Threshold| D["Classify as NEGATIVE"]
    C -->|Disease Present| E["TRUE POSITIVE TP"]
    C -->|Disease Absent| F["FALSE POSITIVE FP"]
    D -->|Disease Absent| G["TRUE NEGATIVE TN"]
    D -->|Disease Present| H["FALSE NEGATIVE FN"]
    G --> I["Specificity increases when threshold is HIGH\nfewer healthy people falsely flagged"]
    F --> J["FPR increases when threshold is LOW\nmore healthy people wrongly flagged"]
    E --> K["Sensitivity increases when threshold is LOW\nmore diseased people caught"]
    H --> L["FNR increases when threshold is HIGH\nmore diseased people missed"]
```

## 4.4 KTU Block-Level Architecture: Specificity in ML Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Healthcare Data EHR, Imaging, Labs"] --> B["Preprocessing Cleaning, Normalization"]
    B --> C["Train Test Split stratified"]
    C --> D["Classification Model SVM, Random Forest, CNN"]
    D --> E["Predicted Labels on Test Set"]
    F["Gold Standard Labels from Clinician"] --> G["Confusion Matrix Construction"]
    E --> G
    G --> H["Compute TP, FP, TN, FN"]
    H --> I["Specificity equals TN divided by TN plus FP"]
    H --> J["Sensitivity equals TP divided by TP plus FN"]
    I --> K["Performance Report"]
    J --> K
    K --> L{"Specificity acceptable per clinical needs?"}
    L -->|No| M["Threshold Tuning, Feature Engineering"]
    M --> D
    L -->|Yes| N["Deploy Clinical Decision Support"]
```

## 4.5 ROC Curve Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["0,0 Origin"] --> B["1,0 FPR equals 1 Specificity equals 0"]
    B --> C["1,1 Perfect Classifier TPR equals 1 FPR equals 0"]
    A --> D["0,1 TPR equals 1 Specificity equals 1"]
    D --> C
    A -.->|Random Classifier Diagonal| C
    E["Specificity values along x axis decreasing from 1 to 0"] -.-> B
    F["Sensitivity values along y axis increasing from 0 to 1"] -.-> D
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Define Specificity in the context of healthcare diagnostic testing. Why is it considered a critical metric in clinical decision-making?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Specificity is a diagnostic performance metric that measures the proportion of truly disease-free individuals (true negatives) correctly identified as negative by a test. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$$

**(1 mark)** for the definition and formula.

It is critical in clinical decision-making because a high Specificity indicates a low false positive rate, meaning the test rarely misclassifies healthy patients as diseased. This prevents unnecessary invasive procedures, treatments, psychological trauma, and healthcare costs. **(1 mark)**.

A test with high Specificity is particularly valuable for **confirmatory diagnosis** — a positive result from a highly specific test can reliably rule IN disease (the **"SpIn"** rule). **(1 mark)**.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Differentiate between Specificity and Sensitivity. Under what clinical scenario would you prioritize Specificity over Sensitivity?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Sensitivity | Specificity |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| Definition | Proportion of diseased patients correctly identified | Proportion of healthy patients correctly identified |
| Formula | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $\frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ |
| Measures | Detection ability | Exclusion ability |
| Mnemonic | SnNout (neg result rules out) | SpIn (pos result rules in) |

**(2 marks)** for the differentiation.

**Scenario Prioritizing Specificity:** In **confirmatory/follow-up testing** where false positives lead to harmful consequences. **Example:** Confirming an HIV diagnosis after a positive ELISA screening test — a false positive could devastate a patient psychologically, so a highly specific confirmatory test (e.g., Western Blot) is used. **(1 mark)**.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the formula for Specificity from first principles using conditional probability. Explain each step in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hospital deploys a deep learning model to detect pneumonia from chest X-ray images. On a test set of 2,000 images (1,000 confirmed pneumonia, 1,000 normal), the model produces: TP = 920, FN = 80, FP = 60, TN = 940. Compute Specificity, Sensitivity, False Positive Rate, Negative Predictive Value, and Youden's J-index. Interpret each value clinically. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Derivation of Specificity (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the test scenario.

Let $D$ = event that disease is present, $\bar{D}$ = event that disease is absent. Let $T^+$ = test positive, $T^-$ = test negative.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Write Specificity as a conditional probability.

$$\text{Specificity} = P(T^- \mid \bar{D})$$

This represents: *"Given the patient is disease-free, what is the probability the test returns negative?"*

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply the definition of conditional probability.

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{P(T^- \cap \bar{D})}{P(\bar{D})}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Express joint and marginal probabilities in terms of counts over total population $N$.

$$P(T^- \cap \bar{D}) = \frac{TN}{N}, \quad P(\bar{D}) = \frac{TN + FP}{N}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Substitute and simplify.

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN/N}{(TN + FP)/N} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Conclude with the final formula and note its range $[0, 1]$.

$$\boxed{\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} \in [0, 1]}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Mention that the complement $1 - \text{Specificity}$ is the False Positive Rate (FPR), representing the probability of Type I error.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:** TP = 920, FN = 80, FP = 60, TN = 940. Total $N = 2000$.

**Step 1 (1.5 marks): Compute Specificity.**

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{940}{940 + 60} = \frac{940}{1000} = 0.94 \ (94\%)$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks): Compute Sensitivity.**

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{920}{920 + 80} = \frac{920}{1000} = 0.92 \ (92\%)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark): Compute False Positive Rate.**

$$\text{FPR} = 1 - \text{Specificity} = 1 - 0.94 = 0.06 \ (6\%)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark): Compute NPV.**

$$\text{NPV} = \frac{TN}{TN + FN} = \frac{940}{940 + 80} = \frac{940}{1020} \approx 0.9216 \ (92.16\%)$$

**Step 5 (1 mark): Compute Youden's J-Index.**

$$J = \text{Sensitivity} + \text{Specificity} - 1 = 0.92 + 0.94 - 1 = 0.86$$

**Step 6 (1 mark): Clinical Interpretation.**

- **Specificity 94%:** Only 6% of normal X-rays wrongly flagged as pneumonia — low false alarm rate.
- **Sensitivity 92%:** Catches 92% of actual pneumonia cases.
- **NPV 92.16%:** A negative result rules out pneumonia with high confidence.
- **Youden's J = 0.86:** Strong diagnostic discrimination; the model is clinically viable with minor threshold tuning for screening deployment.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Specificity with a suitable clinical example. Discuss the **Specificity-Sensitivity trade-off** using the ROC curve. Illustrate how varying the decision threshold affects Specificity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A new cardiac troponin-I rapid test is evaluated on 500 patients. Of these, 150 are confirmed MI cases (gold standard) and 350 are non-MI. Test results: TP = 135, FN = 15, FP = 35, TN = 315. Calculate: (i) Specificity, (ii) Sensitivity, (iii) Accuracy, (iv) Likelihood Ratio ($-$). Comment on whether this test is suitable as a **rule-out test** for MI in an emergency department. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Concept, Trade-off, ROC (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1.5 marks): Concept and Clinical Example.**

Specificity is the proportion of disease-free individuals correctly identified as negative by a test. **Example:** A mammography screening program for breast cancer — Specificity measures the proportion of women without cancer whose mammograms are correctly read as normal. High Specificity here is essential to avoid unnecessary biopsies in healthy women.

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks): Specificity-Sensitivity Trade-off.**

The two metrics are **inversely related** in most diagnostic tests:
- Lowering the decision threshold → catches more true positives (↑ Sensitivity) but also flags more healthy people (↓ Specificity).
- Raising the decision threshold → fewer false alarms (↑ Specificity) but more missed cases (↓ Sensitivity).

**Step 3 (2 marks): ROC Curve Explanation.**

The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve** plots:
- Y-axis: Sensitivity (TPR)
- X-axis: $1 - \text{Specificity}$ (FPR)

As the threshold varies from high to low, the curve traces from $(0,0)$ to $(1,1)$. The **Area Under the Curve (AUC)** summarizes overall diagnostic ability.

**Step 4 (2 marks): Threshold Effect Illustration.**

- **High threshold (strict):** Few positives predicted → high Specificity, low Sensitivity (top-left region of ROC).
- **Low threshold (lenient):** Many positives predicted → high Sensitivity, low Specificity (bottom-right region of ROC).
- **Optimal threshold:** Maximizes Youden's $J$ index, balancing both.

---

### Part (b) — Cardiac Troponin Test Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:** TP = 135, FN = 15, FP = 35, TN = 315, Total = 500.

**Step 1 (1.5 marks): Compute Specificity.**

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{315}{315 + 35} = \frac{315}{350} = 0.90 \ (90\%)$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks): Compute Sensitivity.**

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{135}{135 + 15} = \frac{135}{150} = 0.90 \ (90\%)$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks): Compute Accuracy.**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{135 + 315}{500} = \frac{450}{500} = 0.90 \ (90\%)$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks): Compute Likelihood Ratio ($-$).**

$$LR(-) = \frac{1 - \text{Sensitivity}}{\text{Specificity}} = \frac{1 - 0.90}{0.90} = \frac{0.10}{0.90} \approx 0.111$$

**Step 5 (1 mark): Clinical Comment — Rule-out Suitability.**

A **good rule-out test** requires:
- Very **high Sensitivity** (≥95%) so that a negative result reliably excludes disease.
- **Low LR(−)** (≤0.1 is considered strong evidence against disease).

In this case, Sensitivity is only 90% and $LR(-) \approx 0.111$, which is **borderline** (just above the 0.1 threshold). Therefore, this rapid troponin test is **NOT ideal as a standalone rule-out test** in an emergency department. It should be used in conjunction with clinical symptoms, ECG findings, and serial troponin measurements. A more sensitive high-sensitivity troponin assay would be preferred for early MI rule-out.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU EXAMS:**
> 
> 1. **Swapping the formula:** Students often write Specificity as $\frac{TN}{TP + FN}$ or confuse it with Precision/PPV. **MEMORIZE:** Specificity denominator is always $TN + FP$ (the actual negatives).
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the complement:** Examiners often ask for "False Positive Rate" — students compute Specificity instead. Recall: $\text{FPR} = 1 - \text{Specificity}$.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up Sensitivity and Specificity columns:** In the confusion matrix, Specificity is the **column** of actual negatives; Sensitivity is the **column** of actual positives. Wrong column = wrong metric = zero marks.
> 
> 4. **Ignoring prevalence:** When the question mentions "rare disease," students should explicitly note that Specificity has greater impact on NPV in low-prevalence settings.
> 
> 5. **Not stating units/interpretation:** A numerical answer without clinical interpretation loses at least 1 mark in Part B questions.
> 
> 6. **Confusing NPV with Specificity:** NPV depends on prevalence; Specificity does not. Do not interchange them in derivations.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **⚡ RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — SPECIFICITY ⚡**
> 
> - ✅ **Definition:** Proportion of true negatives correctly identified = $\frac{TN}{TN + FP}$
> - ✅ **Also Called:** True Negative Rate (TNR)
> - ✅ **Range:** $[0, 1]$; ideal is 1
> - ✅ **Complement:** $1 - \text{Specificity}$ = False Positive Rate (FPR) = Type I Error
> - ✅ **Mnemonic:** **"SpIn"** — Specificity positive rules IN disease
> - ✅ **High Specificity test** → confirms disease when positive; rules IN
> - ✅ **Trade-off:** Inversely related to Sensitivity (controlled by decision threshold)
> - ✅ **ROC Curve:** Specificity appears as $1 - \text{FPR}$ on the x-axis
> - ✅ **Youden's J:** $J = \text{Sensitivity} + \text{Specificity} - 1$; ranges $[0, 1]$; higher is better
> - ✅ **Clinical Use:** Confirmatory tests; ruling IN disease; avoiding false positives
> - ✅ **High Prevalence vs. Low Prevalence:** Specificity drives PPV in high-prevalence settings; NPV remains high in low-prevalence settings
> - ✅ **Kerala Context:** Useful for evaluating dengue, Nipah, COVID-19, and TB screening programs
> - ✅ **Common Confusions:** ≠ Precision (PPV); ≠ NPV; ≠ Accuracy
> - ✅ **Multi-class:** Computed per class using One-vs-Rest strategy
> - ✅ **Likelihood Ratio (−):** $LR(-) = \frac{1 - \text{Sensitivity}}{\text{Specificity}}$; lower is better
> - ✅ **Likelihood Ratio (+):** $LR(+) = \frac{\text{Sensitivity}}{1 - \text{Specificity}}$; higher is better

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accuracy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT862 - HEALTHCARE DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Accuracy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Healthcare Data Analytics**, **Accuracy** is formally defined as the degree of closeness between a measured, computed, or predicted value and its corresponding **true value**, **reference standard**, or **ground truth**. It is a fundamental quantitative descriptor of data quality and model performance, and serves as a primary metric in clinical decision support systems, diagnostic algorithms, predictive risk modeling, and epidemiological surveillance.

Mathematically, accuracy can be expressed as the inverse of **error**, where error is the deviation between the observed value ($\hat{y}$) and the actual value ($y$).

$$\text{Accuracy} \propto \frac{1}{\lvert \hat{y} - y \rvert}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Accuracy is **NOT** a single unified metric. In healthcare analytics, it manifests in three distinct operational forms: **Measurement Accuracy**, **Classification Accuracy**, and **Predictive (Regression) Accuracy**. Examiners frequently test the student's ability to distinguish between these three forms.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Archery Target"

Imagine an archer shooting arrows at a circular target:

- **High Accuracy, Low Precision:** Arrows land close to the bullseye (center) but scattered apart.
- **Low Accuracy, High Precision:** Arrows land clustered tightly together, but far from the bullseye.
- **High Accuracy, High Precision:** Arrows land tightly clustered at the bullseye — the ideal scenario.

In healthcare analytics:
- **The bullseye** represents the **true patient outcome** (ground truth).
- **Each arrow** represents a **prediction** made by the model.
- **Accuracy** measures whether predictions are *close to the truth on average*.

> [!NOTE]
> A model with **high accuracy but low precision** may still be useful for population-level screening, but is dangerous for individual clinical decisions. Conversely, **high precision but low accuracy** indicates systematic bias (e.g., a glucose meter consistently reading 15 mg/dL higher than actual).

### 1.3 The Three Operational Forms of Accuracy

| Form | Application Domain | Ground Truth Reference |
|------|--------------------|------------------------|
| **Measurement Accuracy** | Lab instruments, sensors, vital sign monitors | Calibrated reference standard |
| **Classification Accuracy** | Disease diagnosis, tumor detection (malignant/benign) | Pathologist's verified diagnosis |
| **Predictive Accuracy** | Length-of-stay forecasting, readmission risk | Observed future outcome |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Accuracy Analysis

**Pillar 1 — Measurement Accuracy (Instrument-Level)**

Used for physical sensors like ECG monitors, blood pressure cuffs, and pulse oximeters. Defined as:

$$A_{m} = 1 - \frac{\lvert x_{measured} - x_{true} \rvert}{x_{true}}$$

Expressed often as a **percentage error**:

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{\lvert x_{measured} - x_{true} \rvert}{x_{true}} \times 100$$

**Pillar 2 — Classification Accuracy (Diagnostic Models)**

For binary classification (e.g., disease present vs. absent), accuracy is derived from the **Confusion Matrix**:

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

Where:
- $TP$ = True Positives (correctly identified disease cases)
- $TN$ = True Negatives (correctly identified healthy cases)
- $FP$ = False Positives (Type I Error — false alarm)
- $FN$ = False Negatives (Type II Error — missed diagnosis)

**Pillar 3 — Predictive Accuracy (Regression Models)**

For continuous outcomes (e.g., predicting blood glucose, ICU stay duration):

**Mean Absolute Error (MAE):**

$$MAE = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lvert y_i - \hat{y}_i \rvert$$

**Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE):**

$$RMSE = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$$

**Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE):**

$$MAPE = \frac{100\%}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lvert \frac{y_i - \hat{y}_i}{y_i} \right\rvert$$

**Coefficient of Determination ($R^2$):**

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$$

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Range | Ideal Value | Use Case |
|--------|---------|-------|-------------|----------|
| **Measurement Error** | $\lvert x_m - x_t \rvert$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $0$ | Sensor calibration |
| **% Error** | $\dfrac{\lvert x_m - x_t \rvert}{x_t} \times 100$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $0$ | Device validation |
| **Classification Accuracy** | $\dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | $[0,1]$ | $1$ | Diagnostic tests |
| **Sensitivity (Recall)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $[0,1]$ | $1$ | Disease detection |
| **Specificity** | $\dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | $[0,1]$ | $1$ | Ruling out disease |
| **Precision (PPV)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $[0,1]$ | $1$ | Confirmatory tests |
| **F1-Score** | $\dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | $[0,1]$ | $1$ | Imbalanced datasets |
| **MAE** | $\dfrac{1}{n}\sum \lvert y_i - \hat{y}_i \rvert$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $0$ | Robust to outliers |
| **RMSE** | $\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n}\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $0$ | Penalizes large errors |
| **MAPE** | $\dfrac{100}{n}\sum \left\lvert \dfrac{y_i - \hat{y}_i}{y_i} \right\rvert$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $0$ | Scale-independent |
| **$R^2$ Score** | $1 - \dfrac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ | $(-\infty, 1]$ | $1$ | Variance explained |

### 2.3 The Why Behind Each Metric

- **Why RMSE penalizes large errors more than MAE?** Because of the **square operation** — errors are magnified quadratically. In clinical settings, this is vital: missing a tumor by 10 mm must be penalized far more than missing it by 2 mm.
- **Why classification accuracy fails on imbalanced data?** Consider a screening dataset with **990 healthy** and **10 diseased** patients. A naive model predicting "healthy" always achieves $99\%$ accuracy but fails completely to detect any disease. Hence, **sensitivity**, **specificity**, and **F1-score** are preferred.
- **Why $R^2$ can be negative?** When the model performs **worse than simply predicting the mean** ($\bar{y}$). The residual sum of squares exceeds the total sum of squares.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In FDA-approved medical AI systems (e.g., radiology CAD systems), the **minimum acceptable accuracy threshold** is typically **$\geq 90\%$** for binary classification and **RMSE $\leq 5\%$ of measurement range** for continuous monitoring devices. These are the regulatory benchmarks tested in viva voce.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Raw Error to Classification Accuracy

We begin from first principles. Suppose a binary classifier predicts whether a patient has a disease ($+1$) or is healthy ($-1$). For each patient $i$, let $y_i \in \{0, 1\}$ be the true label and $\hat{y}_i \in \{0, 1\}$ be the prediction.

**Step 1:** Define the indicator of a correct prediction:

$$\mathbb{1}_{correct}(i) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \hat{y}_i = y_i \\ 0 & \text{if } \hat{y}_i \neq y_i \end{cases}$$

**Step 2:** Total correct predictions across $n$ patients:

$$N_{correct} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}_{correct}(i)$$

**Step 3:** Accuracy is the fraction of correct predictions:

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{N_{correct}}{n} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}_{correct}(i)$$

**Step 4:** Expand the indicator. A correct prediction occurs in two cases:
- True Positive: $y_i = 1$ and $\hat{y}_i = 1$
- True Negative: $y_i = 0$ and $\hat{y}_i = 0$

**Step 5:** Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{1}{n}\left[\sum_{i: y_i=1, \hat{y}_i=1} 1 + \sum_{i: y_i=0, \hat{y}_i=0} 1\right] \\
&= \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}
\end{aligned}$$

This completes the derivation. Note that $TP + TN + FP + FN = n$ (total patients).

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Confusion Matrix Accuracy

**Problem Statement:** A new AI model for detecting pneumonia from chest X-rays is tested on **200 patients**. The confusion matrix is as follows:

| | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actually Positive** | $TP = 85$ | $FN = 15$ |
| **Actually Negative** | $FP = 10$ | $TN = 90$ |

**Step 1 — Total Patients Verification:**

$$TP + TN + FP + FN = 85 + 90 + 10 + 15 = 200 \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Compute Classification Accuracy:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} \\
&= \frac{85 + 90}{200} \\
&= \frac{175}{200} \\
&= 0.875 = 87.5\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Sensitivity (Recall):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Sensitivity} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\
&= \frac{85}{85 + 15} \\
&= \frac{85}{100} \\
&= 0.85 = 85\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Specificity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Specificity} &= \frac{TN}{TN + FP} \\
&= \frac{90}{90 + 10} \\
&= \frac{90}{100} \\
&= 0.90 = 90\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Precision (PPV):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Precision} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\
&= \frac{85}{85 + 10} \\
&= \frac{85}{95} \\
&= 0.8947 \approx 89.47\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Compute F1-Score:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F1 &= \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 0.8947 \times 0.85}{0.8947 + 0.85} \\
&= \frac{1.5210}{1.7447} \\
&= 0.8719 \approx 87.19\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Although overall accuracy is $87.5\%$, the **sensitivity ($85\%$)** is lower than **specificity ($90\%$)**. This indicates the model is better at ruling out disease than detecting it. For a screening tool, sensitivity should be prioritized (above $95\%$ ideally).

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: RMSE Derivation and Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A predictive model forecasts the **length of hospital stay (in days)** for 5 patients. Actual vs. predicted:

| Patient $i$ | Actual $y_i$ | Predicted $\hat{y}_i$ |
|------------|--------------|----------------------|
| 1 | 5 | 6 |
| 2 | 3 | 2 |
| 3 | 8 | 7 |
| 4 | 2 | 4 |
| 5 | 6 | 5 |

**Step 1 — Compute Residuals:**

$$\begin{aligned}
e_1 &= 5 - 6 = -1 \\
e_2 &= 3 - 2 = +1 \\
e_3 &= 8 - 7 = +1 \\
e_4 &= 2 - 4 = -2 \\
e_5 &= 6 - 5 = +1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Squared Residuals:**

$$\begin{aligned}
e_1^2 &= 1 \\
e_2^2 &= 1 \\
e_3^2 &= 1 \\
e_4^2 &= 4 \\
e_5^2 &= 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Sum of Squared Residuals (SSR):**

$$SSR = 1 + 1 + 1 + 4 + 1 = 8$$

**Step 4 — Compute MSE:**

$$MSE = \frac{SSR}{n} = \frac{8}{5} = 1.6$$

**Step 5 — Compute RMSE:**

$$RMSE = \sqrt{1.6} = 1.2649 \text{ days}$$

**Step 6 — Compute MAE for Comparison:**

$$\begin{aligned}
MAE &= \frac{1}{5}(\lvert -1 \rvert + \lvert 1 \rvert + \lvert 1 \rvert + \lvert -2 \rvert + \lvert 1 \rvert) \\
&= \frac{1}{5}(1 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 1) \\
&= \frac{6}{5} = 1.2 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** RMSE ($1.26$) > MAE ($1.2$) because RMSE penalizes the larger residual ($-2$ for patient 4) more heavily via squaring. This is the **desired behavior** in healthcare — large prediction errors must be flagged prominently.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define classification accuracy. Why is it an insufficient metric when dealing with imbalanced healthcare datasets?**

**Model Answer:**

Classification accuracy is defined as the ratio of correctly classified instances to the total number of instances:

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

It is insufficient for imbalanced datasets because consider a cancer screening dataset with **9,900 healthy** and **100 diseased** patients. A naive model predicting "healthy" for everyone achieves $99\%$ accuracy, yet fails to identify a single cancer case. In such scenarios, metrics like **sensitivity**, **specificity**, **precision**, **recall**, and **F1-score** provide a more meaningful evaluation.

**(3 Marks — 1 Mark definition, 2 Marks justification with example)**

---

**Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between MAE and RMSE as predictive accuracy metrics. Which one penalizes large errors more severely and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | MAE | RMSE |
|--------|-----|------|
| Formula | $\frac{1}{n}\sum \lvert y_i - \hat{y}_i \rvert$ | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$ |
| Sensitivity to outliers | Linear | Quadratic |
| Mathematical differentiability | Not at $0$ | Differentiable everywhere |

**RMSE penalizes large errors more severely** because of the **square operation** on residuals before averaging. A residual of magnitude $5$ contributes $25$ to RMSE but only $5$ to MAE. In clinical applications (e.g., drug dosage prediction), this property is essential to prevent catastrophic prediction failures.

**(3 Marks — 2 Marks comparison, 1 Mark reasoning)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern Note:** Students must answer ONE full question per module (either A or B). Each question contains two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define the **Confusion Matrix** for a binary diagnostic test. With a suitable example, derive the expressions for **accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and precision**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **Confusion Matrix** is a $2 \times 2$ tabular representation that compares the predictions of a classifier against the actual ground truth, broken down into four outcomes:

| | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actually Positive** | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) |
| **Actually Negative** | False Positive (FP) | True Negative (TN) |

**Example Context:** Consider an AI model predicting whether a patient has **diabetes** ($+$) or not ($-$) based on biomarker levels.

**Derivation:**

**1. Accuracy:**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{Correct Predictions}}{\text{Total Predictions}} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

**2. Sensitivity (True Positive Rate):**

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

This measures the model's ability to correctly identify **all actual positive cases**.

**3. Specificity (True Negative Rate):**

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$$

This measures the model's ability to correctly identify **all actual negative cases**.

**4. Precision (Positive Predictive Value):**

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

This measures, among all patients predicted positive, **how many truly have the disease**.

**Conclusion:** In clinical practice, **sensitivity** is prioritized for screening tests (do not miss cases), while **specificity** and **precision** are prioritized for confirmatory tests (avoid false alarms).

---

**(b)** A hospital deploys a deep learning model for detecting **cardiac arrhythmia** from ECG signals. The confusion matrix from a test of **500 patients** is:

| | **Predicted Arrhythmia** | **Predicted Normal** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Arrhythmia** | $TP = 180$ | $FN = 20$ |
| **Actual Normal** | $FP = 30$ | $TN = 270$ |

**Compute the following and interpret the clinical significance of each:**

(i) Classification Accuracy  
(ii) Sensitivity  
(iii) Specificity  
(iv) Precision  
(v) F1-Score  

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Verify Total:**

$$TP + TN + FP + FN = 180 + 270 + 30 + 20 = 500 \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Accuracy:**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{180 + 270}{500} = \frac{450}{500} = 0.90 = 90\%$$

This means the model correctly classifies $90\%$ of all patients.

**Step 3 — Sensitivity:**

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 = 90\%$$

$90\%$ of patients **with arrhythmia** are correctly identified. **Clinical significance:** For a life-threatening condition, missing $10\%$ (20 patients) is critical — further improvement is needed.

**Step 4 — Specificity:**

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{270}{270 + 30} = \frac{270}{300} = 0.90 = 90\%$$

$90\%$ of **normal patients** are correctly identified. **Clinical significance:** $10\%$ (30 patients) will undergo unnecessary stress tests and anxiety.
