> 2. **Forgetting that `bool` is a subclass of `int`:** `True + True` evaluates to `2` in Python. Students who write "TypeError" in such cases lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing `str` and `int` in concatenation:** `"Age: " + 45` raises `TypeError`. Always cast: `"Age: " + str(45)`.
> 4. **Treating `dict` keys as ordered by value:** Dictionaries preserve insertion order (Python 3.7+), but conceptually they are keyed lookups, not sorted structures.
> 5. **Forgetting to import or close files** when extending these examples — examiners expect production-quality code.
> 6. **Single vs double quotes:** Both work in Python, but be consistent. Examiners appreciate PEP 8 compliance.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Python Variables and Types**

- ☐ A Python **variable is a name bound to an object**, not a container of the value.
- ☐ Python uses **dynamic typing** — type is inferred at runtime, no declaration required.
- ☐ Use `type()` to inspect a variable's type, `isinstance()` for safe type checking.
- ☐ **Numeric types:** `int` (whole), `float` (decimal), `complex` (real + imaginary).
- ☐ **Sequence types:** `str` (immutable), `list` (mutable), `tuple` (immutable).
- ☐ **Mapping type:** `dict` (key-value pairs, mutable).
- ☐ **Set type:** `set` (unique unordered elements, mutable).
- ☐ **Boolean:** `bool` is a subclass of `int` (`True == 1`, `False == 0`).
- ☐ **NoneType:** The singleton `None` represents absence of a value.
- ☐ **Mutability rule:** `list`, `dict`, `set` are mutable; `int`, `float`, `str`, `tuple`, `bool` are immutable.
- ☐ **Casting functions:** `int()`, `float()`, `str()`, `list()`, `tuple()`, `set()`, `dict()`.
- ☐ **Implicit promotion:** `int + float → float`; `int / int → float` in Python 3.
- ☐ **PEP 8 naming:** Use `snake_case` for variables; names are case-sensitive.
- ☐ Reserved keywords (`if`, `for`, `class`, `def`, `return`, `True`, `False`, `None`) cannot be used as variable names.
- ☐ In **healthcare analytics**, always validate input types before clinical calculations to prevent silent data errors.
- ☐ Prefer `isinstance()` over `type() ==` in production code for inheritance-aware checks.
- ☐ Strings can be delimited by single `'...'`, double `"..."`, or triple `"""..."""` quotes.

---

> **End of Module 2 — Python: Variables and Types**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | PECDT862 Healthcare Data Analytics*

topics.title: Data Structures and Containers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT862 - HEALTHCARE DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Structures and Containers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Healthcare Data Analytics (PECDT862)**, **Data Structures and Containers** refer to the organized, systematic storage formats used to hold, retrieve, manipulate, and analyze heterogeneous and high-dimensional medical data — including Electronic Health Records (EHR), clinical trial datasets, genomic sequences, IoT-derived physiological signals, and insurance claim records.

Per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, the term *Containers* is used in a broader Python-ecosystem sense: built-in container types (`list`, `tuple`, `set`, `dict`) combined with scientific libraries such as **`numpy.ndarray`**, **`pandas.DataFrame`**, and **`pandas.Series`**, all of which are essential primitives for ingesting, cleaning, and transforming clinical data prior to downstream analytics or machine-learning pipelines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** *Data Structures and Containers* is a foundational topic. Mastery of indexing, mutability, hashability, time complexity, and axis-based operations in NumPy/Pandas is mandatory for answering both Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) ESE questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **Hospital Records Department**. Every incoming patient record is a paper file. How you file it determines how fast a doctor can find it later.

- A **`list`** is like a **stack of files in a single drawer** — ordered, you can add/remove from either end, and files can repeat.
- A **`tuple`** is like a **sealed envelope** — the contents (e.g., a patient's date of birth) cannot be altered once sealed.
- A **`set`** is like a **unique-ID register** — every entry must be unique; no duplicates allowed, and order is irrelevant.
- A **`dictionary`** is like an **indexed cabinet** — each file has a unique label (e.g., MRN — Medical Record Number), and you retrieve it instantly by label.
- A **`pandas.DataFrame`** is the **entire multi-column spreadsheet** — rows are patients, columns are clinical variables (BP, Glucose, Cholesterol).
- A **`numpy.ndarray`** is a **high-speed laboratory grid** — uniform type, vectorized arithmetic, optimized for medical imaging matrices and signal arrays.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world healthcare context:** A hospital database with **2 million patient records** requires a hash-based container (`dict`) for **O(1)** MRN lookups; sequential search through a `list` would be **O(n)** and clinically unacceptable in emergency retrieval scenarios.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Properties (Bolded Key Terms)

The key measurable characteristics of every container are:
- **Time Complexity** — Big-O notation for insertion, deletion, and lookup.
- **Space Complexity** — Memory overhead per element.
- **Mutability** — Ability to modify in-place after creation.
- **Hashability** — Ability to be used as a dictionary key or set element.
- **Homogeneity** — Whether all elements must be of the same data type.
- **Order Preservation** — Whether insertion order is retained.

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU board exam, always explicitly state the **Big-O complexity** when discussing any data structure. Examiners award marks for time-complexity justifications.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Data Containers

Data containers used in healthcare analytics are classified along three orthogonal axes:

### A. By Storage Philosophy
1. **Built-in Python Containers** — `list`, `tuple`, `set`, `dict`
2. **NumPy Containers** — `ndarray` (N-dimensional array)
3. **Pandas Containers** — `Series`, `DataFrame`, `Index`

### B. By Dimensionality
| Dimensionality | Container | Healthcare Example |
|---|---|---|
| 0-D (Scalar) | `numpy scalar` | A single patient's systolic BP = 120 mmHg |
| 1-D | `list`, `tuple`, `set`, `numpy 1-D`, `pandas Series` | Heart rate time series of one patient |
| 2-D | `list of lists`, `numpy 2-D`, `pandas DataFrame` | Patient × Lab-Test matrix |
| N-D | `numpy ndarray` | 3-D MRI / CT scan voxel volume |

### C. By Access Pattern
- **Sequential Access** — `list`, `tuple`
- **Random Access by Index** — `list`, `tuple`, `numpy ndarray`
- **Random Access by Key (Hash Map)** — `dict`, `set`, `pandas Series/DataFrame.loc`

## 2.2 Detailed Operational Breakdown of Each Container

### 1. `list` — The Mutable Sequence
- **What:** Ordered, mutable, allows duplicates, heterogeneous types.
- **Why in healthcare:** Storing a patient's chronological list of diagnoses (ICD-10 codes) where order matters.
- **How it works internally:** A `list` in CPython is implemented as a **dynamic array of pointers (PyObject*)**. Appending is amortized **O(1)**, but occasional resizing incurs **O(n)** cost.
- **Key Operations:**
  - Append / Extend / Insert — **O(1)** amortized / **O(k)** / **O(n)**
  - Indexing / Slicing — **O(1)** / **O(k)**
  - Membership test (`in`) — **O(n)**
  - Sort — **O(n log n)** (Timsort)

### 2. `tuple` — The Immutable Sequence
- **What:** Ordered, immutable, hashable (if contents are hashable).
- **Why in healthcare:** Storing immutable constants like `(patient_id, visit_date)` as a composite dictionary key.
- **How it works:** Fixed-size array of pointers; slightly faster iteration than `list` due to no overhead of mutation checks.
- **Key Operations:**
  - Indexing — **O(1)**
  - Concatenation — creates a new tuple, **O(n)**
  - Hashing — **O(n)** for n elements.

### 3. `set` — The Unordered Unique Collection
- **What:** Unordered, mutable, no duplicates, hashable elements only.
- **Why in healthcare:** Maintaining a registry of unique ICD-10 codes for cohort selection; set difference (`A − B`) for finding patients on a specific drug vs. control group.
- **How it works:** Backed by a **hash table** with open addressing. Average operations are **O(1)**.
- **Key Operations:**
  - Add / Remove / Membership — average **O(1)**, worst **O(n)**
  - Union, Intersection, Difference — **O(min(len(A), len(B)))** to **O(len(A) + len(B))**

### 4. `dict` — The Key-Value Hash Map
- **What:** Ordered (Python 3.7+ insertion order), mutable, unique keys, hashable keys.
- **Why in healthcare:** Fast lookup of patient records by MRN. Mapping comorbidity codes to their clinical descriptions.
- **How it works:** **Hash table** with open addressing; insertion-order is preserved as a separate linked list.
- **Key Operations:**
  - Get / Set / Delete — average **O(1)**, worst **O(n)**
  - Iteration over keys/values/items — **O(n)**
  - Resizing — triggered at 2/3 load factor; **O(n)** amortized.

### 5. `numpy.ndarray` — The Homogeneous N-Dimensional Array
- **What:** Fixed-type, fixed-size, contiguous (or strided) memory, vectorized arithmetic.
- **Why in healthcare:** Medical imaging (MRI/CT pixel intensity matrices), ECG/EEG signal arrays, batch processing of lab values.
- **How it works:** A **strided memory view** over a single contiguous buffer. Strides define how to jump in memory for each axis.
- **Key Operations:**
  - Element-wise arithmetic — **O(n)** (vectorized C loop, ~10–100x faster than Python loops)
  - Reshape, Transpose, Broadcast — **O(1)** (view) or **O(n)** (copy)
  - Aggregation along axis — **O(n)**
  - Fancy Indexing / Boolean Masking — **O(n)**

### 6. `pandas.DataFrame` — The Labeled 2-D Heterogeneous Table
- **What:** Labeled rows (index) and labeled columns; each column is a `Series`; supports heterogeneous types; built on NumPy.
- **Why in healthcare:** **The de-facto standard for clinical/tabular analytics** — patients as rows, clinical variables as columns, with metadata labels.
- **How it works:** Internally, a `DataFrame` is a collection of **column-aligned `ndarray` blocks** plus an `Index` object. Operations are vectorized via NumPy.
- **Key Operations:**
  - `loc[]` (label-based) / `iloc[]` (integer-based) — **O(1)** to **O(n)**
  - `groupby()` — **O(n log n)**
  - `merge()` / `join()` — **O(n + m)** to **O(n log n)**
  - `apply()` row-/column-wise — **O(n)** per call
  - `query()` / Boolean filtering — **O(n)**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Container | Mutable | Ordered | Duplicates | Indexing | Avg. Lookup | Avg. Insert | Memory Model | Healthcare Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `list` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | Integer | **O(1)** | **O(1)** amortized | Dynamic array of pointers | Chronological visit log |
| `tuple` | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | Integer | **O(1)** | N/A (immutable) | Fixed array of pointers | Composite key `(MRN, date)` |
| `set` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | N/A | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | Hash table | Unique ICD-10 code registry |
| `dict` | ✅ | ✅ (insertion) | Keys ❌, Values ✅ | Key (hash) | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | Hash table | MRN → patient record |
| `numpy.ndarray` | ❌ (view ✅) | ✅ (axis) | ✅ | Integer / slice | **O(1)** | N/A | Contiguous / strided buffer | MRI pixel matrix |
| `pandas.Series` | ✅ | ✅ (index) | ✅ (values) | Label / Integer | **O(1)** | **O(1)** amortized | NumPy array + Index | Single lab test time series |
| `pandas.DataFrame` | ✅ | ✅ (rows & cols) | ✅ | Label / Integer | **O(1)** | **O(1)** amortized | BlockManager over Ndarrays | Patient × Variable clinical table |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Board Tip:** When asked "which container is best for X?", justify using **three criteria**: (1) access pattern (key vs index vs position), (2) mutability requirement, (3) time complexity of the dominant operation. Marks are awarded for justification, not just naming.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospital Information Systems (HIS):** `dict` enables **O(1)** patient lookup, critical for emergency departments where retrieval latency directly impacts mortality.
- **Genomics & Bioinformatics:** `numpy.ndarray` stores DNA/RNA sequence data; vectorized operations enable fast GC-content, k-mer counting, and alignment score matrices.
- **Clinical Trials (CDISC SDTM/ADaM):** `pandas.DataFrame` is the industry standard; FDA submissions increasingly accept pandas-readable formats.
- **Epidemiology:** `set` operations (intersection, union) enable fast computation of disease prevalence, comorbidity overlap, and cohort intersection.
- **IoT Wearables (continuous vitals):** `numpy` and `pandas` enable **rolling-window** analytics (e.g., 5-min moving average of SpO₂) at scale.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Optimal Container

To make this section KTU-board-ready, we derive a **decision matrix** for healthcare-data storage. The process is shown step-by-step so that you can reproduce it in the exam.

### Derivation: Amortized Cost of `list.append()`

A Python `list` over-allocates memory geometrically. Suppose the list doubles in capacity each time it is full. After *k* appends, the total number of element copies is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(k) &= 1 + 2 + 4 + 8 + \dots + 2^{\lfloor \log_2 k \rfloor} \\
     &= 2^{\lfloor \log_2 k \rfloor + 1} - 1 \\
     &\le 2k - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step logic:**
- After append 1 → no resize (capacity 4 or 8).
- After appends 1, 2, 3, 4 → resize to 8, copy 4 elements.
- After appends 5, 6, 7, 8 → resize to 16, copy 8 elements.
- Pattern: copies are $1 + 2 + 4 + \dots + 2^{m-1} = 2^m - 1$.

**Amortized cost per append:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Amortized cost} &= \frac{T(k)}{k} = \frac{2k - 1}{k} \le 2 \\
                      &= O(1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Healthcare interpretation:** Appending patient vitals to a streaming list is **O(1)** amortized, making it efficient for ICU real-time monitoring buffers.

---

### Derivation: Hash Table Load Factor and Resize Trigger

For a `dict` or `set`, the **load factor** $\alpha$ is defined as:

$$
\alpha = \frac{n}{m}
$$

where $n$ is the number of stored elements and $m$ is the number of slots in the hash table.

**Python's CPython implementation resizes when $\alpha \ge 2/3$** to maintain average-case **O(1)** lookup.

The expected number of probes for an unsuccessful search using open addressing with uniform hashing is:

$$
E[\text{probes}] = \frac{1}{1 - \alpha}
$$

**Step-by-step analysis:**
- At $\alpha = 0.5$ → expected probes ≈ **2.0**
- At $\alpha = 0.66$ (resize trigger) → expected probes ≈ **3.0**
- At $\alpha = 0.9$ → expected probes ≈ **10.0** (degraded)

**Healthcare interpretation:** For a patient registry with **1 million MRNs**, maintaining $\alpha \le 0.66$ ensures MRN lookups remain fast (≈3 probes), vital for emergency response systems.

---

### Derivation: NumPy Vectorization Speedup over Python Loop

Let $n$ be the length of an ECG signal array. Computing the mean using a Python loop:

$$
T_{\text{Python}}(n) = c_{\text{loop}} \cdot n
$$

where $c_{\text{loop}}$ includes per-iteration overhead (type dispatch, bytecode interpretation). Using NumPy vectorization:

$$
T_{\text{NumPy}}(n) = c_{\text{C}} \cdot n
$$

where $c_{\text{C}}$ is the C-level optimized constant. The **speedup** $S$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{T_{\text{Python}}(n)}{T_{\text{NumPy}}(n)} = \frac{c_{\text{loop}}}{c_{\text{C}}} \\
  &\approx 10 \text{ to } 100 \text{ (typical)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For an **n = 250,000** sample 30-min ECG recording at 250 Hz:
- Python loop: ~250,000 iterations × ~100 ns = **25 ms**
- NumPy vectorized: ~250,000 × ~1 ns = **0.25 ms**
- **Speedup ≈ 100x**

**Healthcare interpretation:** In a 12-bed ICU processing real-time ECG, vectorization frees CPU cycles for alarm-generation algorithms, reducing false-alarm latency.

---

### Derivation: Memory Footprint of `list` vs `numpy.ndarray` (Heterogeneous vs Homogeneous)

For a list of $n$ Python floats:
- Each `float` is a PyObject ≈ **28 bytes** (in 64-bit CPython)
- Each pointer in the list array ≈ **8 bytes**
- Total per element: $28 + 8 = 36$ bytes
- Total: $36n$ bytes

For a NumPy `float64` array:
- Each element: **8 bytes** (raw IEEE-754 double)
- Total: $8n$ bytes

**Compression ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{\text{Memory}_{\text{list}}}{\text{Memory}_{\text{ndarray}}} = \frac{36n}{8n} = 4.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Healthcare interpretation:** A 10-million-row lab dataset stored as a `list` consumes ~**360 MB**, but as a NumPy array only ~**80 MB** — a 4.5× reduction, critical for hospital servers with limited RAM.

---

### Derivation: `pandas.DataFrame` Boolean Masking — Cohort Selection

To select all patients with `age > 65` **AND** `diabetic == True` from a DataFrame $D$ of shape $(n, k)$:

$$
D_{\text{filtered}} = D \left[ (D[\text{age}] > 65) \;\&\; (D[\text{diabetic}] == \text{True}) \right]
$$

**Step-by-step cost:**
- Step 1: Compute boolean mask for `age > 65` — **O(n)**, allocates array of size $n$.
- Step 2: Compute boolean mask for `diabetic == True` — **O(n)**.
- Step 3: Element-wise AND — **O(n)**.
- Step 4: Fancy indexing to extract rows — **O(m)** where $m$ is the number of matching rows.

**Total: O(n)**, dominated by the linear scan.

**Healthcare interpretation:** For an elderly diabetic cohort study on $n = 100{,}000$ records, the filter completes in ~10 ms — a negligible cost compared to chart-review time.

---

## 3.2 Comparative Performance Summary Table

| Operation | `list` | `tuple` | `set` | `dict` | `numpy.ndarray` | `pandas.DataFrame` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lookup by key/index | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | **O(1)** to **O(n)** |
| Membership test | **O(n)** | **O(n)** | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | **O(n)** (linear scan) | **O(n)** |
| Insert at end | **O(1)** amort. | ❌ | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | ❌ | **O(1)** amort. |
| Insert at front/middle | **O(n)** | ❌ | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | ❌ | **O(n)** |
| Sort | **O(n log n)** | **O(n log n)** | ❌ | ❌ | **O(n log n)** | **O(n log n)** |
| Set algebra (∪, ∩) | Manual | Manual | **O(n + m)** | Keys only | N/A | N/A |
| Memory per element | ~36 B (float) | ~36 B | ~72 B | ~72 B (k-v pair) | **8 B** (float64) | ~36–72 B |
| Type heterogeneity | ✅ | ✅ | Elements must be hashable | Keys hashable, Values any | ❌ (homogeneous) | ✅ (per column) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Container Classification Flowchart

```mermaid

    A[Healthcare Data Containers] --> B[Built-in Python]

```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Healthcare Data Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC[Data Sources]
        S1[EHR System]
        S2[Lab Information System]
        S3[IoT Wearables]
        S4[Insurance Claims]
    end

    subgraph ING[Ingestion Containers]
        I1[list of dicts streaming buffer]
        I2[dict MRN keyed lookup]
        I3[ndarray high frequency signal]
    end

        T1[pandas DataFrame tabular]

    end

    S1 --> I1
    S2 --> I2
    S3 --> I3

    I2 --> T2

    style SRC fill:#fce4d6

    style ANALYTICS fill:#e2efda
```

## 4.3 Hash Table Internal Layout for `dict`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Hash Function hash MRN] --> I1[Slot 0 empty]
    H --> I2[Slot 1 MRN 1001 record]
    H --> I3[Slot 2 MRN 1042 record]
    H --> I4[Slot 3 empty]
    H --> I5[Slot 4 MRN 1073 record]
    H --> I6[Slot 5 collision MRN 1009]
    I6 --> P1[Probed to Slot 7]
    H --> I7[Slot 6 empty]
    H --> I8[Slot 7 MRN 1009 record]

    style H fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style I2 fill:#c6e0b4
    style I3 fill:#c6e0b4
    style I5 fill:#c6e0b4
    style I6 fill:#ffd966
    style I8 fill:#c6e0b4
    style I1 fill:#f2f2f2
    style I4 fill:#f2f2f2
    style I7 fill:#f2f2f2
    style P1 fill:#ffd966
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The collision at slot 5 (MRN 1009) is resolved by **open addressing with probing** to slot 7. The **load factor** is 4/8 = 0.5, which is well below Python's resize threshold of 2/3.

## 4.4 NumPy `ndarray` Strided Memory Layout (2-D ECG Segment)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ndarray shape 2x4 strides 32 bytes 8 bytes] --> B[Row 0 Lead II ECG]
    A --> C[Row 1 Lead V5 ECG]
    B --> B1[Sample 0]
    B --> B2[Sample 1]
    B --> B3[Sample 2]
    B --> B4[Sample 3]
    C --> C1[Sample 0]
    C --> C2[Sample 1]
    C --> C3[Sample 2]
    C --> C4[Sample 3]

    B1 -. offset 0 .-> M1[Memory buffer contiguous]
    B2 -. offset 8 .-> M1
    B3 -. offset 16 .-> M1
    B4 -. offset 24 .-> M1
    C1 -. offset 32 .-> M1
    C2 -. offset 40 .-> M1
    C3 -. offset 48 .-> M1
    C4 -. offset 56 .-> M1

    style A fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#e2efda
    style C fill:#e2efda
    style M1 fill:#bf9000,color:#ffffff
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
Differentiate between a Python `list` and a `tuple`. Give **one** healthcare-specific use case for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `list` | `tuple` |
|---|---|---|
| Mutability | Mutable — can append, remove, modify | Immutable — fixed after creation |
| Syntax | `[1, 2, 3]` | `(1, 2, 3)` |
| Performance | Slower (extra overhead) | Faster (no mutation checks) |
| Hashable | ❌ No (unhashable) | ✅ Yes (if contents hashable) |

- **List use case (1 mark):** Storing a patient's evolving list of prescribed medications where new drugs are added/removed over time.
- **Tuple use case (1 mark):** Storing an immutable composite key like `(patient_MRN, visit_date)` to uniquely identify a clinical encounter in a `dict` lookup.
- **Key difference (1 mark):** Mutability and hashability.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
Explain with an example why a `pandas.DataFrame` is preferred over a Python `list` for storing tabular clinical data.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Columnar typed storage (1 mark):** Each column in a `DataFrame` has a homogeneous NumPy-backed dtype (e.g., `float64` for glucose values), enabling vectorized operations and ~10–100× speedup over Python loops.
- **Label-based indexing (1 mark):** Rows and columns are addressable by labels (e.g., `df.loc["MRN_1001", "glucose"]`), making code self-documenting in clinical contexts.
- **Example (1 mark):** `df[df["age"] > 65]` filters all elderly patients in **O(n)**, whereas the same in a list requires manual loops and is error-prone and slower.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**(a)** Compare and contrast the Python built-in containers `list`, `tuple`, `set`, and `dict` in terms of mutability, order, duplicates, hashing, and average time complexity of lookup and insertion. Tabulate your answer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hospital maintains a registry of 5,00,000 (5 lakh) patient records, each identified by a unique Medical Record Number (MRN). For emergency retrieval, the system must fetch a patient's full record by MRN in **O(1)** average time. Design a suitable container architecture, justify your choice, and explain how Python's `dict` achieves O(1) lookup using hash tables. Discuss the role of the **load factor** and **collision resolution**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Tabular Comparison (7 marks):**

| Property | `list` | `tuple` | `set` | `dict` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mutability | Mutable | Immutable | Mutable | Mutable |
| Ordered? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (insertion order in Py3.7+ is incidental) | ✅ Yes (insertion order since Py3.7) |
| Duplicates allowed? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Keys: ❌ / Values: ✅ |
| Hashable? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (if contents hashable) | N/A (it is hashable) | N/A (it is hashable) |
| Indexing type | Integer position | Integer position | N/A | Key (any hashable) |
| Avg. Lookup | **O(1)** by index | **O(1)** by index | **O(1)** by hash | **O(1)** by key |
| Avg. Insertion | **O(1)** amortized (end) | ❌ | **O(1)** | **O(1)** |
| Avg. Deletion | **O(n)** (front) / **O(1)** (end) | ❌ | **O(1)** | **O(1)** |
| Memory per element | ~36 B (float) | ~36 B | ~72 B | ~72 B (k-v pair) |
| Iteration order | Insertion | Insertion | Undefined | Insertion (Py3.7+) |
| Healthcare example | Visit log | Composite key | Unique ICD-10 codes | MRN → record |

**(b) Container Architecture for 5 Lakh Patient Records (7 marks):**

**1. Container Choice — `dict` (2 marks):**
- Mapping MRN (string/int) → patient record (dict of clinical fields).
- Average lookup is **O(1)**, essential for emergency retrieval where sub-second response is critical.
- Average insertion is **O(1)** amortized; resizing cost is amortized over many operations.

**2. Hash Table Mechanics in `dict` (3 marks):**
- The MRN is passed through Python's built-in `hash()` function, producing a 64-bit integer hash.
- The hash is mapped to a slot index: `slot = hash(MRN) & (capacity - 1)` (bitwise AND with capacity-1, since capacity is a power of 2).
- The (key, value) pair is stored at the computed slot.
- Lookup: hash the MRN, compute the slot, compare the key — if equal, return value; if not (collision), probe to the next slot.
- Average case: **O(1)** because with a good hash function and load factor < 2/3, probing terminates in ~1–3 steps.

**3. Load Factor & Collision Resolution (2 marks):**
- **Load factor** $\alpha = n / m$ where $n$ = entries, $m$ = slots.
- Python resizes the table (typically doubling capacity) when $\alpha \ge 2/3$ to keep probing short.
- For 5,00,000 entries, the table resizes to $m = 2^{\lceil \log_2(5,00,000 / (2/3)) \rceil} = 2^{20} = 1,048,576$ slots.
- **Collision resolution:** Python uses **open addressing with a perturbation-based probing sequence** (a variant of double hashing) to handle hash collisions, ensuring cache-friendly access and predictable performance.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Explain the internal structure of a `numpy.ndarray`. How does **strided memory layout** enable efficient operations on multi-dimensional healthcare data such as 3-D MRI voxel volumes? Illustrate with a diagram description. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A clinical dataset has 1,00,000 patient records with columns: `patient_id`, `age`, `glucose_mg_dL`, `systolic_BP`, `diabetic` (bool), and `visit_date`. You are required to (i) compute the mean glucose and systolic BP for diabetic patients above age 60, (ii) count unique ICD-10 codes, and (iii) identify the top-5 most frequent diagnoses. Specify the optimal container for each sub-task and justify using Big-O complexity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) `numpy.ndarray` Internal Structure (7 marks):**

**1. Data Buffer (2 marks):**
- An `ndarray` is a **view over a contiguous (or strided) memory buffer** holding raw, fixed-type elements (e.g., `float64` = 8 bytes each).
- Unlike a Python `list`, all elements share the **same dtype**, eliminating per-element type pointers and yielding ~4.5× memory savings.

**2. Strided Memory Layout (2 marks):**
- The array has metadata: `shape`, `dtype`, `strides`, and a pointer to the data buffer.
- For a 3-D MRI volume of shape `(D, H, W) = (200, 256, 256)` with `float32` (4 bytes):
  - Stride along W = **4 bytes**
  - Stride along H = $256 \times 4 = 1024$ bytes
  - Stride along D = $256 \times 256 \times 4 = 262144$ bytes
- Indexing `arr[d, h, w]` is computed as:
  $$
  \text{offset} = d \cdot s_d + h \cdot s_h + w \cdot s_w
  $$
  This **O(1)** pointer arithmetic enables instant random access without copying.

**3. Vectorized Operations & Broadcasting (2 marks):**
- Element-wise ops (e.g., `arr * 2`, `arr > threshold`) execute in optimized C loops over the contiguous buffer — **O(n)** but ~10–100× faster than Python.
- **Broadcasting** allows arithmetic between arrays of compatible shapes (e.g., subtracting a 1-D mean vector from a 2-D slice) **without allocating extra memory**, crucial for medical image normalization.

**4. Diagram (1 mark):**
- See Section 4.4 above for a 2-D ECG segment strided layout; the same principle extends to N-D.

**(b) Optimal Container Selection for Each Sub-task (7 marks):**

| Sub-task | Operation | Optimal Container | Justification (Big-O) | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Mean glucose & systolic BP for `diabetic == True` AND `age > 60` | Filter + group aggregation | **`pandas.DataFrame`** | Boolean masking is **O(n)**; `groupby().mean()` is **O(n)**. NumPy-backed columns give vectorized speed. | 3 |
| (ii) Count unique ICD-10 codes | Set operations | **`set`** (or `Series.unique()`) | Set construction is **O(n)** average; `len(set)` is **O(1)**. Hash table enables **O(1)** membership. | 2 |
| (iii) Top-5 most frequent diagnoses | Frequency counting | **`collections.Counter`** (a `dict` subclass) or `pandas.Series.value_counts()` | Counter is **O(n)** average; `most_common(k)` is **O(k log k)** (heap) or **O(n log k)**. | 2 |

**Code Skeleton (conceptual, not executable):**
- Step 1: `mask = (df["diabetic"] == True) & (df["age"] > 60)` — **O(n)**
- Step 2: `subset = df.loc[mask]` — fancy indexing, **O(m)** where m = matches.
- Step 3: `subset[["glucose_mg_dL", "systolic_BP"]].mean()` — vectorized, **O(m)**.
- Step 4: `unique_codes = set(df["ICD10"])` — **O(n)**.
- Step 5: `top5 = df["ICD10"].value_counts().head(5)` — **O(n)**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing mutability with immutability:** Students often claim `tuple` is "constant" but forget to mention hashability. Always write *"immutable and hashable (if elements are hashable)"* for full marks.
> 2. **Wrong Big-O:** Writing **O(n)** for `dict` lookup is the most common error. The correct average is **O(1)**; only pathological collisions give **O(n)**.
> 3. **Ignoring load factor:** When justifying `dict` choice, examiners expect the **load factor α ≤ 2/3** and **resize doubling** mentioned explicitly.
> 4. **Conflating NumPy and Pandas:** NumPy is for **homogeneous N-D arrays**; Pandas is for **labeled tabular data**. Mixing them loses marks.
> 5. **No justification:** Naming a container without justifying *why* in terms of access pattern, mutability, and complexity costs at least **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Print & Pin to Your Wall!**

- ⭐ **`list`:** Mutable, ordered, dynamic-array backend, **O(1)** amortized append, **O(n)** membership, **O(n log n)** Timsort. Use for chronological event logs.
- ⭐ **`tuple`:** Immutable, hashable (if contents hashable), fixed-size, slightly faster than `list`. Use for composite keys and constants.
- ⭐ **`set`:** Unordered (Py3.7+ insertion order incidental), unique, hash-table backed, **O(1)** avg membership. Use for unique code registries and set algebra (∪, ∩, −).
- ⭐ **`dict`:** Ordered (Py3.7+ insertion), key-value hash map, **O(1)** avg get/set, resize at **α ≥ 2/3** via open addressing with perturbation probing. Use for MRN-keyed lookups.
- ⭐ **`numpy.ndarray`:** Homogeneous, fixed-dtype, **strided memory layout**, vectorized C-level ops, ~10–100× faster than Python loops, ~4.5× more memory-efficient. Use for imaging, signals, batch numerics.
- ⭐ **`pandas.Series`:** 1-D labeled NumPy-backed array with `Index`. Use for single-variable time series.
- ⭐ **`pandas.DataFrame`:** 2-D labeled table; column-aligned `ndarray` blocks; supports heterogeneous dtypes per column; built-in `loc[]`/`iloc[]`/`groupby()`/`merge()`. **The de-facto standard for clinical/tabular analytics.**
- ⭐ **Big-O you MUST memorize:** `list` lookup **O(1)**, `list` membership **O(n)**, `set`/`dict` ops **O(1)** avg, `sort` **O(n log n)**, `DataFrame` filter **O(n)**, `groupby` **O(n log n)**, `merge` **O(n + m)** to **O(n log n)**.
- ⭐ **Load factor α = n/m:** Python `dict` resizes at **α ≥ 2/3** to maintain O(1) average; expected probes = $1/(1 - \alpha)$.
- ⭐ **NumPy stride formula:** offset = $\sum_i \text{index}_i \cdot \text{stride}_i$.
- ⭐ **Hashability rule:** A container is hashable iff (a) it is immutable, and (b) all its elements are hashable. Hence `tuple` of `int`s is hashable; `list` of `int`s is not.
- ⭐ **Always state three justifications** in exam answers: (1) access pattern, (2) mutability, (3) dominant-operation Big-O.
- ⭐ **Memory hierarchy:** `ndarray` (8 B/element) < `tuple`/`list` of floats (~36 B/element) < `set`/`dict` (~72 B/element).
- ⭐ **Healthcare analogy:** `list` = visit log, `tuple` = sealed envelope, `set` = unique-ID register, `dict` = MRN-keyed cabinet, `ndarray` = imaging grid, `DataFrame` = full clinical spreadsheet.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 2 — Pandas DataFrame: Operations

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PECDT862 — Module 2):** This module deals with the **Python Data Analysis Library (Pandas)** — the de-facto standard for structured data manipulation in healthcare analytics. Mastery of `DataFrame` operations is a **mandatory CO (Course Outcome)** deliverable for the elective.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pandas DataFrame** is a **two-dimensional, size-mutable, tabular data structure** with **heterogeneously-typed, labeled axes (rows and columns)**. Formally, it is a dict-like container of `Series` objects, where each column is a one-dimensional `ndarray` with an associated index label.

Mathematically, a DataFrame $D$ can be expressed as:

$$D = \{(c_j, \mathbf{v}_j) \mid c_j \in \mathcal{C},\ \mathbf{v}_j \in \mathbb{R}^{n}\}_{j=1}^{m}$$

where $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of column labels, $m$ is the number of columns, and $\mathbf{v}_j$ is the value vector of length $n$ for column $c_j$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU-Exam Terminology to Memorize:**
> - **Index (axis 0):** Row labels.
> - **Columns (axis 1):** Column labels.
> - **Axes:** DataFrame has exactly **2 axes** (unlike Series, which has 1).
> - **dtypes:** A property that returns the data type of every column.
> - **shape:** Returns the tuple `(n_rows, n_columns)`.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Hospital Spreadsheet on Steroids 🏥**
>
> Imagine a hospital's **Electronic Medical Record (EMR) master sheet**. Each **column** is a clinical attribute (Patient ID, Age, BP, Glucose, Diagnosis), each **row** is a unique patient, and the **index** is the patient admission number.
>
> A **Pandas DataFrame is exactly that sheet** — but with three superpowers:
> 1. **Vectorized Arithmetic:** Compute the BMI of 10,000 patients in a single line, no loops.
> 2. **Label-Based Access:** Retrieve "all diabetic patients above age 60" using natural language filters.
> 3. **Missing-Data Tolerance:** Distinguish between `NaN`, `NaT`, and `None` natively, which a plain CSV cannot.
>
> In short: **CSV = static photograph; DataFrame = live, queryable database in memory.**

### 1.3 Why DataFrames are the Backbone of Healthcare Analytics

| Healthcare Scenario | DataFrame Operation Used |
|---|---|
| Cleaning ICU vital-sign logs | `dropna()`, `fillna()` |
| Cohort segmentation (e.g., diabetic vs non-diabetic) | Boolean masking: `df[df['Diagnosis']=='Diabetes']` |
| Merging patient demographics with lab results | `pd.merge()`, `pd.concat()` |
| Aggregating monthly admission counts | `groupby()` + `agg()` |
| Feature engineering for ML (e.g., BMI = W/H²) | Vectorized arithmetic, `apply()` |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a DataFrame

A DataFrame $D$ is internally composed of three components:

$$D = (\text{Index},\ \text{Columns},\ \text{Data Block})$$

- **Index (`df.index`):** Immutable sequence of row labels.
- **Columns (`df.columns`):** Mutable index object of column labels.
- **Data Block (`df.values`):** A 2-D `numpy.ndarray` (or mixed-extension array) holding the actual values.

> [!NOTE]
> **Homogeneity Rule:** Within a **single column**, all values share the same `dtype`. Across **columns**, `dtypes` may differ — this is what makes a DataFrame *heterogeneous by column* but *homogeneous by column*.

---

## 2.2 Classification of DataFrame Operations

For KTU board examinations, every operation falls into one of the following **six functional families**:

### Family 1 — Creation & I/O Operations

| Function | Purpose | Healthcare Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| `pd.DataFrame(dict_or_2d)` | Construct from dict / list-of-lists | Build patient cohort from in-memory list |
| `pd.read_csv("file.csv")` | Load CSV file | Read EHR exports |
| `pd.read_excel(...)` | Load Excel workbook | Hospital MIS reports |
| `df.to_csv("out.csv")` | Persist to disk | Save cleaned dataset |

### Family 2 — Selection, Indexing & Filtering

| Function | Syntax | Selection Mode |
|---|---|---|
| `df['col']` | Label-based, single col | Returns a `Series` |
| `df[['c1','c2']]` | Label-based, multi col | Returns a `DataFrame` |
| `df.loc[i, j]` | **Label-based**, single cell | `i` = row label, `j` = col label |
| `df.iloc[i, j]` | **Position-based**, single cell | `i, j` are integer positions |
| `df.loc[mask, :]` | Boolean masking | Row filter |
| `df.query("Age > 60")` | SQL-like expression | Filter |

### Family 3 — Structural Manipulation (Add / Delete / Rename)

| Function | Effect |
|---|---|
| `df['new'] = ...` | Add new column (right-aligned) |
| `df.insert(loc, col, val)` | Insert column at position `loc` |
| `df.drop('col', axis=1)` | Delete column |
| `df.drop(idx, axis=0)` | Delete row(s) |
| `df.rename(columns={...})` | Rename column labels |
| `df.set_index('col')` | Promote column to index |
| `df.reset_index()` | Demote index to column |

### Family 4 — Arithmetic & Statistical Operations

The following are **vectorized** (i.e., applied element-wise without explicit loops):

| Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `df + 10` | Scalar broadcasting — adds 10 to every cell |
| `df1 + df2` | Element-wise addition (aligned on labels) |
| `df.sum()`, `df.mean()` | Aggregate along axis (default `axis=0`) |
| `df.describe()` | Quick statistical summary |
| `df.corr()`, `df.cov()` | Pairwise correlation / covariance matrix |
| `df.value_counts()` | Frequency count per category |
| `df.nlargest(n, 'col')` | Top-n rows by column |

### Family 5 — Missing Data Handling

| Function | Behavior |
|---|---|
| `df.isnull()` / `df.notnull()` | Boolean mask of missing values |
| `df.dropna()` | Drop rows or columns containing `NaN` |
| `df.fillna(value)` | Replace `NaN` with a scalar or dict |
| `df.interpolate()` | Linear / polynomial interpolation |
| `df.duplicated()` | Boolean mask of duplicate rows |
| `df.drop_duplicates()` | Remove duplicate rows |

### Family 6 — Reshaping, Merging & Grouping

| Function | Purpose | Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| `df.pivot_table(...)` | Cross-tabulation | Excel pivot table |
| `df.melt()` | Wide → Long format | UNPIVOT in SQL |
| `pd.concat([df1, df2])` | Stack vertically/horizontally | `UNION ALL` in SQL |
| `pd.merge(df1, df2, on=...)` | SQL-style join | `JOIN` in SQL |
| `df.groupby('key')` | Split-Apply-Combine | `GROUP BY` in SQL |
| `df.pipe(f)` | Method chaining | Unix-style piping |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is a **single-page revision aid** covering all mathematically tractable DataFrame operations. **Memorize this verbatim** — these expressions are tested directly in Part-A questions.

| # | Operation | Formula / Syntax | Returns |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean of column $c$ | $\bar{x}_c = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} D_{i,c}$ | float |
| 2 | Variance of column $c$ | $s_c^{2} = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(D_{i,c}-\bar{x}_c)^{2}$ | float |
| 3 | Std. Deviation | $s_c = \sqrt{s_c^{2}}$ | float |
| 4 | Pearson correlation $\rho$ between $c_1, c_2$ | $\rho_{c_1,c_2}=\dfrac{\sum (D_{i,c_1}-\bar{x}_{c_1})(D_{i,c_2}-\bar{x}_{c_2})}{\sqrt{\sum (D_{i,c_1}-\bar{x}_{c_1})^{2}\sum (D_{i,c_2}-\bar{x}_{c_2})^{2}}}$ | float $\in [-1,1]$ |
| 5 | Z-score normalization | $z_{i,c} = \dfrac{D_{i,c} - \bar{x}_c}{s_c}$ | float |
| 6 | Min-Max normalization | $x'_{i,c} = \dfrac{D_{i,c} - \min(D_{:,c})}{\max(D_{:,c}) - \min(D_{:,c})}$ | float $\in [0,1]$ |
| 7 | BMI (clinical feature) | $\text{BMI} = \dfrac{W\,(\text{kg})}{H\,(\text{m})^{2}}$ | float |
| 8 | Body Surface Area (Mosteller) | $\text{BSA} = \sqrt{\dfrac{H\,(\text{cm}) \times W\,(\text{kg})}{3600}}$ | m² |
| 9 | Group-wise count | $N_{g} = \vert\{i : G_i = g\}\vert$ | int |
| 10 | Pivot table aggregation | $P_{r,c} = \text{agg}(\{D_{i,k} : R_i = r, C_i = c\})$ | numeric |

---

## 2.4 The Split-Apply-Combine Paradigm

The **`groupby()`** operation follows Hans Rosling's classical *Split-Apply-Combine* pattern:

$$D \xrightarrow{\text{Split by key } k} \{D_{g}\}_{g \in \mathcal{K}} \xrightarrow{\text{Apply } f} \{f(D_{g})\}_{g \in \mathcal{K}} \xrightarrow{\text{Combine}} D'$$

This is mathematically equivalent to a **partition of the index set** $I = \bigsqcup_{g} I_{g}$, followed by a function $f: D \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{p}$, then a final concatenation of the per-part outputs.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** In hospital analytics, groupby is used to compute the **mean LOS (Length of Stay)** per department, the **readmission rate** per diagnosis code, or the **average cost** per insurance provider.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section moves from **conceptual understanding** to **mathematical derivations and code-level analytical steps**, ensuring students can solve both **numerical and applied programming problems** in the KTU ESE.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example A — Building & Inspecting a Patient DataFrame

**Problem:** Create a DataFrame of 5 patients with columns `PatientID`, `Name`, `Age`, `Glucose`, `Diagnosis`. Then:
1. Display its `shape`, `dtypes`, and `describe()` summary.
2. Derive the **mean**, **variance**, and **standard deviation** of the `Glucose` column manually using the formula in §2.3.

### Step 1 — Data Creation

We construct the DataFrame from a Python `dict` (the most common exam-friendly approach):

$$\text{patients} = \begin{bmatrix} \text{PID} & \text{Name} & \text{Age} & \text{Glucose} & \text{Diagnosis} \\ \hline 101 & \text{Asha} & 45 & 110 & \text{Normal} \\ 102 & \text{Raj} & 62 & 180 & \text{Diabetes} \\ 103 & \text{Maya} & 34 & 95 & \text{Normal} \\ 104 & \text{Vinod} & 58 & 165 & \text{Diabetes} \\ 105 & \text{Leena} & 71 & 200 & \text{Diabetes} \end{bmatrix}$$

After loading, the structural metadata is:

$$D.\text{shape} = (5, 5), \quad D.\text{dtypes} = \{\text{PID: int64, Age: int64, Glucose: int64, Name: object, Diagnosis: object}\}$$

### Step 2 — Derivation of Mean of `Glucose`

The `Glucose` column vector is:

$$\mathbf{g} = [110,\ 180,\ 95,\ 165,\ 200]^{T}, \quad n = 5$$

Apply the mean formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x}_{g} &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} g_{i} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{5}(110 + 180 + 95 + 165 + 200) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{750}{5} = 150\ \text{mg/dL}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Derivation of Variance

The deviations are: $g_i - \bar{x}_g = [-40,\ +30,\ -55,\ +15,\ +50]$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{g}^{2} &= \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(g_i - \bar{x}_{g})^{2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{4}\big[(-40)^{2}+(30)^{2}+(-55)^{2}+(15)^{2}+(50)^{2}\big] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{4}[1600+900+3025+225+2500] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{8250}{4} = 2062.5\ (\text{mg/dL})^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Derivation of Standard Deviation

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{g} &= \sqrt{s_{g}^{2}} = \sqrt{2062.5} \\[4pt]
&\approx 45.42\ \text{mg/dL}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Verification:** `df['Glucose'].describe()` will return `mean = 150.0`, `std ≈ 45.415`. The slight difference (45.415 vs 45.42) is due to floating-point rounding; both are accepted by KTU evaluators.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example B — Boolean Indexing & Feature Engineering

**Problem:** From the patient DataFrame above:
1. Select all diabetic patients with `Age > 60`.
2. Engineer a new `BMI` column using **BMI = W / H²**, given a `Weight_kg` and `Height_m` dictionary.

### Step 1 — Boolean Mask Construction

The mask expression is a **logical AND** of two predicates:

$$\text{mask} = (D_{\text{Diagnosis}} = \text{'Diabetes'}) \ \wedge\ (D_{\text{Age}} > 60)$$

The filtered subset becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{filtered}} &= D[\text{mask}] \\[4pt]
&= \{(102, 180, 62), (105, 200, 71)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Vectorized BMI Computation

Let the new columns be defined as:
- $W = [70, 80, 55, 75, 68]^{T}$ (kg)
- $H = [1.65, 1.72, 1.60, 1.80, 1.55]^{T}$ (m)

Apply element-wise:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BMI}_i &= \frac{W_i}{H_i^{2}} \\[4pt]
\text{BMI} &= \left[\frac{70}{1.65^{2}},\ \frac{80}{1.72^{2}},\ \frac{55}{1.60^{2}},\ \frac{75}{1.80^{2}},\ \frac{68}{1.55^{2}}\right]^{T} \\[4pt]
&\approx [25.71,\ 27.04,\ 21.48,\ 23.15,\ 28.30]^{T}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** The expression `df['Weight_kg'] / (df['Height_m']**2)` is **vectorized** — it exploits NumPy's C-level SIMD instructions, making it **~100× faster** than a Python `for` loop. This is the single most important reason DataFrames are preferred in clinical pipelines.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example C — GroupBy Aggregation (Split-Apply-Combine)

**Problem:** Group the patient DataFrame by `Diagnosis` and compute the mean `Age` and mean `Glucose` per group.

### Step 1 — The Split

The index set $I = \{1,2,3,4,5\}$ is partitioned by the key `Diagnosis`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{Normal}} &= \{i : D_{i,\text{Diagnosis}} = \text{Normal}\} = \{1, 3\} \\[4pt]
I_{\text{Diabetes}} &= \{i : D_{i,\text{Diagnosis}} = \text{Diabetes}\} = \{2, 4, 5\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — The Apply (Mean Aggregation)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x}_{\text{Age, Normal}} &= \frac{45+34}{2} = 39.5 \\[4pt]
\bar{x}_{\text{Age, Diabetes}} &= \frac{62+58+71}{3} = 63.67 \\[6pt]
\bar{x}_{\text{Glucose, Normal}} &= \frac{110+95}{2} = 102.5 \\[4pt]
\bar{x}_{\text{Glucose, Diabetes}} &= \frac{180+165+200}{3} = 181.67
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — The Combine

The final result is a new DataFrame:

$$
D_{\text{grouped}} = \begin{bmatrix} \text{Diagnosis} & \text{Age} & \text{Glucose} \\ \hline \text{Normal} & 39.5 & 102.5 \\ \text{Diabetes} & 63.67 & 181.67 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is precisely the output of `df.groupby('Diagnosis').mean()`.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example D — Merge (SQL-Style Join)

**Problem:** You have two DataFrames:
- `df_patients` (5 rows, columns: `PatientID, Name, Age`)
- `df_visits` (6 rows, columns: `PatientID, Date, BP`)

Perform an **inner join** on `PatientID`.

### Step 1 — Conceptual Join Algebra

For an **inner join**, the result is:

$$D_{\text{merged}} = \{r \in D_{\text{patients}} \times D_{\text{visits}} : r.\text{PatientID}_{\text{left}} = r.\text{PatientID}_{\text{right}}\}$$

Equivalently, in set-builder notation:

$$D_{\text{merged}} = D_{\text{patients}} \bowtie_{\text{PatientID}} D_{\text{visits}}$$

### Step 2 — Cardinality Reasoning

If $|D_{\text{patients}}| = n_1$, $|D_{\text{visits}}| = n_2$, and a patient has on average $\lambda$ visits:

$$|D_{\text{inner}}| \approx n_1 \cdot \lambda$$

If a patient has **no visit**, they are **dropped** in an inner join. To preserve them, use a **left join**:

$$D_{\text{left}} = D_{\text{patients}} \ltimes_{\text{PatientID}} D_{\text{visits}} \ \text{(unmatched rows filled with NaN)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Students often confuse the `how` parameter in `pd.merge()`. The valid values are `'inner'`, `'outer'`, `'left'`, `'right'`, `'cross'`. The **default is `'inner'`** in `merge()` but **NOT** in `join()` (default is `left`).

---

## 3.5 Worked Example E — Pivot Table

**Problem:** Given a `df_visits` DataFrame with `Department, Month, PatientsSeen`, create a pivot where rows = `Department`, columns = `Month`, values = `PatientsSeen` (aggregated by `sum`).

### Step 1 — Mathematical Formulation

A pivot table computes:

$$P_{(d, m)} = \sum_{i:\ D_{i,\text{Dept}}=d,\ D_{i,\text{Month}}=m} D_{i,\text{PatientsSeen}}$$

Equivalently:

$$P = \text{AGG}\big(D,\ \text{index}=\text{Department},\ \text{columns}=\text{Month},\ \text{values}=\text{PatientsSeen},\ \text{aggfunc}=\text{sum}\big)$$

### Step 2 — NaN Filling

If a (Department, Month) pair has no observation, the cell is set to `NaN` (or a user-supplied `fill_value`).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Use-Case:** Hospital management uses pivot tables to generate the **monthly department-wise patient load report** — a mandatory KPI for NABH accreditation.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Operation Family | Time Complexity (avg) | Space Complexity | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Indexing / Selection | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | 🔥🔥🔥🔥🔥 |
| Boolean Masking | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | 🔥🔥🔥🔥 |
| GroupBy + Agg | $O(n \log k)$ | $O(k)$ | 🔥🔥🔥🔥🔥 |
| Merge (Inner) | $O(n_1 + n_2)$ hash | $O(n_1 + n_2)$ | 🔥🔥🔥🔥 |
| Pivot | $O(n)$ | $O(r \cdot c)$ | 🔥🔥🔥 |
| Sort | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | 🔥🔥 |
| Apply (row-wise) | $O(n)$ Python overhead | $O(n)$ | 🔥🔥 |

> [!NOTE]
> **$n$** = number of rows, **$k$** = number of unique group keys, **$r, c$** = rows/columns in pivot output.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are modeled on the **KTU 2024 End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern, with full **model solutions** and **examiner-style mark allocation**. Each question is tagged with its **CO (Course Outcome)**, **Bloom's Level**, and **simulated past-year reference** for familiarity.

---

## 📝 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, CO2, Bloom: Remember]**

> Define a Pandas DataFrame. List **any four** attributes that can be used to inspect its structure.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Pandas DataFrame** is a **two-dimensional, labeled data structure** in the Pandas library, capable of holding columns of different data types (heterogeneous). It is analogous to a spreadsheet or a SQL table.

**Four structural inspection attributes:**

1. **`df.shape`** → returns a tuple `(n_rows, n_columns)`. *(1 mark)*
2. **`df.dtypes`** → returns the data type of each column. *(1 mark)*
3. **`df.index`** → returns the row label index object. *(½ mark)*
4. **`df.columns`** → returns the column label index object. *(½ mark)*

*(Bonus: `df.values` returns the underlying NumPy array; `df.size` returns total cell count.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, CO2, Bloom: Understand]**

> Differentiate between `df.loc[]` and `df.iloc[]` with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `df.loc[]` | `df.iloc[]` |
|---|---|---|
| Indexing basis | **Label-based** (uses index/column names) | **Position-based** (uses integer positions) |
| Includes end label? | Yes, **inclusive** of both start and end labels | Yes, **exclusive** of end position (Python convention) |
| Example | `df.loc['P101', 'Glucose']` → returns glucose of patient with label P101 | `df.iloc[0, 3]` → returns the cell in the 1st row, 4th column |
| Boolean allowed? | Yes | Yes |

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for table, 1 mark for example.)*
