## 📝 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation §5.3**, every 14-mark question **must offer an internal choice** between two alternatives. The model below provides both **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

### **Question A — [14 Marks, CO2, CO3, Bloom: Apply + Analyze]**

> A hospital has maintained the following patient dataset in a CSV file `patients.csv`:
>
> | PatientID | Age | Weight_kg | Height_m | Glucose | BP | Diagnosis |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | 201 | 55 | 72 | 1.68 | 145 | 130 | Diabetes |
> | 202 | 48 | 65 | 1.60 | 110 | 120 | Normal |
> | 203 | 67 | 80 | 1.75 | 180 | 145 | Diabetes |
> | 204 | 35 | 58 | 1.62 | 95  | 115 | Normal |
> | 205 | 72 | 75 | 1.70 | 200 | 155 | Diabetes |
>
> **(a)** Write a complete Python program (using Pandas) to:
> - (i) Load the dataset,
> - (ii) Compute and display the **mean, median, and standard deviation** of the `Glucose` column,
> - (iii) Engineer a new column `BMI = Weight / Height²`,
> - (iv) Display the row corresponding to the **maximum Glucose** value. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Using the same dataset:
> - (i) Filter all **diabetic patients** above age **60**,
> - (ii) Group the dataset by `Diagnosis` and compute the **mean Age and mean Glucose** per group,
> - (iii) Create a **pivot table** with `Diagnosis` as rows, `BP_category` (Low < 120, Normal 120–140, High > 140) as columns, and the **count of patients** as values. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)** *(7 marks)*

**Step (i) — Load the data** *(1 mark)*

```python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("patients.csv")
print(df)
```

**Step (ii) — Descriptive statistics of Glucose** *(2 marks)*

```python
g = df['Glucose']
print("Mean   =", g.mean())      # → 146.0
print("Median =", g.median())    # → 145.0
print("Std Dev=", g.std())       # → 43.74
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Manual verification (for ½ mark of analytical reasoning):**
> Mean = (145+110+180+95+200)/5 = 730/5 = **146.0**
> Median = middle value = **145.0**

**Step (iii) — Vectorized BMI computation** *(2 marks)*

```python
df['BMI'] = df['Weight_kg'] / (df['Height_m'] ** 2)
print(df[['PatientID', 'BMI']])
```

**Step (iv) — Row with max Glucose** *(2 marks)*

```python
max_idx = df['Glucose'].idxmax()
print(df.loc[max_idx])
```

Output: row `205` with `Glucose=200, Diagnosis=Diabetes`.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)** *(7 marks)*

**Step (i) — Boolean masking for elderly diabetics** *(2 marks)*

```python
elderly_diabetics = df[(df['Diagnosis'] == 'Diabetes') & (df['Age'] > 60)]
print(elderly_diabetics)
```

The mask is a **logical AND** of two predicates evaluated element-wise. The output will contain patients `203` and `205`.

**Step (ii) — GroupBy aggregation** *(2 marks)*

```python
grouped = df.groupby('Diagnosis')[['Age', 'Glucose']].mean()
print(grouped)
```

**Step (iii) — Pivot table with `BP_category`** *(3 marks)*

```python
def categorize_bp(bp):
    if bp < 120:   return 'Low'
    elif bp <= 140: return 'Normal'
    else:           return 'High'

df['BP_category'] = df['BP'].apply(categorize_bp)

pivot = df.pivot_table(
    index='Diagnosis',
    columns='BP_category',
    values='PatientID',
    aggfunc='count',
    fill_value=0
)
print(pivot)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting parentheses** in compound conditions: `df['Diagnosis']=='Diabetes' & df['Age']>60` raises an `ValueError` due to **operator-precedence bug**. Always wrap each predicate in `()`.
> 2. **Using `&` vs `and`:** In Pandas, the **bitwise `&`** is used for element-wise boolean combination — **not** the Python keyword `and`. Wrong operator = **0 marks** for the masking step.
> 3. **BMI without parentheses** around the squared term: `df['Weight_kg'] / df['Height_m']**2` is correct, but `df['Weight_kg'] / df['Height_m']**2` differs from `(df['Weight_kg'] / df['Height_m'])**2`. Use explicit parentheses.
> 4. **Pivot without `fill_value`:** Missing combinations become `NaN`, which may fail a downstream `.sum()`. Always set `fill_value=0`.

---

### **Question B — [14 Marks, CO2, CO3, Bloom: Apply + Analyze] — ALTERNATIVE**

> Given two Pandas DataFrames:
>
> **DataFrame 1 — `df_admissions`** (5 rows):
> | AdmID | PatientID | Department | AdmissionDate |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | A01 | 201 | Cardiology | 2024-01-15 |
> | A02 | 202 | Neurology  | 2024-01-17 |
> | A03 | 203 | Cardiology | 2024-02-01 |
> | A04 | 204 | Orthopedics| 2024-02-10 |
> | A05 | 205 | Cardiology | 2024-03-05 |
>
> **DataFrame 2 — `df_discharge`** (4 rows):
> | AdmID | DischargeDate | Outcome |
> |---|---|---|
> | A01 | 2024-01-22 | Recovered |
> | A02 | 2024-01-25 | Recovered |
> | A03 | 2024-02-09 | Recovered |
> | A04 | 2024-02-15 | Transferred |
>
> **(a)** Perform the following operations: *(7 marks)*
> - (i) Construct both DataFrames using `pd.DataFrame()`.
> - (ii) Merge them on `AdmID` using an **inner join** and report the merged shape.
> - (iii) Compute the **Length of Stay (LOS)** in days for each patient, adding it as a new column.
> - (iv) Identify and display the **department with the longest average LOS**.
>
> **(b)** Then: *(7 marks)*
> - (i) Perform a **left join** and explain why the result has a different number of rows.
> - (ii) Concatenate a new single-row DataFrame for a **new admission** with the original `df_admissions`.
> - (iii) Handle any missing values in the merged DataFrame using `fillna('Pending')` and report the change.

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)** *(7 marks)*

**Step (i) — Constructing DataFrames** *(2 marks)*

```python
import pandas as pd

df_admissions = pd.DataFrame({
    'AdmID': ['A01','A02','A03','A04','A05'],
    'PatientID': [201,202,203,204,205],
    'Department': ['Cardiology','Neurology','Cardiology','Orthopedics','Cardiology'],
    'AdmissionDate': pd.to_datetime(['2024-01-15','2024-01-17','2024-02-01','2024-02-10','2024-03-05'])
})

df_discharge = pd.DataFrame({
    'AdmID': ['A01','A02','A03','A04'],
    'DischargeDate': pd.to_datetime(['2024-01-22','2024-01-25','2024-02-09','2024-02-15']),
    'Outcome': ['Recovered','Recovered','Recovered','Transferred']
})
```

**Step (ii) — Inner merge** *(2 marks)*

```python
merged_inner = pd.merge(df_admissions, df_discharge, on='AdmID', how='inner')
print("Shape:", merged_inner.shape)   # → (4, 6)
```

**Step (iii) — Length of Stay (LOS)** *(2 marks)*

```python
merged_inner['LOS_days'] = (merged_inner['DischargeDate'] - merged_inner['AdmissionDate']).dt.days
print(merged_inner[['AdmID','Department','LOS_days']])
```

**Step (iv) — Department with longest average LOS** *(1 mark)*

```python
avg_los = merged_inner.groupby('Department')['LOS_days'].mean()
print(avg_los.idxmax(), "with avg LOS =", avg_los.max(), "days")
```

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)** *(7 marks)*

**Step (i) — Left join explanation** *(2 marks)*

```python
merged_left = pd.merge(df_admissions, df_discharge, on='AdmID', how='left')
print("Shape:", merged_left.shape)   # → (5, 6)
```

**Explanation:** The left join keeps **all 5 rows** of `df_admissions`, including patient `A05` who has **no matching discharge record** — the discharge columns for that row will be `NaN`. The inner join only kept 4 rows, dropping A05.

**Step (ii) — Concatenation** *(2 marks)*

```python
new_row = pd.DataFrame({
    'AdmID':['A06'], 'PatientID':[206],
    'Department':['Neurology'],
    'AdmissionDate': pd.to_datetime(['2024-03-20'])
})

df_admissions_ext = pd.concat([df_admissions, new_row], ignore_index=True)
print(df_admissions_ext.tail(2))
```

**Step (iii) — Missing-value handling** *(3 marks)*

```python
merged_filled = merged_left.fillna({'DischargeDate':'Pending','Outcome':'Pending'})
print(merged_filled.tail(2))
```

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> 1. **Date arithmetic without `pd.to_datetime`:** Subtracting raw strings raises `TypeError`. Convert first.
> 2. **Forgetting `ignore_index=True` in `concat`:** The new row will inherit index `0` of the original — causing a **duplicate index**. Always pass `ignore_index=True` for clean sequential indexing.
> 3. **Confusing `how='outer'` vs `how='left'`:** Outer join gives the **union of both** sides (5+1=6 rows if A05 has no match, the discharge-only rows also get retained). Use `how='left'` if you want to preserve the *left* table's keys.
> 4. **`fillna` applied to a DataFrame replaces `NaN` globally**; pass a **dict** to fill specific columns with different values (e.g., `'Pending'` for `Outcome`, `pd.NaT` for `DischargeDate`).

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and stick it on your wall** — this is your single-page KTU exam revision sheet for Module 2.

### ✅ Conceptual Mastery Checklist

- [ ] A **DataFrame** is a 2-D labeled table; each **column** is a `Series`; axes are `(0=rows, 1=cols)`.
- [ ] `df.shape` → `(rows, cols)`; `df.dtypes` → column-wise types; `df.values` → NumPy block.
- [ ] `loc` = **label**; `iloc` = **position**; `[]` with a single label returns a `Series`, with a list returns a `DataFrame`.
- [ ] Boolean masking uses **bitwise `&` and `~`**, not `and/or/not`. **Parenthesize** each condition.
- [ ] Vectorized arithmetic is **fast** (NumPy SIMD) — prefer it over `for` loops or `.apply()` for arithmetic.
- [ ] `groupby` follows the **Split → Apply → Combine** paradigm; chain it with `.agg()` for multi-stat outputs.
- [ ] `merge(how=...)` ∈ `{'inner','left','right','outer','cross'}`; default is `inner`.
- [ ] `concat` is for **stacking**; `merge` is for **joining on keys**; `join` is a convenience wrapper for `merge`.
- [ ] `pivot_table` requires an `aggfunc`; missing combinations become `NaN` unless `fill_value` is set.
- [ ] `dropna` / `fillna` are first-class citizens — **always** profile missing data with `df.isnull().sum()` first.
- [ ] `df['new_col'] = ...` adds a column; `df.insert(loc, name, val)` adds at a specific position.
- [ ] `df.drop('col', axis=1)` removes a column; `df.drop(idx, axis=0)` removes a row.

### ⚠️ Most Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in KTU ESE

1. **Forgetting `()` in compound boolean conditions** → `ValueError`.
2. **Using `and`/`or` instead of `&`/`|`** in `df[(cond1) & (cond2)]`.
3. **Confusing `loc` (label) with `iloc` (position)**.
4. **Forgetting to import pandas as `pd`** at the top.
5. **Using `for` loops for arithmetic** instead of vectorized expressions — penalised in CO3 (Apply).
6. **Not handling `NaN`** before computing `mean()` / `std()` — results in `NaN` propagation.
7. **In `groupby`, forgetting `.reset_index()`** when the result must be re-used as a DataFrame.

### 🔑 Formula Flashcards

| Formula | One-liner meaning |
|---|---|
| $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$ | Mean |
| $s^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2$ | Sample variance |
| $\rho = \frac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y}$ | Pearson correlation |
| $z = \frac{x-\bar{x}}{s}$ | Standardization (Z-score) |
| $\text{BMI} = \frac{W}{H^2}$ | Body Mass Index |
| $\text{BSA} = \sqrt{\frac{H_{cm}\cdot W_{kg}}{3600}}$ | Body Surface Area (Mosteller) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** In Part-B, always start with a **data-creation step**, then proceed in the **exact order** asked in the sub-parts. State the **expected output shape** after each operation — KTU examiners award **½ to 1 mark** for correctly anticipating the output dimension.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scikit-Learn: Pre-processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT862 - HEALTHCARE DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Scikit-Learn: Pre-processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Data Pre-processing** in the context of Scikit-Learn is the systematic application of well-defined statistical and algebraic transformations to raw feature matrices and label vectors so that downstream machine learning estimators (classifiers, regressors, clusterers) can learn optimal decision boundaries under the assumption of homogeneous, scaled, and clean input distributions.

In the Scikit-Learn API (sklearn.preprocessing module), pre-processing is implemented through a unified **Transformer** interface governed by three canonical methods:

1. `fit(X, y=None)` — Estimates the parameters (e.g., mean, variance, min, max) from the training data.
2. `transform(X)` — Applies the learned transformation to any dataset.
3. `fit_transform(X, y=None)` — Optimized shortcut that performs both steps in one call (used only on **training data** to prevent data leakage).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** A Scikit-Learn pre-processor is a stateful object that exposes the `fit/transform/fit_transform` API contract and must always be fitted **only on the training partition** to avoid *data leakage*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Lab Prep Room

Imagine a hospital diagnostic lab that receives blood samples from 5,000 patients. Each patient's report contains wildly different units — glucose in mg/dL for some, mmol/L for others; cholesterol ranging 50–400; blood pressure 60–200. A doctor cannot visually compare these numbers on a single chart because the **scales are incompatible**.

The lab technician (the **pre-processor**) normalizes every report into a common, comparable unit system (e.g., convert all to mmol/L, then rescale to a 0–100 health-index scale). Once all reports speak the same numerical "language," the diagnostic AI model (the **estimator**) can reliably detect patterns.

**Key insight from the analogy:** The technician must lock in the conversion rules (μ, σ, min, max) **only from the training samples** before applying them to new patients. Using statistics from the test patients to define the rules is a form of "cheating" called **data leakage** — and KTU examiners love testing this concept.

## 1.3 Why Pre-processing is Mandatory in Healthcare Analytics

Healthcare data has notorious problems that pre-processing directly addresses:

| Real-World Issue | Scikit-Learn Solution |
|---|---|
| Mixed units (mg/dL vs mmol/L) | `StandardScaler`, `MinMaxScaler` |
| Missing values (patient skipped a test) | `SimpleImputer`, `KNNImputer` |
| Categorical diagnoses (Yes/No, Stage I/II/III/IV) | `OneHotEncoder`, `OrdinalEncoder` |
| Outliers from measurement errors | `RobustScaler`, `QuantileTransformer` |
| Skewed distributions (e.g., length of hospital stay) | `PowerTransformer` (Yeo-Johnson, Box-Cox) |
| Non-linear feature interactions | `PolynomialFeatures` |

> [!NOTE]
> Scikit-Learn estimators expect a **2D NumPy array of shape `(n_samples, n_features)`** for features `X` and a **1D array of shape `(n_samples,)`** for labels `y`. Violating this shape contract raises a `ValueError` and is a frequent KTU viva question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Scikit-Learn Transformer Contract

Every pre-processor inherits from `TransformerMixin` and `BaseEstimator`. This enforces a strict **stateless-transform / stateful-fit** separation:

```
fit(X)  →  learn parameters θ (μ, σ, min, max, categories, etc.)
transform(X)  →  apply f(X; θ)
fit_transform(X)  →  fit + transform in one call
```

### 2.1.1 The Cardinal Rule of Fit/Transform Separation

The same fitted object used on training data **must** be reused (via `transform`, not `fit_transform`) on validation, test, and production data. This guarantees that the same scaling constants (μ, σ) learned from training are applied to new samples.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Calling `fit_transform` on the test set. This is **data leakage** and inflates accuracy by 5–30% on small healthcare datasets.

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundation of Each Scaler

### 2.2.1 StandardScaler (Z-Score Standardization)

Centers each feature to mean **0** and unit variance **1**.

$$z_i = \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$$

where $\mu = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ and $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2}$.

**When to use:** When features are approximately Gaussian and the estimator is sensitive to magnitude (Logistic Regression, SVM, PCA, K-Means, Neural Networks).

### 2.2.2 MinMaxScaler (Min-Max Normalization)

Rescales each feature to a bounded interval, default $[0, 1]$.

$$x'_i = \frac{x_i - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$$

For a custom range $[a, b]$:

$$x'_i = a + \frac{(x_i - x_{\min})(b - a)}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$$

**When to use:** Neural networks, image pixel intensities, when you need a fixed bounded range. **Highly sensitive to outliers** because $x_{\max}$ and $x_{\min}$ are pulled by extreme values.

### 2.2.3 RobustScaler (Median and IQR Based)

Uses statistics that are **robust to outliers**.

$$x'_i = \frac{x_i - \text{median}(x)}{\text{IQR}(x)}$$

where $\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$ (75th percentile − 25th percentile).

**When to use:** Healthcare datasets with measurement errors, extreme outliers (e.g., ICU billing amounts, rare disease costs).

### 2.2.4 MaxAbsScaler

Divides each feature by its maximum absolute value, preserving sparsity.

$$x'_i = \frac{x_i}{\max(\vert x_i \vert)}$$

Resulting range is $[-1, 1]$. **Does not center the data**, so it is safe for sparse matrices.

### 2.2.5 Normalizer (Sample-wise Normalization)

Scales **each row (sample)** independently to unit norm, not column-wise. Three norms are supported:

**L1 Norm:**

$$x'_i = \frac{x_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{m} \vert x_j \vert}$$

**L2 Norm (default):**

$$x'_i = \frac{x_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{m} x_j^2}}$$

**Max Norm:**

$$x'_i = \frac{x_i}{\max_{j}(\vert x_j \vert)}$$

**When to use:** Text classification (TF-IDF vectors), cosine-similarity-based estimators.

### 2.2.6 QuantileTransformer

Maps features to a **uniform** or **normal** distribution using their rank (quantile) information. Robust to outliers by design.

Default output distribution: `uniform` in $[0, 1]$, or `normal` $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$.

### 2.2.7 PowerTransformer

Applies a power transformation to make data more Gaussian. Supports **Yeo-Johnson** (handles zero and negative values) and **Box-Cox** (strictly positive values only).

## 2.3 Categorical Encoding

### 2.3.1 OrdinalEncoder vs OneHotEncoder

| Encoder | Input | Output Shape | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| `OrdinalEncoder` | 2D `(n, k)` categorical | 2D `(n, k)` integer codes | Tree-based models, ordinal categories (Stage I<II<III<IV) |
| `OneHotEncoder` | 2D `(n, k)` categorical | 2D `(n, k×unique)` binary | Linear models, nominal categories (Blood Group A/B/AB/O) |
| `LabelEncoder` | 1D `y` labels | 1D integer codes | **Target variable `y` only** |

**One-Hot encoding rule:**

$$x_{i,j}^{(k)} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if sample } i \text{ has category } k \text{ in feature } j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** `LabelEncoder` is **only for the target `y`**, never for features `X`. Using it on features imposes a false ordinal relationship (e.g., encoding "Malignant"=2 as greater than "Benign"=1), which linear models misinterpret.

### 2.3.2 Handling Unknown Categories

The `handle_unknown='ignore'` parameter in `OneHotEncoder` prevents crashes when a test-set category was unseen during training. By default (`'error'`), it raises an exception — this is the KTU-recommended safe default.

## 2.4 Handling Missing Values — Imputation

### 2.4.1 SimpleImputer Strategies

| Strategy | Formula | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| `'mean'` | $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$ | Numeric, no outliers |
| `'median'` | middle value | Numeric, with outliers |
| `'most_frequent'` | mode | Categorical |
| `'constant'` | fill_value | Domain knowledge (e.g., 0 means "not tested") |

### 2.4.2 KNNImputer

Imputes missing values using the **k-nearest neighbors** in feature space. For a sample with a missing value in feature $j$, it finds the $k$ nearest samples (by Euclidean distance using the other features) and averages their $j$-th feature values, weighted by distance.

$$\hat{x}_i^{(j)} = \frac{\sum_{l \in \text{NN}_k(i)} w_{il} \cdot x_l^{(j)}}{\sum_{l \in \text{NN}_k(i)} w_{il}}$$

with $w_{il} = 1/d(x_i, x_l)$.

### 2.4.3 IterativeImputer

Models each feature with missing values as a function of other features iteratively (multivariate regression). Produces the most statistically sound imputations but is computationally expensive.

## 2.5 PolynomialFeatures and Binarizer

### 2.5.1 PolynomialFeatures (degree = d)

Generates all polynomial combinations of features up to degree $d$. For input $[a, b]$ with $d=2$:

$$[1, a, b, a^2, ab, b^2]$$

General formula: generates all monomials $x_1^{e_1} x_2^{e_2} \cdots x_m^{e_m}$ where $e_1 + e_2 + \cdots + e_m \le d$.

### 2.5.2 Binarizer

Threshold-based binarization:

$$x'_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } x_i > \text{threshold} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Common `threshold = 0.0`. Useful for converting probability outputs to hard decisions.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — One-Page Cheat Sheet

| Formula Name | Equation | Where Used |
|---|---|---|
| Z-Score | $z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$ | `StandardScaler` |
| Min-Max | $x' = (x - x_{\min})/(x_{\max} - x_{\min})$ | `MinMaxScaler` |
| Robust | $x' = (x - \text{median})/\text{IQR}$ | `RobustScaler` |
| MaxAbs | $x' = x / \max(\vert x \vert)$ | `MaxAbsScaler` |
| L2 Norm | $x' = x / \sqrt{\sum x_j^2}$ | `Normalizer(norm='l2')` |
| L1 Norm | $x' = x / \sum \vert x_j \vert$ | `Normalizer(norm='l1')` |
| One-Hot | $x_{ij}^{(k)} \in \{0, 1\}$ | `OneHotEncoder` |
| Binarize | $x' = \mathbb{1}[x > \tau]$ | `Binarizer` |
| IQR | $Q_3 - Q_1$ | outlier detection |
| Sample mean | $\mu = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$ | imputation/scaling |
| Sample std (pop.) | $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum(x_i - \mu)^2}$ | Scikit-Learn default |

> [!NOTE]
> Scikit-Learn's `StandardScaler` uses **population standard deviation** (denominator $n$, not $n-1$) by default, controlled by `with_std=True`. Setting `ddof=1` in NumPy gives the sample std. This distinction is a common viva question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — MinMaxScaler on a Healthcare Feature

**Problem:** A healthcare dataset has a column `cholesterol_mg_dL` with values `[120, 180, 200, 240, 360]`. Apply `MinMaxScaler(feature_range=(0, 1))` to produce the transformed array. Then rescale to range $(−1, +1)$ for a neural network.

### Step 1: Identify the Extrema

$$x_{\min} = 120, \quad x_{\max} = 360$$

### Step 2: Apply the Min-Max Formula

For $x_i = 120$:

$$x'_1 = \frac{120 - 120}{360 - 120} = \frac{0}{240} = 0.0$$

For $x_i = 180$:

$$x'_2 = \frac{180 - 120}{360 - 120} = \frac{60}{240} = 0.25$$

For $x_i = 200$:

$$x'_3 = \frac{200 - 120}{360 - 120} = \frac{80}{240} \approx 0.3333$$

For $x_i = 240$:

$$x'_4 = \frac{240 - 120}{360 - 120} = \frac{120}{240} = 0.5$$

For $x_i = 360$:

$$x'_5 = \frac{360 - 120}{360 - 120} = \frac{240}{240} = 1.0$$

Resulting array: `[0.0, 0.25, 0.3333, 0.5, 1.0]`.

### Step 3: Rescale to $(−1, +1)$ Using the General Formula

With $a = -1$, $b = 1$:

$$x''_i = -1 + \frac{(x_i - 120)(1 - (-1))}{360 - 120} = -1 + \frac{2(x_i - 120)}{240}$$

For $x_i = 180$:

$$x''_2 = -1 + \frac{2(60)}{240} = -1 + 0.5 = -0.5$$

For $x_i = 360$:

$$x''_5 = -1 + \frac{2(240)}{240} = -1 + 2.0 = 1.0$$

Rescaled array: `[-1.0, -0.5, -0.3333, 0.0, 1.0]`.

## 3.2 Worked Example — StandardScaler Manual Derivation

**Problem:** Compute the Z-score for `glucose_mg_dL = [85, 90, 95, 100, 200]` (note: 200 is a likely outlier).

### Step 1: Compute the Mean

$$\mu = \frac{85 + 90 + 95 + 100 + 200}{5} = \frac{570}{5} = 114$$

### Step 2: Compute the Population Standard Deviation

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5}(x_i - \mu)^2}$$

Compute each squared deviation:

$$(85 - 114)^2 = (-29)^2 = 841$$
$$(90 - 114)^2 = (-24)^2 = 576$$
$$(95 - 114)^2 = (-19)^2 = 361$$
$$(100 - 114)^2 = (-14)^2 = 196$$
$$(200 - 114)^2 = (86)^2 = 7396$$

Sum: $841 + 576 + 361 + 196 + 7396 = 9370$.

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{9370}{5}} = \sqrt{1874} \approx 43.29$$

### Step 3: Compute the Z-Scores

$$z_1 = \frac{85 - 114}{43.29} = \frac{-29}{43.29} \approx -0.670$$
$$z_2 = \frac{90 - 114}{43.29} \approx -0.554$$
$$z_3 = \frac{95 - 114}{43.29} \approx -0.439$$
$$z_4 = \frac{100 - 114}{43.29} \approx -0.323$$
$$z_5 = \frac{200 - 114}{43.29} \approx 1.986$$

Resulting array: `[-0.670, -0.554, -0.439, -0.323, 1.986]`. **Observation:** The outlier 200 is mapped to $z \approx 1.99$, showing how `StandardScaler` is **sensitive to outliers** because $\mu$ and $\sigma$ are pulled by extreme values. Compare with `RobustScaler`:

$$\text{median} = 95, \quad Q_1 = 90, \quad Q_3 = 100, \quad \text{IQR} = 10$$
$$x'_5 = \frac{200 - 95}{10} = 10.5$$

The outlier is exposed more cleanly, demonstrating why `RobustScaler` is preferred for healthcare data.

## 3.3 Worked Example — OneHotEncoder on Diagnosis Categories

**Problem:** Encode the diagnosis column `["Malignant", "Benign", "Malignant", "Benign"]` using `OneHotEncoder(sparse_output=False)`.

### Step 1: Identify Unique Categories (Fit Stage)

Unique categories (alphabetical by default in Scikit-Learn): `["Benign", "Malignant"]`.

### Step 2: Build the Binary Matrix (Transform Stage)

Each unique category becomes a column. For each sample, a 1 is placed in the matching column.

$$X_{\text{encoded}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

where column 0 = "Benign", column 1 = "Malignant".

### Step 3: Drop First Category (Optional `drop='first'`)

To avoid the **dummy variable trap** (multicollinearity) in linear regression, drop one column:

$$X_{\text{encoded, dropped}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

where 1 = Malignant, 0 = Benign. This is the **binary encoding** of a binary target.

## 3.4 Worked Example — SimpleImputer Mean Strategy

**Problem:** Given `blood_pressure = [120, NaN, 130, 140, 125]`, impute the missing value using mean strategy.

### Step 1: Drop NaN and Compute Mean

Non-missing values: `[120, 130, 140, 125]`, count $n = 4$.

$$\mu = \frac{120 + 130 + 140 + 125}{4} = \frac{515}{4} = 128.75$$

### Step 2: Fill the NaN

$$X_{\text{imputed}} = [120, 128.75, 130, 140, 125]$$

## 3.5 Pipeline Integration — Complete Pre-processing Chain

In practice, Scikit-Learn pre-processors are chained using `Pipeline` to prevent leakage and enable end-to-end fitting:

```python
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

pipe = Pipeline([
    ('imputer', SimpleImputer(strategy='median')),
    ('scaler', StandardScaler()),
    ('clf', LogisticRegression())
])

pipe.fit(X_train, y_train)   # Each step is fit sequentially on transformed output of previous
pipe.predict(X_test)         # Each step transforms using its TRAINING-learned parameters
```

**Critical KTU point:** Inside `Pipeline.fit`, when `imputer.transform` is called on `X_train`, it uses the mean/median learned **from X_train only**. The same constants are then reused when `predict(X_test)` triggers the chain — this is the leak-proof way.

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Scaler to Choose?

| Scenario | Recommended Scaler | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Linear Regression on glucose, BP, age | `StandardScaler` | Centers features, equal weighting |
| Neural Network on pixel data | `MinMaxScaler` | Bounded [0,1] matches sigmoid/relu inputs |
| ICU cost data with extreme outliers | `RobustScaler` | Median/IQR are outlier-resistant |
| Sparse text TF-IDF matrix | `MaxAbsScaler` or `Normalizer` | Preserves sparsity |
| Skewed gene expression data | `PowerTransformer` or `QuantileTransformer` | Gaussianizes the distribution |
| Cosine-similarity text classification | `Normalizer(norm='l2')` | Row-wise L2 normalization |
| Tree-based models (Random Forest, XGBoost) | **None (no scaling needed)** | Trees are scale-invariant |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Scikit-Learn Transformer API Lifecycle

This diagram maps the complete state-machine of a pre-processing object across the train/validation/test split, the most critical concept for KTU questions on data leakage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset X y] --> B[Train Test Split]
    B --> C[X train y train]
    B --> D[X test y test]

    C --> E[Preprocessor fit]
    E --> F[Learned parameters theta]
    F --> G[transform X train]
    G --> H[X train scaled]
    H --> I[Estimator fit]
    I --> J[Trained Model]

    D --> K[transform X test using same theta]
    F --> K
    K --> L[X test scaled]
    L --> M[Model predict X test]
    J --> M
    M --> N[Predictions y hat]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#0e7490,stroke:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#365314,stroke:#365314,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#581c87,stroke:#581c87,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#365314,stroke:#365314,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#9a3412,stroke:#9a3412,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#7e22ce,stroke:#7e22ce,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#365314,stroke:#365314,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#9a3412,stroke:#9a3412,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#166534,stroke:#166534,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Taxonomy of Scikit-Learn Pre-processors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[sklearn preprocessing] --> S[Scalers]
    P --> E[Encoders]
    P --> I[Imputers]
    P --> M[Matrix Transformers]
    P --> F[Functional Transformers]

    S --> S1[StandardScaler]
    S --> S2[MinMaxScaler]
    S --> S3[RobustScaler]
    S --> S4[MaxAbsScaler]
    S --> S5[Normalizer]
    S --> S6[QuantileTransformer]
    S --> S7[PowerTransformer]

    E --> E1[OneHotEncoder]
    E --> E2[OrdinalEncoder]
    E --> E3[LabelEncoder]
    E --> E4[TargetEncoder]

    I --> I1[SimpleImputer]
    I --> I2[KNNImputer]
    I --> I3[IterativeImputer]

    M --> M1[PolynomialFeatures]
    M --> M2[Binarizer]
    M --> M3[KBinsDiscretizer]

    F --> F1[FunctionTransformer]
    F --> F2[KernelCenterer]

    style P fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style S fill:#0e7490,stroke:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#365314,stroke:#365314,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#581c87,stroke:#581c87,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#9a3412,stroke:#9a3412,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Data Leakage vs Safe Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph WRONG[WRONG APPROACH DATA LEAKAGE]
        W1[X train] --> W2[fit transform Scaler]
        W3[X test] --> W4[fit transform Scaler]
        W2 --> W5[Model train on scaled X train]
        W4 --> W6[Model predict scaled X test]
        W5 --> W7[Inflated Accuracy]
        W6 --> W7
    end

    subgraph RIGHT[CORRECT APPROACH PIPELINE]
        R1[X train] --> R2[fit Scaler]
        R2 --> R3[transform X train]
        R3 --> R4[Model fit]
        R5[X test] --> R6[transform X test using train stats]
        R4 --> R7[Model predict]
        R6 --> R7
        R7 --> R8[Honest Accuracy]
    end

    style W7 fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    style R8 fill:#166534,stroke:#166534,color:#ffffff
    style WRONG fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000000
    style RIGHT fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000000
```

## 4.4 End-to-End Healthcare Pipeline (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw EHR Data] --> B[SimpleImputer median]
    B --> C[Outlier Cap Winsorize]
    C --> D[OneHotEncoder categories]
    D --> E[StandardScaler numeric]
    E --> F[PCA dim reduction]
    F --> G[Classifier SVM or RandomForest]
    G --> H[Prediction Stage Output]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#0e7490,stroke:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#365314,stroke:#365314,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#581c87,stroke:#581c87,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#9a3412,stroke:#9a3412,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#7e22ce,stroke:#7e22ce,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#166534,stroke:#166534,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.5 Fit/Transform Sequence Diagram (Code-to-Concept Mapping)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DS as Dataset X
    participant TR as Transformer
    participant ES as Estimator
    participant TS as Test Data

    DS->>TR: fit X train
    TR->>TR: learn mu sigma min max
    TR-->>DS: return self
    DS->>TR: transform X train
    TR-->>ES: X train scaled
    ES->>ES: fit X train scaled y train
    ES-->>ES: model trained
    TS->>TR: transform X test
    TR-->>ES: X test scaled
    ES-->>ES: predict X test scaled
    ES-->>ES: y hat predictions
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the difference between `StandardScaler` and `MinMaxScaler` in Scikit-Learn. State one scenario where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **`StandardScaler`** transforms each feature to have mean **0** and standard deviation **1** using the formula $z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$. It does not bound the output to a fixed range, so outliers are not compressed. It is preferred for **distance-based algorithms** such as K-Nearest Neighbors, SVM, PCA, and Logistic Regression, which assume features are centered around zero. (1.5 marks)
- **`MinMaxScaler`** rescales each feature to a bounded interval, default $[0, 1]$, using $x' = (x - x_{\min})/(x_{\max} - x_{\min})$. It is preferred for **neural networks and image data** where bounded inputs (0 to 1) are required for activation functions like sigmoid and ReLU. However, it is highly sensitive to outliers because $x_{\max}$ and $x_{\min}$ are pulled by extreme values. (1.5 marks)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Why must the `fit` method of a pre-processor be called only on training data and not on the full dataset? What is this anti-pattern called?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Calling `fit` on the full dataset (including test data) causes the pre-processor to learn statistics — mean, standard deviation, min, max, category levels — from information the model is not supposed to see during evaluation. When the same statistics are then used to transform the test set, the model has effectively "peeked" at the test distribution, producing **over-optimistic evaluation metrics** that do not generalize to truly unseen data. (2 marks)

This anti-pattern is called **data leakage** (specifically, *train-test contamination*). It is one of the most common pitfalls in machine learning workflows and is a frequent KTU viva question. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With suitable equations, explain the working of `StandardScaler` and `RobustScaler` in Scikit-Learn. Compare their robustness to outliers using a numerical example with the dataset $[1, 2, 3, 4, 100]$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the purpose of `OneHotEncoder` and `LabelEncoder` in Scikit-Learn. Why is `LabelEncoder` strictly not recommended for encoding feature matrix `X`? Illustrate with a healthcare example involving the column "Tumor Type" with values `{Benign, Malignant, Pre-malignant}`.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**StandardScaler** applies the Z-score formula:

$$z_i = \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$$

For the dataset $X = [1, 2, 3, 4, 100]$, $n = 5$:

$$\mu = \frac{1+2+3+4+100}{5} = \frac{110}{5} = 22$$

Squared deviations: $(1-22)^2 = 441$, $(2-22)^2 = 400$, $(3-22)^2 = 361$, $(4-22)^2 = 324$, $(100-22)^2 = 6084$. Sum $= 7610$.

$$\sigma = \sqrt{7610/5} = \sqrt{1522} \approx 39.01$$

Z-scores: $-0.538, -0.513, -0.487, -0.462, +2.000$. **The outlier 100 dominates the variance**, compressing all non-outlier points into a narrow range $[-0.54, -0.46]$.

**RobustScaler** uses:

$$x'_i = \frac{x_i - \text{median}(x)}{\text{IQR}(x)}$$

For the same data: median $= 3$, $Q_1 = 2$, $Q_3 = 4$, $\text{IQR} = 2$.

Transformed values: $(1-3)/2 = -1.0$, $(2-3)/2 = -0.5$, $(3-3)/2 = 0$, $(4-3)/2 = 0.5$, $(100-3)/2 = 48.5$. **The non-outlier points are well-spread** in $[-1, 0.5]$, while the outlier is exposed as a large value (48.5).

**Conclusion:** `RobustScaler` is preferred for healthcare data with measurement errors and outliers. `StandardScaler` is preferred when data is approximately Gaussian with no significant outliers. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (b):

**`OneHotEncoder`** converts each categorical value into a binary vector of length equal to the number of unique categories, with a single 1 indicating the active category.

**`LabelEncoder`** converts each unique category into a single integer code (e.g., Benign=0, Malignant=1, Pre-malignant=2). It outputs a **1D array of integer labels** and is intended **only for the target variable `y`**, not for the feature matrix `X`.

**Why `LabelEncoder` is not recommended for `X`:** The integer codes impose a **false ordinal relationship** that linear and distance-based models misinterpret. For the column "Tumor Type" with values `{Benign, Malignant, Pre-malignant}`, `LabelEncoder` might assign Benign=0, Malignant=1, Pre-malignant=2. A linear model would then conclude that "Malignant" (1) is **numerically closer** to "Pre-malignant" (2) than to "Benign" (0), and that "Malignant" is **greater than** "Benign" in a meaningful sense. This is medically incorrect — these are nominal categories with no inherent ordering.

The correct approach is to use `OneHotEncoder`, producing a binary matrix with three columns (one per category), ensuring no artificial ordering is imposed. (7 marks)

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the Scikit-Learn `Pipeline` class and explain how it prevents data leakage during cross-validation. Show the syntax for combining `SimpleImputer`, `StandardScaler`, and `LogisticRegression` into a single pipeline.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of `PolynomialFeatures` with degree=2 for the input vector $[a, b]$. Also explain the `Binarizer` transformer with `threshold=0.5` for the probability vector $[0.1, 0.4, 0.7, 0.9]$.

#### Model Solution to (a):

The `Pipeline` class in Scikit-Learn chains multiple transformers and a final estimator into a single composite object. It sequentially applies `fit` and `transform` through each intermediate step, and only the final estimator is fit on the fully transformed data. The `fit_transform` method of each intermediate step is invoked **only on the training fold** during cross-validation, ensuring that leakage-prevention statistics (mean, median, etc.) are recomputed per fold from training data alone. (3 marks)

**Syntax:**

```python
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

pipe = Pipeline(steps=[
    ('imputer', SimpleImputer(strategy='median')),
    ('scaler', StandardScaler()),
    ('clf', LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000))
])
```

The pipeline is fit on training data and used to predict on test/production data. When used inside `cross_val_score` or `GridSearchCV`, the entire pipeline is re-fit on each training fold, preventing any test-fold statistics from leaking into the model. (4 marks)

#### Model Solution to (b):

**`PolynomialFeatures(degree=2)`** for input vector $[a, b]$ generates all polynomial combinations up to degree 2:

$$[1, a, b, a^2, ab, b^2]$$

The constant term 1 is included by default (`include_bias=True`). For a dataset of $m$ features, the output has $\binom{m+d}{d}$ columns. This transformer is useful for **linearizing non-linear relationships** — a linear model fit on polynomial features can learn non-linear decision boundaries. (3.5 marks)

**`Binarizer(threshold=0.5)`** applies a hard threshold:

$$x'_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } x_i > 0.5 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

For the probability vector $[0.1, 0.4, 0.7, 0.9]$:

$$[0.1 \to 0, \quad 0.4 \to 0, \quad 0.7 \to 1, \quad 0.9 \to 1]$$

Result: $[0, 0, 1, 1]$. This is commonly used to convert soft probability outputs into hard class predictions. (3.5 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `fit_transform` with `transform`:** Many students call `fit_transform` on test data, causing data leakage. Always use `pipe.fit(X_train)` then `pipe.predict(X_test)`.
> 2. **Mixing up `LabelEncoder` and `OrdinalEncoder`:** `LabelEncoder` is for 1D target `y`; `OrdinalEncoder` is for 2D feature `X`. Using `LabelEncoder` on `X` is a 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting `sparse_output=False`:** In recent Scikit-Learn versions, `OneHotEncoder` returns a sparse matrix by default. Students expecting a dense NumPy array crash. Use `sparse_output=False`.
> 4. **Applying `StandardScaler` before train-test split:** The scaler must be fit **only on training data**. Scaling before splitting is data leakage.
> 5. **Ignoring `handle_unknown`:** When new categorical values appear in test data, `OneHotEncoder` raises an error by default. Use `handle_unknown='ignore'` for production robustness.
> 6. **Standardization on categorical integer codes:** Applying `StandardScaler` to integer-encoded categories is meaningless and distorts the categorical structure.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The Transformer API contract** is `fit → transform → fit_transform`. This contract is the foundation of all Scikit-Learn pre-processors.
- **`fit` must always be called on training data only.** Calling `fit` on the full dataset causes data leakage and inflated accuracy.
- **`StandardScaler`** uses Z-score: $z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$. Sensitive to outliers. Use when data is approximately Gaussian.
- **`MinMaxScaler`** uses $(x - x_{\min})/(x_{\max} - x_{\min})$. Bounded output, highly sensitive to outliers. Use for neural networks.
- **`RobustScaler`** uses $(x - \text{median})/\text{IQR}$. Outlier-resistant. Preferred for healthcare data with measurement errors.
- **`MaxAbsScaler`** uses $x / \max(\vert x \vert)$. Preserves sparsity. Use for sparse matrices.
- **`Normalizer`** scales row-wise (sample-wise) to unit norm. Use for text classification and cosine similarity.
- **`OneHotEncoder`** produces a binary matrix for nominal categories. **`OrdinalEncoder`** produces integer codes for ordinal categories. **`LabelEncoder`** is for 1D target `y` only.
- **`SimpleImputer`** strategies: `'mean'`, `'median'`, `'most_frequent'`, `'constant'`. Use `'median'` for healthcare with outliers.
- **`KNNImputer`** uses k-nearest-neighbor-based multivariate imputation — more accurate than mean/median but computationally expensive.
- **`PolynomialFeatures`** generates all monomials up to degree $d$. Useful for fitting non-linear patterns with linear models.
- **`Binarizer`** applies a hard threshold. Used to convert probabilities to hard predictions.
- **`Pipeline`** chains transformers + final estimator. The single most important tool for leak-proof ML workflows.
- **Tree-based models** (Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM) do **not require feature scaling** — this is a frequent viva question.
- **Sparse matrices** must be handled with `MaxAbsScaler` or `Normalizer`; do not use `StandardScaler` or `MinMaxScaler` (they densify the matrix).
- **Scikit-Learn uses population standard deviation** (denominator $n$) in `StandardScaler` by default — different from NumPy's default `ddof=0` matches but differs from pandas's `ddof=1`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feature Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT862 - HEALTHCARE DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Feature Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feature Selection (FS)** is the process of identifying and selecting a subset of relevant features (variables, predictors, or attributes) from a larger set of available features in a dataset, such that the selected subset optimally represents the underlying structure of the data while discarding irrelevant, redundant, or noisy features.

In the context of **Healthcare Data Analytics**, feature selection involves choosing the most clinically meaningful attributes (e.g., patient demographics, lab values, vital signs, genomic markers) that contribute most to predicting health outcomes such as disease diagnosis, prognosis, hospital readmission, or treatment response.

Mathematically, given a dataset with $n$ features $F = \{f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_n\}$ and a target variable $Y$, feature selection aims to find an optimal subset $F_{opt} \subseteq F$ such that:

$$F_{opt} = \arg\min_{S \subseteq F} \text{Loss}(S, Y) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \vert S \vert = k, \quad k \leq n$$

where $\text{Loss}(S, Y)$ is the predictive loss function, and $k$ is the number of selected features.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Doctor's Diagnostic Toolkit"**
> Imagine a doctor trying to diagnose heart disease. There are potentially **hundreds** of possible tests: blood sugar, cholesterol, blood pressure, ECG, chest X-ray, genetic markers, family history, BMI, smoking status, stress levels, sleep patterns, and so on. Running ALL tests on every patient is **expensive, time-consuming, and sometimes risky**. The doctor's brain instinctively performs **feature selection** — choosing the 5–10 most informative tests that give the highest diagnostic accuracy.
> - **Irrelevant features** (e.g., patient's favorite color) add no diagnostic value.
> - **Redundant features** (e.g., measuring blood pressure in 3 different ways) don't add new information.
> - **Relevant features** (e.g., cholesterol, ECG, blood pressure) are kept.
> This is exactly what feature selection algorithms do — they automate this "clinical intuition" statistically.

### 1.3 Why Feature Selection is Critical in Healthcare

| Reason | Clinical Impact |
|---|---|
| **Curse of Dimensionality** | High-dimensional data (e.g., genomics) causes overfitting |
| **Cost Reduction** | Fewer medical tests = lower patient cost |
| **Interpretability** | Doctors need explainable models for clinical trust |
| **Computational Efficiency** | Smaller models train faster (critical in real-time monitoring) |
| **Noise Reduction** | Removes irrelevant features that degrade accuracy |
| **Multicollinearity** | Eliminates correlated features (e.g., height and weight) |

### 1.4 Feature Selection vs. Feature Extraction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feature Selection** keeps the **original features** unchanged. **Feature Extraction** (e.g., PCA) **transforms** original features into new ones. In healthcare, feature selection is preferred because clinicians need to interpret results in terms of original medical measurements (e.g., "Glucose > 126 mg/dL indicates diabetes").
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Feature Selection Methods

Feature selection methods are broadly classified into three categories based on how they interact with the learning algorithm:

#### **Pillar 1: Filter Methods** (Model-Agnostic, Fast)

Filter methods evaluate features **independently of any learning algorithm** using statistical measures. They rank features by intrinsic properties such as correlation, information content, or statistical significance.

**Advantages:**
- Computationally efficient — $O(n)$ complexity
- No risk of overfitting
- Works with any downstream model

**Disadvantages:**
- Ignores feature interactions
- May select redundant features

##### Key Filter Techniques

**(a) Chi-Square Test ($\chi^2$) — For Categorical Features**

The chi-square statistic measures the **dependence between a categorical feature and the target class**:

$$\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{r} \sum_{j=1}^{c} \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$$

where:
- $O_{ij}$ = observed frequency in cell $(i,j)$
- $E_{ij}$ = expected frequency under the null hypothesis
- $r$ = number of rows, $c$ = number of columns

Degrees of freedom: $df = (r-1)(c-1)$

**Healthcare Example:** Testing whether "smoking status" (Yes/No) is independent of "lung cancer" (Yes/No).

> **Null Hypothesis ($H_0$):** Feature $X$ is independent of target $Y$.
> **Alternate Hypothesis ($H_1$):** Feature $X$ is dependent on target $Y$.
> Higher $\chi^2$ value → Stronger evidence to reject $H_0$ → More important feature.

**(b) Information Gain (IG) — Entropy-Based**

Information Gain is based on **Shannon's Entropy**, which measures the impurity/disorder in a dataset.

$$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2(p_i)$$

where $p_i$ is the proportion of samples belonging to class $i$.

The **Information Gain** for feature $X$ is:

$$IG(X, S) = H(S) - H(S \mid X)$$

where the conditional entropy is:

$$H(S \mid X) = \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(X)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \cdot H(S_v)$$

**(c) Gain Ratio — Normalized Information Gain**

$$GR(X) = \frac{IG(X)}{H_X(S)} = \frac{IG(X)}{-\sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}}$$

This corrects IG's bias toward features with many values.

**(d) Pearson Correlation Coefficient**

For continuous features:

$$r = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$$

Values close to $+1$ or $-1$ indicate strong correlation.

**(e) ANOVA F-Statistic (f_classif)**

For one feature vs. multi-class target:

$$F = \frac{\text{Between-group variance}}{\text{Within-group variance}} = \frac{MS_B}{MS_W}$$

**(f) Mutual Information**

$$I(X; Y) = \sum_{x \in X} \sum_{y \in Y} p(x, y) \log \frac{p(x, y)}{p(x) p(y)}$$

Captures both linear and non-linear dependencies.

---

#### **Pillar 2: Wrapper Methods** (Model-Dependent, Accurate)

Wrapper methods use a **specific learning algorithm as a black box** to score feature subsets. They search the space of feature subsets and use model performance as the evaluation metric.

**Advantages:**
- Captures feature interactions
- Higher accuracy than filters

**Disadvantages:**
- Computationally expensive — $O(2^n)$ in worst case
- Risk of overfitting
- Tied to one specific model

##### Key Wrapper Techniques

**(a) Forward Selection (Sequential Forward Selection — SFS)**

Greedy algorithm that **starts with zero features** and **adds one feature at a time** — the one that most improves model performance.

```
Algorithm: Forward Selection
1. Start with F_selected = {}
2. Repeat until k features selected:
   a. For each unselected feature f:
      - Train model with F_selected ∪ {f}
      - Evaluate cross-validation score
   b. Add the feature f* that gives best score
   c. F_selected = F_selected ∪ {f*}
3. Return F_selected
```

**Complexity:** $O(k \cdot n \cdot \text{cost(model training)})$

**(b) Backward Elimination (Sequential Backward Selection — SBS)**

The opposite of forward selection — **starts with all features** and **removes the least important feature** at each step.

**(c) Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE)**

```
Algorithm: RFE
1. Start with F_selected = F (all features)
2. Train the model on F_selected
3. Rank features by importance (e.g., coefficients or feature importances)
4. Remove the bottom-ranked feature
5. Repeat steps 2–4 until desired number of features k is reached
6. Return F_selected
```

**Healthcare Use Case:** RFE with Logistic Regression for predicting diabetes — removing features one-by-one to find the optimal subset.

**(d) Exhaustive Search (Brute Force)**

Tries **all possible $\binom{n}{k}$ subsets** — guaranteed optimal but computationally infeasible for $n > 20$.

**(e) Genetic Algorithm (GA) for Feature Selection**

Uses evolutionary biology concepts:
- Each feature subset = a "chromosome" (binary string of length $n$)
- Fitness function = model accuracy
- Selection, Crossover, Mutation operators evolve better subsets

---

#### **Pillar 3: Embedded Methods** (Integrated into Model Training)

Embedded methods perform feature selection **as part of the model training process itself**. The model learns which features to keep.

**Advantages:**
- Best of both worlds — fast AND captures interactions
- Less prone to overfitting than wrappers

##### Key Embedded Techniques

**(a) L1 Regularization (Lasso — Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator)**

The Lasso objective function adds an L1 penalty to the loss:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{Lasso}}(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \beta_j \vert$$

where $\lambda$ is the regularization parameter and $\beta_j$ is the coefficient of feature $j$.

**Key Property:** Lasso drives some $\beta_j$ to **exactly zero**, effectively performing feature selection.

**Comparison:**
- **L1 (Lasso):** $\sum \vert \beta_j \vert$ → Sparse solutions, feature selection
- **L2 (Ridge):** $\sum \beta_j^2$ → Shrinks coefficients but doesn't zero them
- **Elastic Net:** $\alpha \sum \vert \beta_j \vert + (1-\alpha) \sum \beta_j^2$ → Combined penalty

**(b) Tree-Based Feature Importance**

Decision trees and ensemble methods (Random Forest, XGBoost) compute feature importance based on:
- **Gini Importance / Mean Decrease Impurity (MDI):** Total reduction in node impurity
- **Permutation Importance:** Decrease in model score when a feature's values are randomly shuffled

$$\text{Importance}(X_j) = \sum_{t \in \text{Trees}} \Delta_{\text{impurity}}(t, j)$$

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Method | Key Formula | Use Case | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Entropy** | $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Measure impurity | $O(n)$ |
| 2 | **Information Gain** | $IG(X) = H(S) - H(S\mid X)$ | Feature ranking | $O(n \cdot v)$ |
| 3 | **Gain Ratio** | $GR = IG(X) / H_X(S)$ | Normalized IG | $O(n \cdot v)$ |
| 4 | **Chi-Square** | $\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$ | Categorical features | $O(n \cdot c)$ |
| 5 | **Pearson Correlation** | $r = \frac{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2 \sum(y_i-\bar{y})^2}}$ | Continuous features | $O(n)$ |
| 6 | **ANOVA F-Statistic** | $F = MS_B / MS_W$ | Multi-class targets | $O(n \cdot c)$ |
| 7 | **Mutual Information** | $I(X;Y) = \sum\sum p(x,y)\log\frac{p(x,y)}{p(x)p(y)}$ | Non-linear dependency | $O(n \cdot k)$ |
| 8 | **Lasso Objective** | $\mathcal{L} = \sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + \lambda\sum\vert\beta_j\vert$ | Embedded FS | $O(n \cdot p)$ |
| 9 | **Ridge Objective** | $\mathcal{L} = \sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + \lambda\sum\beta_j^2$ | Coefficient shrinkage | $O(n \cdot p)$ |
| 10 | **Gini Impurity** | $G = 1 - \sum p_i^2$ | Tree-based importance | $O(n \cdot d)$ |
| 11 | **Forward Selection** | Greedy add | Wrapper | $O(k \cdot n \cdot C_{model})$ |
| 12 | **Backward Elimination** | Greedy remove | Wrapper | $O((n-k) \cdot n \cdot C_{model})$ |

> **Notation:** $n$ = samples, $p$ = features, $v$ = values per feature, $c$ = classes, $k$ = bins for MI, $d$ = tree depth, $C_{model}$ = cost of training the model.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Healthcare

1. **Genomic Data (e.g., Gene Expression):** Select the 50 most relevant genes out of 20,000+ to predict cancer subtypes using ANOVA + RFE.
2. **EHR (Electronic Health Records):** Choose relevant clinical features (e.g., age, BMI, HbA1c) for diabetes prediction using Information Gain.
3. **Medical Imaging:** Select discriminative features from extracted radiomics features using Lasso regression.
4. **ICU Monitoring:** Choose the most predictive vital signs (HR, BP, SpO2) for sepsis prediction using Mutual Information.
5. **Drug Discovery:** Filter irrelevant molecular descriptors using Chi-Square + L1 regularization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Chi-Square Test for Feature Selection

> **Problem Context:** A hospital has collected data on **200 patients** to determine if **"Smoking Status"** is a significant predictor of **"Lung Cancer"**. Construct the contingency table and compute the chi-square statistic.

#### Step 1: Build the Contingency Table

|  | Lung Cancer (Yes) | Lung Cancer (No) | **Row Total** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Smoker** | $O_{11} = 50$ | $O_{12} = 30$ | $R_1 = 80$ |
| **Non-Smoker** | $O_{21} = 20$ | $O_{22} = 100$ | $R_2 = 120$ |
| **Column Total** | $C_1 = 70$ | $C_2 = 130$ | $N = 200$ |

#### Step 2: Compute Expected Frequencies

The expected frequency for each cell is:

$$E_{ij} = \frac{R_i \cdot C_j}{N}$$

**Step 2a:** For cell $(1,1)$ — Smoker with Cancer:

$$E_{11} = \frac{R_1 \cdot C_1}{N} = \frac{80 \times 70}{200} = \frac{5600}{200} = 28$$

**Step 2b:** For cell $(1,2)$ — Smoker without Cancer:

$$E_{12} = \frac{R_1 \cdot C_2}{N} = \frac{80 \times 130}{200} = \frac{10400}{200} = 52$$

**Step 2c:** For cell $(2,1)$ — Non-Smoker with Cancer:

$$E_{21} = \frac{R_2 \cdot C_1}{N} = \frac{120 \times 70}{200} = \frac{8400}{200} = 42$$

**Step 2d:** For cell $(2,2)$ — Non-Smoker without Cancer:

$$E_{22} = \frac{R_2 \cdot C_2}{N} = \frac{120 \times 130}{200} = \frac{15600}{200} = 78$$

**Verification:** $\sum E_{ij} = 28 + 52 + 42 + 78 = 200 = N$ ✓

#### Step 3: Compute Chi-Square Statistic

$$\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{2} \sum_{j=1}^{2} \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$$

**Term 1:** Cell $(1,1)$:

$$\frac{(50 - 28)^2}{28} = \frac{484}{28} = 17.286$$

**Term 2:** Cell $(1,2)$:

$$\frac{(30 - 52)^2}{52} = \frac{484}{52} = 9.308$$

**Term 3:** Cell $(2,1)$:

$$\frac{(20 - 42)^2}{42} = \frac{484}{42} = 11.524$$

**Term 4:** Cell $(2,2)$:

$$\frac{(100 - 78)^2}{78} = \frac{484}{78} = 6.205$$

**Sum:**

$$\chi^2 = 17.286 + 9.308 + 11.524 + 6.205 = 44.323$$

#### Step 4: Determine Degrees of Freedom and Critical Value

$$df = (r - 1)(c - 1) = (2-1)(2-1) = 1$$

At significance level $\alpha = 0.05$ with $df = 1$, the **critical value** from the chi-square table is $\chi^2_{0.05, 1} = 3.841$.

#### Step 5: Decision

Since $\chi^2_{\text{calculated}} = 44.323 > \chi^2_{\text{critical}} = 3.841$:

> **Conclusion:** We **reject the null hypothesis**. Smoking status is **highly significantly associated** with lung cancer. Therefore, "Smoking Status" is an important feature and should be **selected** for the prediction model.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Information Gain Computation

> **Problem Context:** A hospital wants to predict whether a patient has **"Heart Disease"** (Yes/No) based on the feature **"Chest Pain Type"** with values: {Typical Angina, Atypical Angina, Non-anginal, Asymptomatic}. The parent dataset has **100 patients**: 60 with heart disease, 40 without.

#### Step 1: Compute Parent Entropy

$$H(\text{Parent}) = -\left(\frac{60}{100} \log_2 \frac{60}{100} + \frac{40}{100} \log_2 \frac{40}{100}\right)$$

$$= -(0.6 \times \log_2 0.6 + 0.4 \times \log_2 0.4)$$

$$= -(0.6 \times (-0.737) + 0.4 \times (-1.322))$$

$$= -(-0.442 - 0.529) = 0.971 \text{ bits}$$

#### Step 2: Split Data by "Chest Pain Type"

| Chest Pain Type | Heart Disease (Yes) | Heart Disease (No) | Subset Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| Typical Angina | 35 | 5 | 40 |
| Atypical Angina | 15 | 10 | 25 |
| Non-anginal | 7 | 8 | 15 |
| Asymptomatic | 3 | 17 | 20 |

#### Step 3: Compute Entropy for Each Subset

**Subset 1 — Typical Angina (40 samples: 35 Yes, 5 No):**

$$H_1 = -\left(\frac{35}{40}\log_2\frac{35}{40} + \frac{5}{40}\log_2\frac{5}{40}\right) = -(0.875 \times (-0.192) + 0.125 \times (-3.000)) = 0.544 \text{ bits}$$

**Subset 2 — Atypical Angina (25 samples: 15 Yes, 10 No):**

$$H_2 = -\left(\frac{15}{25}\log_2\frac{15}{25} + \frac{10}{25}\log_2\frac{10}{25}\right) = -(0.6 \times (-0.737) + 0.4 \times (-1.322)) = 0.971 \text{ bits}$$

**Subset 3 — Non-anginal (15 samples: 7 Yes, 8 No):**

$$H_3 = -\left(\frac{7}{15}\log_2\frac{7}{15} + \frac{8}{15}\log_2\frac{8}{15}\right) = -(0.467 \times (-1.099) + 0.533 \times (-0.908)) = 0.997 \text{ bits}$$

**Subset 4 — Asymptomatic (20 samples: 3 Yes, 17 No):**

$$H_4 = -\left(\frac{3}{20}\log_2\frac{3}{20} + \frac{17}{20}\log_2\frac{17}{20}\right) = -(0.15 \times (-2.737) + 0.85 \times (-0.234)) = 0.610 \text{ bits}$$

#### Step 4: Compute Weighted Average Conditional Entropy

$$H(\text{Parent} \mid \text{ChestPain}) = \frac{40}{100}(0.544) + \frac{25}{100}(0.971) + \frac{15}{100}(0.997) + \frac{20}{100}(0.610)$$

$$= 0.4(0.544) + 0.25(0.971) + 0.15(0.997) + 0.20(0.610)$$

$$= 0.2176 + 0.2428 + 0.1496 + 0.1220 = 0.7320 \text{ bits}$$

#### Step 5: Compute Information Gain

$$IG(\text{ChestPain}) = H(\text{Parent}) - H(\text{Parent} \mid \text{ChestPain}) = 0.971 - 0.732 = 0.239 \text{ bits}$$

> **Conclusion:** "Chest Pain Type" provides an Information Gain of **0.239 bits**, indicating it is a useful feature for predicting heart disease. Higher IG values are preferred for feature selection.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Filter Methods | Wrapper Methods | Embedded Methods |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Speed** | ⚡ Very Fast | 🐢 Very Slow | ⚡ Fast |
| **Accuracy** | Moderate | High | High |
| **Feature Interactions** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Partial |
| **Overfitting Risk** | Low | High | Low |
| **Model Dependency** | None | Specific model | Specific model |
| **Scalability** | Excellent | Poor | Good |
| **Best For** | Initial screening | Small datasets, high accuracy | Large-scale, balanced approach |
| **Healthcare Example** | Gene ranking with ANOVA | RFE for cancer prediction | Lasso for clinical risk scores |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
