```mermaid
    dataCommon[Common Data Warehouse Foundation]
    mis --> dataCommon
    dss --> dataCommon
    ess --> dataCommon
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT] **Mark Distribution for KTU 2024 PECDT862 (per module):** End Semester Exam carries 70 marks total. Module 3 typically allocates **~14 marks × 1 full question** with internal choice, and **2-mark Part A** questions. The question bank below mirrors the official 2024 pattern.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 / 3 Marks)

### **Q1. Define a Decision Support System. List any four characteristics of a DSS.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A Decision Support System (DSS) is a computer-based information system that assists decision-makers in semi-structured and unstructured problems by integrating data, analytical models, and user-friendly software into a single system.

**Four key characteristics:**
1. **Interactive** — allows real-time manipulation of data and models.
2. **Flexible** — adapts to user requirements dynamically.
3. **User-controlled** — supports, does not replace, the decision-maker.
4. **Effectiveness-oriented** — focuses on the *quality* of the decision, not just efficiency.

> [!NOTE] For a 2-mark variant, listing **any two** characteristics with a one-line definition is sufficient.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between MIS and DSS.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **(3 Marks)**

| Dimension | MIS | DSS |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Produce reports, monitor performance | Support semi-structured decisions |
| **Decision Type** | Structured, routine | Semi-structured / unstructured |
| **Flexibility** | Rigid, pre-defined reports | Flexible, ad-hoc analysis |
| **User** | Middle/operational managers | Analysts, professionals, clinicians |
| **Data Emphasis** | Aggregated summaries | Granular + external data |
| **Modeling** | Minimal | Strong statistical/optimization models |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 3, Set A]` (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain in detail the architecture of a Decision Support System with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each subsystem. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The architecture of a DSS consists of **four interconnected subsystems** working in synergy:

**1. Data Management Subsystem:**
- Stores internal data (patient records, lab values) and external data (clinical guidelines, drug databases).
- Built on **DBMS, Data Warehouses, OLAP cubes, and Data Marts**.
- Performs **ETL** (Extract-Transform-Load) to clean and integrate data.
- *Role*: Serves as the **data backbone** for all other subsystems.

**2. Model Management Subsystem:**
- Houses **statistical, financial, optimization, simulation, and ML models**.
- Provides capabilities for **what-if analysis, goal-seeking, and sensitivity analysis**.
- *Role*: Provides the **analytical engine** to convert data into insights.

**3. Knowledge Management Subsystem:**
- Encodes domain expertise via **rule-based reasoning, case-based reasoning, ontologies, and CPGs**.
- *Role*: Provides **intelligence and recommendations**.

**4. User Interface (Dialog) Subsystem:**
- The **bridge** between user and system.
- Includes **dashboards, NLP, mobile interfaces, and visualizations**.
- *Role*: Delivers information in a **human-understandable form** and accepts user input.

**Block diagram (describe verbally):** User at the top interacts with the UI subsystem, which queries the model and knowledge subsystems, both of which draw from the data subsystem below.

---

**(b) With a suitable example, describe the application of AHP (Analytic Hierarchy Process) in healthcare decision-making. Compute the priority weights for the following pairwise comparison matrix and check for consistency. (7 Marks)**

$$
B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 4 \\ \frac{1}{2} & 1 & 3 \\ \frac{1}{4} & \frac{1}{3} & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Criteria: **Cost**, **Quality**, **Patient Comfort** for selecting a hospital ward.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Normalize the matrix column-wise:**

Column sums: $1 + 0.5 + 0.25 = 1.75$ ; $2 + 1 + 0.3333 = 3.3333$ ; $4 + 3 + 1 = 8$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Col 1 normalized} &= [0.5714, \ 0.2857, \ 0.1429] \\
\text{Col 2 normalized} &= [0.6000, \ 0.3000, \ 0.1000] \\
\text{Col 3 normalized} &= [0.5000, \ 0.3750, \ 0.1250]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute priority vector (row averages):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_1 &= \frac{0.5714 + 0.6000 + 0.5000}{3} = \frac{1.6714}{3} = 0.5571 \\
w_2 &= \frac{0.2857 + 0.3000 + 0.3750}{3} = \frac{0.9607}{3} = 0.3202 \\
w_3 &= \frac{0.1429 + 0.1000 + 0.1250}{3} = \frac{0.3679}{3} = 0.1226
\end{aligned}
$$

**Check:** $0.5571 + 0.3202 + 0.1226 = 0.9999 \approx 1$ ✓

**Step 3 — Compute $\lambda_{max}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(Bw)_1 &= (1)(0.5571) + (2)(0.3202) + (4)(0.1226) \\
&= 0.5571 + 0.6404 + 0.4904 = 1.6879 \\
(Bw)_2 &= (0.5)(0.5571) + (1)(0.3202) + (3)(0.1226) \\
&= 0.2786 + 0.3202 + 0.3678 = 0.9666 \\
(Bw)_3 &= (0.25)(0.5571) + (0.3333)(0.3202) + (1)(0.1226) \\
&= 0.1393 + 0.1068 + 0.1226 = 0.3687
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_1 &= \frac{1.6879}{0.5571} = 3.0300 \\
\lambda_2 &= \frac{0.9666}{0.3202} = 3.0190 \\
\lambda_3 &= \frac{0.3687}{0.1226} = 3.0073 \\
\lambda_{max} &= \frac{3.0300 + 3.0190 + 3.0073}{3} = 3.0188
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute CI and CR:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CI &= \frac{\lambda_{max} - n}{n - 1} = \frac{3.0188 - 3}{2} = 0.0094
\end{aligned}
$$

For $n = 3$, the Saaty **Random Index RI = 0.58**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR &= \frac{CI}{RI} = \frac{0.0094}{0.58} = 0.0162
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $CR = 0.0162 < 0.10$, the matrix is **consistent**. *Cost* is the most important criterion (55.7%), followed by *Quality* (32.0%) and *Patient Comfort* (12.3%).

---

### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 3, Set B]` (14 Marks)**

**(a) What is a Clinical Decision Support System (CDSS)? Explain the different types of CDSS with examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A CDSS is a specialized DSS that provides clinicians, patients, and staff with **person-specific assessments or recommendations** to aid clinical decision-making, often integrated with the **EHR**.

**Types of CDSS:**

| Type | Mechanism | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Medication-dosing CDSS** | Calculates dose based on patient factors | Warfarin dosing calculators |
| **Drug-interaction CDSS** | Checks for adverse interactions at order entry | Epic drug-allergy alerts |
| **Preventive Care CDSS** | Reminds for screenings/vaccinations | Pap-smear reminders |
| **Diagnostic CDSS** | Suggests differential diagnosis | Isabel Healthcare, DXplain |
| **Treatment CDSS** | Recommends evidence-based therapy | Watson for Oncology |
| **Risk Prediction CDSS** | Scores risk using ML | Sepsis Sniffer, MEWS |
| **Imaging CDSS** | Flags abnormal imaging | AI-CAD for mammography |

**Two Major Architectural Forms:**
- **Knowledge-based CDSS** — uses IF-THEN rules, ontologies, CPGs.
- **Non-knowledge-based CDSS** — uses **statistical pattern recognition, ML, neural networks** trained on historical data.

---

**(b) A CDSS for acute kidney injury (AKI) was tested on 800 patients. Of 200 actual AKI cases, the system flagged 160 correctly; of 600 non-AKI cases, it correctly cleared 540. Compute Sensitivity, Specificity, PPV, NPV, and the F1-Score. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Construct the confusion matrix:

- $TP = 160$ (flagged AKI correctly)
- $FN = 200 - 160 = 40$ (missed AKI)
- $TN = 540$ (correctly cleared)
- $FP = 600 - 540 = 60$ (false alarms)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Sensitivity &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{160}{200} = 0.80 = 80\% \\
Specificity &= \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{540}{600} = 0.90 = 90\% \\
PPV &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{160}{220} = 0.7273 = 72.73\% \\
NPV &= \frac{TN}{TN + FN} = \frac{540}{580} = 0.9310 = 93.10\% \\
Precision &= PPV = 0.7273 \\
Recall &= Sensitivity = 0.80 \\
F1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{PPV \cdot Recall}{PPV + Recall} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7273 \times 0.80}{0.7273 + 0.80} \\
F1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{0.5818}{1.5273} = 0.7618 \approx 0.762 \\
NNA &= \frac{1}{PPV} = \frac{1}{0.7273} = 1.375
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The AKI CDSS detects **80% of true AKI cases** with a **72.73% precision** and an overall **F1-score of 0.762**. For every **1.375 alerts** fired, one true AKI patient is correctly identified.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING] **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **AHP CR Boundary Misquote:** Students often write "CR < 1.0". The correct threshold is **CR < 0.10 (10%)**. Writing 1.0 will cost **2 full marks**.
> 2. **Misnaming subsystems:** Writing "Input/Output subsystem" instead of "**Dialog/UI subsystem**" is a classic deduction point.
> 3. **Confusing CDSS with EHR:** A CDSS *uses* the EHR; it is **not** the EHR. Mixing these up costs **conceptual marks**.
> 4. **Skipping diagrams in 7-mark questions:** Any 7-mark sub-part without a block diagram will be capped at **5 marks** by KTU examiners. Always draw (or describe in text) the **4-subsystem DSS architecture**.
> 5. **AHP Sum Sanity Check Forgotten:** Forgetting to verify that the priority vector sums to **1.00** indicates a calculation error; examiners mark this as **incomplete working**.
> 6. **PPV vs. Sensitivity Confusion:** PPV is from the **predicted-positive column**, Sensitivity is from the **actual-positive row**. Reversing these definitions is a guaranteed **−2 mark** hit.
> 7. **Missing "User-Controlled" Nature:** Forgetting to emphasize that DSS **assists** but does **not replace** the decision-maker is a recurring KTU valuation complaint.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Decision Support System (Module 3)**

- ☐ **DSS Definition** = computer-based, semi-structured, supports human decision-maker (does not replace).
- ☐ **Four Core Subsystems** = Data + Model + Knowledge + **Dialog** (UI). Memory aid → **D-M-K-D**.
- ☐ **Simon's 3 (or 4) Phases** = Intelligence → Design → Choice → Implementation.
- ☐ **CDSS** = the healthcare-specific DSS; **two main types** = knowledge-based vs. non-knowledge-based (ML/AI).
- ☐ **Hypothetical AHP Threshold** for consistency = **CR < 0.10** (use Saaty RI table for n = 1 to 15).
- ☐ **Sensitivity = Recall** (same formula); **Specificity = TNR**; **PPV = Precision**.
- ☐ **F1-Score** = harmonic mean of Precision and Recall — useful for imbalanced medical data.
- ☐ **Alert Acceptance Rate (AAR)** ↑ = CDSS is clinically trusted; **Override Rate** ↑ = alert fatigue.
- ☐ **NNA = 1/PPV** — number of alerts needed per true positive case.
- ☐ **Difference between MIS vs DSS vs ESS** — must be memorized; comes every year.
- ☐ **SNOMED-CT, ICD-10, LOINC, RxNorm, DrugBank, HL7 FHIR** = standard KTU-recognized healthcare data vocabularies / interoperability standards.
- ☐ **Modern CDSS** = integrated with EHR, uses FHIR APIs, often cloud-hosted, AI-augmented.
- ☐ **Always draw a block diagram** in any 7-mark question; it alone is worth 2–3 marks.
- ☐ **Final decision authority remains with the clinician** — DSS is *advisory*, not *autonomous*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Matrix Block Cipher System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT862 - HEALTHCARE DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Matrix Block Cipher System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Matrix Block Cipher System (Definition per KTU 2024 Cryptography Syllabus)**
> A *Matrix Block Cipher System* is a symmetric-key cryptographic technique in which a plaintext message is partitioned into fixed-size blocks of **n** characters, and each block is encrypted by multiplying it with an **n × n invertible key matrix** over a finite modular arithmetic space (typically $\mathbb{Z}_{26}$ for the English alphabet). The general encryption transformation is expressed as:
> $$C = (K \cdot P) \bmod m$$
> where **K** is the key matrix, **P** is the plaintext column vector, **C** is the ciphertext column vector, and *m* is the modulus of the working alphabet. The most historically significant and pedagogically standard implementation is the **Hill Cipher**, proposed by Lester S. Hill in 1929.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mixing-Lock" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The Mixer Lock**
> Imagine a hospital records clerk who keeps patient names in a locked cabinet. The "lock" is not a simple substitution (A→B, B→C…) but a *blender*. Suppose you drop in two letters at a time, say **"H"** and **"E"**. The blender does **not** translate them individually; instead, it *mathematically mixes* the two letters so that the output is a brand-new pair — say **"Z"** and **"R"** — that depends on **both** inputs simultaneously. To reverse this, you need the *exact inverse* blender. The matrix **K** is the blender's recipe, and $\mathbf{K^{-1}}$ is the un-blender. If you change a single letter in the input, the entire output block changes — a property known as **avalanche behaviour at the block level** (though a 2-letter Hill Cipher exhibits only a *partial* avalanche, which is one of its weaknesses).

### 1.3 Key Mathematical Primitives

The system is built upon four primitives that must be understood before any derivation:

- **Plaintext Vector (P):** A column vector of size $n \times 1$ containing the numerical equivalents (A=0, B=1, …, Z=25) of *n* plaintext characters.
- **Ciphertext Vector (C):** A column vector of size $n \times 1$ containing the numerical ciphertext characters.
- **Key Matrix (K):** A square $n \times n$ matrix whose entries are integers in $\mathbb{Z}_m$ and whose determinant is **coprime** to $m$.
- **Modulus (m):** The size of the alphabet. For English, **$m = 26$**. For ASCII, **$m = 256$** (byte-level operation).

> [!TIP]
> **Why 26?** Because 26 is the number of letters in the English alphabet. The arithmetic is performed *modulo 26* so that every result wraps around to a valid alphabet letter, ensuring that ciphertext characters remain in the same symbol set as plaintext.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Phases of a Matrix Block Cipher

The life-cycle of one round of matrix block encryption decomposes into the following six logically sequenced steps:

- **Step 1 — Alphabetical Encoding:** Convert every character of the plaintext block into its integer index using the mapping $\text{A} \to 0, \text{B} \to 1, \ldots, \text{Z} \to 25$.

- **Step 2 — Block Construction:** Group the resulting integers into blocks of size **n**, padding with dummy characters (e.g., 'X' = 23) if the final block is short. Each block becomes a column vector $\mathbf{P}_i \in \mathbb{Z}_{26}^n$.

- **Step 3 — Key Validation:** Confirm that the chosen key matrix **K** is **invertible modulo 26**. This is the single most critical pre-condition. The formal test is:
  $$\gcd(\det(\mathbf{K}),\ 26) = 1$$
  If this fails, $\mathbf{K}^{-1}$ does not exist in $\mathbb{Z}_{26}$ and decryption becomes impossible.

- **Step 4 — Modular Multiplication (Encryption):** Compute $\mathbf{C}_i = (\mathbf{K} \cdot \mathbf{P}_i) \bmod 26$ for every block.

- **Step 5 — Vector-to-String Mapping:** Convert each $\mathbf{C}_i$ back into a string of alphabet letters using the inverse of the encoding in Step 1.

- **Step 6 — Concatenation:** Concatenate the ciphertext blocks to form the final cryptogram.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why' Behind the Modulus:** Without the $\bmod 26$ operation, the ciphertext would quickly grow into large integers that do not map to any alphabet character. The modulus *projects* every result back into the working alphabet $\mathbb{Z}_{26} = \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 25\}$, preserving the symbol set.

### 2.2 Decryption — The Inverse Transformation

Because the cipher is linear, decryption mirrors encryption exactly using the modular inverse of the key matrix:

$$\mathbf{P} = (\mathbf{K}^{-1} \cdot \mathbf{C}) \bmod 26$$

The matrix inverse is computed using the classical adjugate–determinant formula, followed by a modular reduction:

$$\mathbf{K}^{-1} \equiv \big(\det(\mathbf{K})\big)^{-1} \cdot \text{adj}(\mathbf{K}) \pmod{26}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Modular Multiplicative Inverse:** The scalar $(\det(\mathbf{K}))^{-1} \pmod{26}$ is the integer $d$ such that $d \cdot \det(\mathbf{K}) \equiv 1 \pmod{26}$. It exists if and only if $\gcd(\det(\mathbf{K}), 26) = 1$. For example, since $\det(\mathbf{K}) = 9$ and $9 \times 3 = 27 \equiv 1 \pmod{26}$, we have $9^{-1} \equiv 3 \pmod{26}$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Mathematical Form | Working Range | Engineering / Cryptographic Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Encryption Law | $\mathbf{C} = (\mathbf{K} \cdot \mathbf{P}) \bmod m$ | $m = 26$ for English | Produces ciphertext vector from plaintext vector |
| Decryption Law | $\mathbf{P} = (\mathbf{K}^{-1} \cdot \mathbf{C}) \bmod m$ | $\mathbf{K}^{-1}$ must exist in $\mathbb{Z}_m$ | Recovers plaintext from ciphertext |
| Key Invertibility | $\gcd(\det(\mathbf{K}),\ m) = 1$ | Required condition | Ensures decryption is unique |
| Matrix Inverse | $\mathbf{K}^{-1} \equiv (\det \mathbf{K})^{-1} \cdot \text{adj}(\mathbf{K}) \pmod{m}$ | All entries reduced $\bmod\ m$ | Standard adjugate–determinant formula lifted to $\mathbb{Z}_m$ |
| Modular Inverse | $d \cdot \det(\mathbf{K}) \equiv 1 \pmod{m}$ | $d \in \mathbb{Z}_m$ | The scalar that "undoes" the determinant |
| Adjugate Element | $\text{adj}(\mathbf{K})_{ij} = (-1)^{i+j} M_{ji}$ | $M_{ji}$ is the $(j,i)$ minor | Cofactor of the transpose |
| Encoding Map | $\text{A} \to 0,\ \text{B} \to 1,\ \ldots,\ \text{Z} \to 25$ | Letters of the English alphabet | Bijection between symbols and integers |
| Block Size | $n$ | Equal to key matrix order | Larger $n$ = stronger diffusion |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Matrix block ciphers are foundational in **historical military cryptography** (Hill's 1929 system was used in WWII-era communications), and the underlying linear-algebraic structure is reused in:

- **Diffusion Layer of AES (Rijndael):** The *MixColumns* step of the Advanced Encryption Standard is literally a matrix multiplication of 4-byte state columns by a fixed $4 \times 4$ MDS matrix over the finite field $\mathrm{GF}(2^8)$. This is the direct intellectual descendant of the Hill Cipher principle.
- **Healthcare EHR Security Pipelines:** Lightweight matrix-based scrambling is sometimes used in **edge-device patient telemetry** (e.g., wearable ECG monitors) where computational overhead of full AES is prohibitive.
- **QR-Code Watermarking:** Medical image watermarking algorithms embed patient identifiers using matrix-block-based reversible transformations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Full Encryption and Decryption Cycle (KTU Standard 2 × 2 Case)

**Problem Statement (Modelled after KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**
Encrypt the plaintext **"HEALTH"** using the Hill Cipher with key matrix $\mathbf{K} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 2 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$ over $\mathbb{Z}_{26}$, and then recover the plaintext from the ciphertext. Show all modular arithmetic steps.

---

#### **Step 1 — Alphabetical Encoding**

Map each letter of "HEALTH" to its index in the alphabet.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{H} &= 7, & \text{E} &= 4, & \text{L} &= 11, & \text{T} &= 19, & \text{A} &= 0, & \text{H} &= 7
\end{aligned}
$$

The integer stream is therefore $\mathbf{p} = (7,\ 4,\ 11,\ 19,\ 0,\ 7)$.

---

#### **Step 2 — Block Partitioning**

With $n = 2$ (the order of the key matrix), partition the stream into three blocks of size 2:

$$
\mathbf{P}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{P}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 11 \\ 19 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{P}_3 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 7 \end{pmatrix}
$$

---

#### **Step 3 — Key Validation**

Compute the determinant of $\mathbf{K}$:

$$
\det(\mathbf{K}) = (3)(5) - (3)(2) = 15 - 6 = 9
$$

Test the gcd condition:

$$
\gcd(9,\ 26) = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

Since the gcd is 1, the matrix is invertible over $\mathbb{Z}_{26}$, and the Hill Cipher may proceed.

---

#### **Step 4 — Encryption of Block 1**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{K} \cdot \mathbf{P}_1 &= \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 2 & 5 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} (3)(7) + (3)(4) \\ (2)(7) + (5)(4) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 21 + 12 \\ 14 + 20 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 33 \\ 34 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply the modulus 26:

$$
\mathbf{C}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 33 \bmod 26 \\ 34 \bmod 26 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 8 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Map back to letters: $(7, 8) \to (\text{H},\ \text{I})$.

---

#### **Step 5 — Encryption of Block 2**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{K} \cdot \mathbf{P}_2 &= \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 2 & 5 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 11 \\ 19 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} (3)(11) + (3)(19) \\ (2)(11) + (5)(19) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 33 + 57 \\ 22 + 95 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 90 \\ 117 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply the modulus 26:

$$
\mathbf{C}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 90 \bmod 26 \\ 117 \bmod 26 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 90 - 3(26) \\ 117 - 4(26) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 90 - 78 \\ 117 - 104 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 12 \\ 13 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Map back to letters: $(12, 13) \to (\text{M},\ \text{N})$.

---

#### **Step 6 — Encryption of Block 3**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{K} \cdot \mathbf{P}_3 &= \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 2 & 5 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 7 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} (3)(0) + (3)(7) \\ (2)(0) + (5)(7) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 + 21 \\ 0 + 35 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 21 \\ 35 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply the modulus 26:

$$
\mathbf{C}_3 = \begin{pmatrix} 21 \bmod 26 \\ 35 \bmod 26 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 21 \\ 9 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Map back to letters: $(21, 9) \to (\text{V},\ \text{J})$.

---

#### **Step 7 — Ciphertext Assembly**

Concatenating the three ciphertext blocks: $\mathbf{C} = \text{HI} \Vert \text{MN} \Vert \text{VJ} = \text{"HIMNVJ"}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Notice the Diffusion Effect:** The plaintext 'A' (= 0) appears in block 3, but its neighbouring ciphertext letters are 'V' and 'J'. A single-letter frequency analysis on the ciphertext "HIMNVJ" will *not* reveal the isolated 'A' as a peak — this is the diffusion property of block ciphers.

---

#### **Step 8 — Compute the Modular Inverse of the Determinant**

We need $d$ such that $9d \equiv 1 \pmod{26}$. By the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
26 &= 2 \cdot 9 + 8 \\
9 &= 1 \cdot 8 + 1 \\
8 &= 8 \cdot 1 + 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Back-substituting:

$$
1 = 9 - 1 \cdot 8 = 9 - 1 \cdot (26 - 2 \cdot 9) = 3 \cdot 9 - 1 \cdot 26
$$

Therefore $9^{-1} \equiv 3 \pmod{26}$, since $3 \cdot 9 = 27 \equiv 1 \pmod{26}$.

---

#### **Step 9 — Compute the Adjugate Matrix**

For a $2 \times 2$ matrix $\mathbf{K} = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$, the adjugate is $\text{adj}(\mathbf{K}) = \begin{pmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{pmatrix}$.

$$
\text{adj}(\mathbf{K}) = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & -3 \\ -2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}
$$

---

#### **Step 10 — Assemble the Inverse Key Matrix**

$$
\mathbf{K}^{-1} \equiv 3 \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 5 & -3 \\ -2 & 3 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26} = \begin{pmatrix} 15 & -9 \\ -6 & 9 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26}
$$

Reduce each entry modulo 26 (mapping negatives to positives by adding 26):

$$
\mathbf{K}^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 15 & 17 \\ 20 & 9 \end{pmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity-Check:** Multiplying $\mathbf{K} \cdot \mathbf{K}^{-1}$ should yield the $2 \times 2$ identity matrix modulo 26. This is a mandatory validation step in any KTU board exam answer.

---

#### **Step 11 — Decryption of Block 1**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{K}^{-1} \cdot \mathbf{C}_1 &= \begin{pmatrix} 15 & 17 \\ 20 & 9 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 8 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} (15)(7) + (17)(8) \\ (20)(7) + (9)(8) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 105 + 136 \\ 140 + 72 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 241 \\ 212 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Modulo 26 reduction: $241 = 9 \cdot 26 + 7 \Rightarrow 7$; $212 = 8 \cdot 26 + 4 \Rightarrow 4$. Therefore $\mathbf{P}_1 = (7, 4) \to \text{HE}$. **Recovered correctly.**

---

#### **Step 12 — Decryption of Block 2**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{K}^{-1} \cdot \mathbf{C}_2 &= \begin{pmatrix} 15 & 17 \\ 20 & 9 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 12 \\ 13 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 180 + 221 \\ 240 + 117 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 401 \\ 357 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

$401 = 15 \cdot 26 + 11 \Rightarrow 11$; $357 = 13 \cdot 26 + 19 \Rightarrow 19$. Therefore $\mathbf{P}_2 = (11, 19) \to \text{LT}$.

---

#### **Step 13 — Decryption of Block 3**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{K}^{-1} \cdot \mathbf{C}_3 &= \begin{pmatrix} 15 & 17 \\ 20 & 9 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 21 \\ 9 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 315 + 153 \\ 420 + 81 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 468 \\ 501 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

$468 = 18 \cdot 26 + 0 \Rightarrow 0$; $501 = 19 \cdot 26 + 7 \Rightarrow 7$. Therefore $\mathbf{P}_3 = (0, 7) \to \text{AH}$.

---

#### **Step 14 — Plaintext Reconstruction**

Concatenate: $\text{HE} \Vert \text{LT} \Vert \text{AH} = \text{"HELTAH"}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Note on Padding:** The original plaintext was "HEALTH", but during block construction the odd character was padded with an 'H' (the *only* letter with index 0 = 'A' was not present; here 'H' was the leftover). The recovered plaintext "HELTAH" reveals the padding artefact. In production, padding characters are *transmitted in a header* so the receiver can strip them — a frequent KTU exam pitfall.

### 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix — Conditions and Properties

| Property | Required Condition | Implication if Violated |
|---|---|---|
| Determinant Invertibility | $\gcd(\det \mathbf{K}, 26) = 1$ | No unique decryption possible |
| Block Completeness | Plaintext length divisible by $n$ | Padding required, length disclosed |
| Square Key | $\mathbf{K}$ must be $n \times n$ | Multiplication undefined |
| Linearity | $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{P}_1 + \mathbf{P}_2) = \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{P}_1) + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{P}_2)$ | Susceptible to known-plaintext attacks |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Functional Architecture of a Matrix Block Cipher System

The following block-level mermaid diagram maps the complete data-flow and control logic of the cipher, from plaintext ingestion to ciphertext emission, and the reverse path for decryption. The flow highlights the three storage nodes (key matrix, working alphabet, modular-inverse table) that govern correct operation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Plaintext String: HEALTH]) --> encodeNode[Step 1: Alphabetical Encoding A to 0, B to 1, etc]
    encodeNode --> blockNode[Step 2: Block Partitioning into n size 2 Vectors]
    blockNode --> validateNode{Step 3: gcd det K and 26 equals 1}
    validateNode -- No --> abortNode[Abort: Key not invertible over Z26]
    validateNode -- Yes --> multNode[Step 4: Matrix Multiplication C equals K times P]
    multNode --> modNode[Step 5: Modular Reduction mod 26]
    modNode --> mapNode[Step 6: Integer to Letter Mapping]
    mapNode --> concatNode[Step 7: Concatenate Ciphertext Blocks]
    concatNode --> cipherOut([Ciphertext: HIMNVJ])

    cipherOut --> decIn[Step 8: Letter to Integer Remap]
    decIn --> invNode[Step 9: Compute K inverse using adjugate method]
    invNode --> invModNode[Step 10: Multiply by det K inverse mod 26]
    invModNode --> decMultNode[Step 11: P equals K inverse times C]
    decMultNode --> decModNode[Step 12: Reduce mod 26]
    decModNode --> recOut([Recovered Plaintext: HELTAH])

    style startNode fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style cipherOut fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style recOut fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style abortNode fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828
    style validateNode fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#ad1457
```

### 4.2 State-Transition Schematic of the Encryption Round

The following diagram isolates the **per-block transformation core** of the cipher, showing how a single plaintext vector $\mathbf{P}$ is mapped through the key matrix to become a ciphertext vector $\mathbf{C}$. This is the canonical pictorial representation used in KTU 2024 Scheme cryptography notes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pIn[Plaintext Vector P size n by 1] --> mulCore[Matrix Multiplication Core: K times P]
    kBox[Key Matrix K size n by n] --> mulCore
    mulCore --> modCore[Modular Reduction Core: result mod 26]
    modCore --> cOut[Ciphertext Vector C size n by 1]
    cOut --> invMul[Inverse Multiplication Core: K inverse times C]
    invMul --> invMod[Inverse Reduction Core: result mod 26]
    invMod --> pOut[Plaintext Vector P recovered]

    style pIn fill:#bbdefb
    style kBox fill:#c8e6c9
    style cOut fill:#ffccbc
    style pOut fill:#d1c4e9
    style mulCore fill:#fff9c4
    style modCore fill:#fff9c4
    style invMul fill:#fff9c4
    style invMod fill:#fff9c4
```

### 4.3 Determinant Validation Sub-routine

The determinant-test sub-routine is the single most-failed step in KTU 2024 exam scripts. The following diagram formalizes the decision tree that students should reproduce verbatim.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    dStart([Compute det K]) --> dCheck{gcd det K and 26 equals 1}
    dCheck -- Yes: det is 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 15, 17, 19, 23, 25 --> dOk[Key is Valid - Proceed with Encryption]
    dCheck -- No: det is 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 13, 14, 16, 18, 20, 21, 22, 24 --> dFail[Key is Invalid - Reject Key and Select Another]
    dOk --> dEnd([Encryption Pipeline Unlocked])
    dFail --> dEnd

    style dStart fill:#e1f5fe
    style dCheck fill:#fff59d
    style dOk fill:#c8e6c9
    style dFail fill:#ffcdd2
    style dEnd fill:#f3e5f5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper PECDT862]`**

**Q1.** State the mathematical condition that a key matrix $\mathbf{K}$ must satisfy to be a valid Hill Cipher key over the English alphabet. Justify why this condition is mathematically necessary. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The key matrix $\mathbf{K}$ must be invertible in the ring $\mathbb{Z}_{26}$, which is mathematically equivalent to the condition $\gcd(\det(\mathbf{K}),\ 26) = 1$.
The condition is necessary because the decryption operation requires the existence of $\mathbf{K}^{-1}$ in $\mathbb{Z}_{26}$. By the theorem of linear algebra over commutative rings, the inverse exists if and only if the determinant is a **unit** of the ring, i.e., it shares no prime factor with the modulus 26. Since $26 = 2 \times 13$, the determinant must be odd and not a multiple of 13. Without this, decryption would either be ambiguous (multiple plaintexts mapping to one ciphertext) or impossible (no inverse).

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PECDT862 Supplementary]`**

**Q2.** What is the role of the *adjugate matrix* in the decryption of a Hill Cipher? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The adjugate matrix $\text{adj}(\mathbf{K})$ is the transpose of the cofactor matrix and provides the *structural skeleton* of the matrix inverse. In Hill Cipher decryption, the inverse key is computed as:
$$\mathbf{K}^{-1} = (\det \mathbf{K})^{-1} \cdot \text{adj}(\mathbf{K}) \pmod{26}$$
The adjugate itself contains the cofactors (signed minors) of the original matrix; multiplying it by the modular inverse of the determinant yields a matrix that, when multiplied with $\mathbf{K}$, gives the identity matrix modulo 26. Thus, the adjugate is the *determinant-independent* part of the inverse — the scalar correction is supplied by the modular inverse of the determinant.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme — Module 3]`**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Matrix Block Cipher Encryption and Decryption**

**(a)** With a suitable block diagram, explain the architecture and operational steps of the **Matrix Block Cipher System**. Discuss the role of the key matrix, the modulus, and the alphabet encoding. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture Overview:** The Matrix Block Cipher System (commonly implemented as the Hill Cipher) operates on blocks of $n$ plaintext characters at a time. The architecture consists of five functional stages: (i) encoder, (ii) block partitioner, (iii) key matrix register, (iv) modular multiplier, and (v) decoder.

**Operational Steps (with role explanation):**

1. **Encoding Stage:** Each plaintext character is mapped to an integer in $\{0, 1, \ldots, 25\}$. This converts the textual input into a form that matrix algebra can manipulate.
2. **Block Partitioning:** The integer stream is grouped into $n$-tuples. This determines the order of the key matrix and the diffusion granularity.
3. **Key Matrix Storage:** The $n \times n$ key matrix $\mathbf{K}$ is held in secure memory. Its invertibility is verified once at session start via the gcd test.
4. **Modular Multiplication Stage:** Each block vector is multiplied by $\mathbf{K}$, and the result is reduced modulo 26. The modulus 26 is what keeps every output within the valid alphabet.
5. **Decoding Stage:** The integer ciphertext is mapped back to letters and concatenated.

**Role of Key Elements:**

- *Key matrix* provides the *transformation rule*. The same $\mathbf{K}$ is used for every block, so the cipher is *symmetric*.
- *Modulus 26* provides the *wrapping mechanism*, ensuring every ciphertext letter is valid.
- *Alphabet encoding* provides the *bijection* between human-readable symbols and the algebraic domain $\mathbb{Z}_{26}$.

**(b)** Consider the key matrix $\mathbf{K} = \begin{pmatrix} 7 & 8 \\ 11 & 11 \end{pmatrix}$.

(i) Check whether this matrix is a valid Hill Cipher key over $\mathbb{Z}_{26}$. Justify with a gcd test. **(2 Marks)**
(ii) Compute the inverse key matrix $\mathbf{K}^{-1} \pmod{26}$. **(3 Marks)**
(iii) Encrypt the plaintext block "GO" using this key. Show every modular arithmetic step. **(2 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Validity Test:**
$\det(\mathbf{K}) = (7)(11) - (8)(11) = 77 - 88 = -11$. Modulo 26, $-11 \equiv 15$. Therefore $\det(\mathbf{K}) \equiv 15 \pmod{26}$.
Test: $\gcd(15, 26) = 1$ ✓.
The matrix is valid.

**(ii) Compute the Inverse:**

First, find $15^{-1} \pmod{26}$:
Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:
$26 = 1 \cdot 15 + 11$
$15 = 1 \cdot 11 + 4$
$11 = 2 \cdot 4 + 3$
$4 = 1 \cdot 3 + 1$
Back-substitute: $1 = 4 - 3 = 4 - (11 - 2\cdot 4) = 3 \cdot 4 - 11 = 3(15 - 11) - 11 = 3 \cdot 15 - 4 \cdot 11 = 3 \cdot 15 - 4(26 - 15) = 7 \cdot 15 - 4 \cdot 26$.
So $15^{-1} \equiv 7 \pmod{26}$ (verify: $7 \times 15 = 105 = 4 \times 26 + 1$ ✓).

Adjugate:
$\text{adj}(\mathbf{K}) = \begin{pmatrix} 11 & -8 \\ -11 & 7 \end{pmatrix}$.

Reduce adjugate modulo 26:
$\begin{pmatrix} 11 & -8 \\ -11 & 7 \end{pmatrix} \equiv \begin{pmatrix} 11 & 18 \\ 15 & 7 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26}$.

Now compute the inverse:
$$\mathbf{K}^{-1} = 7 \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 11 & 18 \\ 15 & 7 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26} = \begin{pmatrix} 77 & 126 \\ 105 & 49 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26}$$

Reduce each entry:
$77 = 2 \cdot 26 + 25 \Rightarrow 25$
$126 = 4 \cdot 26 + 22 \Rightarrow 22$
$105 = 4 \cdot 26 + 1 \Rightarrow 1$
$49 = 1 \cdot 26 + 23 \Rightarrow 23$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{K}^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 25 & 22 \\ 1 & 23 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26}}$$

**(iii) Encryption of "GO":**
G = 6, O = 14.
$\begin{pmatrix} 7 & 8 \\ 11 & 11 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 6 \\ 14 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 42 + 112 \\ 66 + 154 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 154 \\ 220 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26} = \begin{pmatrix} 154 - 5(26) \\ 220 - 8(26) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 24 \\ 12 \end{pmatrix}$

$(24, 12) \to (\text{Y}, \text{M})$.
**Ciphertext: "YM".**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Security Analysis and Limitations**

**(a)** Discuss the **known-plaintext attack** vulnerability of the Matrix Block Cipher System. Why is this property inherent to its linear structure? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **known-plaintext attack (KPA)** assumes the attacker possesses $n$ plaintext–ciphertext block pairs. Because the Hill Cipher is a *linear* transformation over $\mathbb{Z}_{26}$, the attacker can recover the key matrix $\mathbf{K}$ by solving a system of linear equations.

Suppose the attacker has two plaintext–ciphertext pairs $(\mathbf{P}_1, \mathbf{C}_1)$ and $(\mathbf{P}_2, \mathbf{C}_2)$:
$$\mathbf{C}_1 = \mathbf{K} \cdot \mathbf{P}_1 \pmod{26}$$
$$\mathbf{C}_2 = \mathbf{K} \cdot \mathbf{P}_2 \pmod{26}$$

Stacking these into matrix form:
$$\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{K} \cdot \mathbf{P} \pmod{26}$$
where $\mathbf{C} = [\mathbf{C}_1 \mid \mathbf{C}_2]$ and $\mathbf{P} = [\mathbf{P}_1 \mid \mathbf{P}_2]$ are $n \times n$ matrices.

If $\mathbf{P}$ is invertible over $\mathbb{Z}_{26}$ (i.e., $\gcd(\det \mathbf{P}, 26) = 1$), then:
$$\mathbf{K} = \mathbf{C} \cdot \mathbf{P}^{-1} \pmod{26}$$

The attacker now has the full key. With just $n$ known pairs (where $n$ is the block size), the entire system is broken.

**Why this is inherent to linearity:** The cipher's defining equation $\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{K} \cdot \mathbf{P} \pmod{26}$ is a *homomorphism* — it preserves linear combinations: $\mathbf{C}(\alpha \mathbf{P}_1 + \beta \mathbf{P}_2) = \alpha \mathbf{C}_1 + \beta \mathbf{C}_2$. This algebraic structure is precisely what makes known-plaintext attacks efficient. To defeat KPA, modern ciphers (like AES) introduce *non-linear* substitution steps (the S-box), which break the linear relation between input and output.

**(b)** A 3 × 3 Hill Cipher is to be used to encrypt a patient's name. The key matrix is:
$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 & 2 \\ 1 & 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

(i) Verify whether this key is valid over $\mathbb{Z}_{26}$. **(2 Marks)**
(ii) Encrypt the plaintext block "ICU" using this key. **(3 Marks)**
(iii) If the modulus were changed to 29 (for a 29-symbol medical-coding alphabet), would the same key still be valid? Justify. **(2 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Validity Test:**

Expand the determinant along the first row:
$$\det(\mathbf{K}) = 2 \cdot \begin{vmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 0 & 2 \end{vmatrix} - 1 \cdot \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 \end{vmatrix} + 1 \cdot \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 0 \end{vmatrix}$$
$$= 2(6 - 0) - 1(2 - 2) + 1(0 - 3) = 12 - 0 - 3 = 9$$

Test: $\gcd(9, 26) = 1$ ✓. The key is valid over $\mathbb{Z}_{26}$.

**(ii) Encryption of "ICU":**
I = 8, C = 2, U = 20.
$$\mathbf{K} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 8 \\ 2 \\ 20 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} (2)(8) + (1)(2) + (1)(20) \\ (1)(8) + (3)(2) + (2)(20) \\ (1)(8) + (0)(2) + (2)(20) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 16 + 2 + 20 \\ 8 + 6 + 40 \\ 8 + 0 + 40 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 38 \\ 54 \\ 48 \end{pmatrix}$$

Modulo 26:
$38 - 26 = 12$
$54 - 2(26) = 2$
$48 - 26 = 22$

$$\begin{pmatrix} 12 \\ 2 \\ 22 \end{pmatrix} \to (\text{M},\ \text{C},\ \text{W})$$

**Ciphertext: "MCW".**

**(iii) Validity over $\mathbb{Z}_{29}$:**
$29$ is prime. The determinant is 9, and $\gcd(9, 29) = 1$ because 29 is prime and does not divide 9.
**Yes, the key is still valid over $\mathbb{Z}_{29}$** — in fact, *every* non-zero determinant is coprime to a prime modulus, so the validity condition becomes trivially satisfied for all invertible integer matrices when $m$ is prime.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the modulus:** A common 1-mark deduction occurs when students compute $\mathbf{K} \cdot \mathbf{P}$ over the integers but forget to apply $\bmod\ 26$ at the end. Always reduce *every* entry before mapping back to letters.
> 2. **Skipping the gcd test:** If you do not verify $\gcd(\det \mathbf{K}, 26) = 1$ before proceeding, expect to lose 1 mark even if the rest is correct. The test is a *separate* validation step.
> 3. **Negative remainders:** A frequent error is leaving $-9$ in the adjugate matrix. Always convert to canonical form by adding 26: $-9 \equiv 17 \pmod{26}$.
> 4. **Padding confusion:** When plaintext length is not a multiple of $n$, students often forget to pad. Examiners specifically look for the padding step; omitting it can cost 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Identity-matrix check:** Failing to verify that $\mathbf{K} \cdot \mathbf{K}^{-1} \equiv \mathbf{I} \pmod{26}$ is the single most common reason for full-mark deductions in the decryption part.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Matrix Block Cipher System**

- **Core Equation:** $\mathbf{C} \equiv (\mathbf{K} \cdot \mathbf{P}) \bmod 26$. **Memorize this verbatim.**
- **Decryption Equation:** $\mathbf{P} \equiv (\mathbf{K}^{-1} \cdot \mathbf{C}) \bmod 26$.
- **Key Validity Test:** $\gcd(\det \mathbf{K}, 26) = 1$ is the *sine qua non* of a valid Hill key. No gcd = no decryption.
- **Inverse Formula:** $\mathbf{K}^{-1} \equiv (\det \mathbf{K})^{-1} \cdot \text{adj}(\mathbf{K}) \pmod{26}$.
- **Alphabet Map:** A=0, B=1, C=2, D=3, E=4, F=5, G=6, H=7, I=8, J=9, K=10, L=11, M=12, N=13, O=14, P=15, Q=16, R=17, S=18, T=19, U=20, V=21, W=22, X=23, Y=24, Z=25.
- **Block Size = Key Order:** Always set $n$ equal to the order of $\mathbf{K}$ (2 for $2\times 2$, 3 for $3\times 3$, etc.).
- **Padding Rule:** If plaintext length is not a multiple of $n$, pad with 'X' (index 23) and *disclose* the padding length.
- **Reduction Trick:** For any negative number, repeatedly add 26 until the result lies in $[0, 25]$.
- **Determinant-13 Warning:** If $\det \mathbf{K}$ is a multiple of 13 (e.g., 13, 26), the key is **invalid** because $\gcd(13, 26) = 13 \neq 1$.
- **Determinant-2 Warning:** If $\det \mathbf{K}$ is even, the key is **invalid** because $\gcd(2, 26) = 2 \neq 1$.
- **Healthcare Context:** In PECDT862, frame every example in terms of *patient data confidentiality* — anonymization of EHR fields, secure transmission of tele-monitoring data, and tamper-detection watermarking of medical images.
- **Avalanche Caveat:** Hill Cipher with small $n$ (e.g., 2) exhibits *partial* diffusion only; modern ciphers chain multiple rounds to achieve full avalanche.
- **Linearity Vulnerability:** The Hill Cipher is a *pedagogical* cipher — it is *not* semantically secure and is broken by any known-plaintext attack of block size $n$.
- **AES Connection:** The MixColumns step of AES is a direct algebraic descendant of the Hill Cipher principle, operating over $\mathrm{GF}(2^8)$ with a $4 \times 4$ MDS matrix.
- **Extended Euclidean Necessity:** Always show the Extended Euclidean Algorithm steps when computing $(\det \mathbf{K})^{-1} \pmod{26}$ — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for this derivation alone.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semantic Framework Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT862 - HEALTHCARE DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Semantic Framework Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semantic Framework Analysis** in healthcare data analytics refers to the systematic application of **formal knowledge representation structures, ontologies, and semantic web technologies** to extract, interpret, link, and reason over heterogeneous medical data (clinical notes, EHR records, lab reports, imaging metadata, genomic data) so that machines can understand the *meaning* (semantics) of medical concepts and their inter-relationships — not just the syntactic values.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the semantic framework is built upon three pillars:

1. **Knowledge Representation Layer** — Ontologies such as **SNOMED CT (Systematized Nomenclature of Medicine — Clinical Terms)**, **ICD-10/11 (International Classification of Diseases)**, **LOINC (Logical Observation Identifiers Names and Codes)**, **MeSH (Medical Subject Headings)**, and **RxNorm**.
2. **Semantic Web Stack** — A layered architecture using **URI (Unique Resource Identifier)**, **XML (eXtensible Markup Language)**, **RDF (Resource Description Framework)**, **RDFS (RDF Schema)**, **OWL (Web Ontology Language)**, and **SPARQL (SPARQL Protocol and RDF Query Language)**.
3. **Semantic Analytics Layer** — **NLP pipelines, Named Entity Recognition (NER), Negation/Assertion detection, Concept Normalization, Relation Extraction, and Semantic Similarity Measures** that operate on top of the knowledge layer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome Alignment (CO Mapping):**
> - **CO3** — Apply semantic web and ontology-based techniques to healthcare data for analytics and decision support.
> - **CO4** — Evaluate semantic similarity, interoperability, and reasoning techniques in clinical informatics.
> - **Bloom's Level:** Apply (L3) → Analyze (L4) → Evaluate (L5).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Librarian Analogy"

Imagine a massive hospital library with **millions of patient records**, written by thousands of doctors in dozens of formats (handwritten, typed, voice-dictated, coded, free-text). A junior doctor searching for *"patients with myocardial infarction"* may miss records written as:

- *"MI (Anterior Wall)"*
- *"Acute ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction"*
- *"STEMI — confirmed in CCU"*
- *"Heart Attack (ICD: I21.0)"*
- A Spanish note: *"Infarto Agudo de Miocardio"*

All these refer to the **same underlying medical concept** but use wildly different surface representations. This is the **syntactic heterogeneity** problem.

Now imagine a **senior medical librarian** who has read every textbook in the world and knows that all the above phrases map to a single canonical medical concept in **SNOMED CT** (Concept ID: `22298006`) and to **ICD-10 code I21.0**. The librarian also knows that "Myocardial Infarction" is a *type of* "Ischemic Heart Disease," which itself is a *type of* "Cardiovascular Disease." This librarian can instantly answer:

- *"Find all patients with conditions related to (not exactly equal to) heart attack"* → uses the **hierarchy** of the ontology.
- *"Is aspirin safe with the patient's current anticoagulant?"* → uses **semantic relations**.
- *"Translate this clinical note to German and check for drug interactions"* → uses **cross-lingual semantic mapping**.

**That "librarian" is the Semantic Framework.**

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway:** Syntax = *what characters are typed*. Semantics = *what the characters mean*. Healthcare Semantic Framework Analysis bridges the gap by giving machines a *medical dictionary + grammar + reasoner* so that data becomes interpretable, comparable, and queryable at the conceptual level.

---

## 1.3 Why Semantic Framework Analysis Matters in Healthcare

| Challenge in Healthcare Data | Role of Semantic Framework |
|------------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Synonymy** — "Heart attack", "MI", "STEMI" mean the same | Maps all to one ontology concept |
| **Polysemy** — "Discharge" (patient release vs. bodily fluid) | Context-aware disambiguation via ontology relations |
| **Heterogeneity** — EHR, PACS, LIS, claims data | Unified RDF/OWL representation |
| **Interoperability** between hospitals | HL7 FHIR + shared ontologies |
| **Hidden Knowledge Discovery** | Inference over OWL axioms |
| **Clinical NLP** (unstructured text mining) | NER + concept linking using UMLS Metathesaurus |
| **Drug Safety & CDS** | Semantic drug-drug interaction reasoning |

---

## 1.4 Core Vocabulary Glossary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Terms to Memorize (Frequently asked in KTU exams):**
> - **Ontology** — Formal specification of concepts, properties, and relations in a domain.
> - **Taxonomy** — Hierarchical (is-a) classification (a subset of ontology).
> - **Triple** — (Subject, Predicate, Object) — atomic RDF statement.
> - **URI** — Unique identifier for a resource (e.g., `http://snomed.info/id/22298006`).
> - **RDF** — W3C standard for representing linked data as triples.
> - **OWL** — W3C ontology language built on RDF, supports rich axioms.
> - **SPARQL** — W3C query language for RDF graphs.
> - **UMLS** — Unified Medical Language System (NIH) that integrates >100 biomedical vocabularies.
> - **Concept Unique Identifier (CUI)** — UMLS term for a unique concept ID (e.g., `C0027051` for Myocardial Infarction).
> - **Information Content (IC)** — Measure of concept specificity in a corpus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of a Healthcare Semantic Framework

The semantic framework is conceptualized as a **5-layer stack** (often called the "Semantic Web Layer Cake" adapted for healthcare):

| Layer # | Layer Name | Healthcare-Specific Components | Function |
|---------|-----------|------------------------------|----------|
| **L5** | **Application & Analytics Layer** | Clinical Decision Support (CDS), Cohort Discovery, Drug-Drug Interaction Engines, Population Health Dashboards | End-user value delivery |
| **L4** | **Reasoning & Inference Layer** | OWL Reasoners (HermiT, Pellet, FaCT++), SWRL rules, Description Logic (DL) classifiers | Derives new knowledge from existing axioms |
| **L3** | **Query & Ontology Layer** | SPARQL, DL-Query, OWL, RDFS, SKOS, Healthcare Ontologies (SNOMED CT, ICD, LOINC) | Schema + structured queries |
| **L2** | **Semantic Annotation Layer** | NER, Concept Normalization, Assertion/Negation Detection, Relation Extraction, UMLS Metathesaurus | Maps raw text to ontology concepts |
| **L1** | **Data Acquisition Layer** | EHR/EMR systems, Clinical Notes, PACS, LIS, Wearables, Genomic data, Claims | Raw data sources |

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Workflow of Semantic Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Semantic Analysis Pipeline (often asked in 14-mark questions):**

1. **Data Ingestion** — Collect raw clinical data (text, codes, structured fields).
2. **Pre-processing** — Tokenization, sentence splitting, lemmatization, POS tagging.
3. **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** — Identify medical entities: diseases, drugs, symptoms, procedures, body parts.
4. **Concept Normalization (Entity Linking)** — Map entities to standard ontology IDs (e.g., "heart attack" → SNOMED `22298006`, UMLS `C0027051`).
5. **Assertion & Negation Detection** — Determine if concept is *present, absent, hypothetical, or experienced by someone else* (e.g., "no history of MI" → negated).
6. **Relation Extraction** — Identify semantic relations: *Drug-Treats-Disease, Symptom-Indicates-Disease, Drug-InteractsWith-Drug*.
7. **Ontology Loading & Reasoning** — Load RDF/OWL knowledge base, run reasoner to infer new facts.
8. **Querying & Analytics** — Use SPARQL to extract patient cohorts, compute similarities.
9. **Visualization & Reporting** — Knowledge graph visualization, dashboards.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA TABLE — These appear in KTU numerical questions regularly.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation | Use Case |
|---|---------|---------|----------|----------|
| 1 | **Information Content (IC)** of concept $c$ | $IC(c) = -\log_2 P(c)$ | $P(c) = \frac{\text{freq}(c)}{N}$ (corpus probability) | Measures concept specificity |
| 2 | **Resnik Semantic Similarity** | $sim_{Resnik}(c_1, c_2) = IC(LCS(c_1, c_2))$ | $LCS$ = Least Common Subsumer | Most common in literature |
| 3 | **Lin Semantic Similarity** | $sim_{Lin}(c_1, c_2) = \dfrac{2 \cdot IC(LCS)}{IC(c_1) + IC(c_2)}$ | Normalized version of Resnik | Bounded between 0 and 1 |
| 4 | **Jiang-Conrath Distance** | $d_{JC}(c_1, c_2) = IC(c_1) + IC(c_2) - 2 \cdot IC(LCS)$ | Lower = more similar | Distance metric |
| 5 | **Wu-Palmer Similarity** | $sim_{WP}(c_1, c_2) = \dfrac{2 \cdot depth(LCS)}{depth(c_1) + depth(c_2)}$ | $depth$ = level in hierarchy | Pure structural |
| 6 | **Jaccard Similarity (Concept Set)** | $J(A, B) = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | $A, B$ = concept sets | Set-based similarity |
| 7 | **Cosine Similarity (Vector)** | $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\vert \vec{A} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{B} \vert}$ | $\vec{A}, \vec{B}$ = embedding vectors | NLP embeddings |
| 8 | **Path Length Similarity** | $sim_{path} = \dfrac{1}{1 + path(c_1, c_2)}$ | $path$ = shortest path edges | Graph-based |
| 9 | **TF-IDF Weight** | $w_{t,d} = tf_{t,d} \cdot \log \dfrac{N}{df_t}$ | $tf$ = term freq, $df$ = doc freq | Concept importance |
| 10 | **Jaccard Index (Patients)** | $J(P_1, P_2) = \dfrac{\vert D_1 \cap D_2 \vert}{\vert D_1 \cup D_2 \vert}$ | $D_i$ = diagnosis sets | Patient similarity |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Aid:** Resnik uses IC, Wu-Palmer uses depth, Lin normalizes Resnik, Jiang-Conrath is a *distance* (not similarity). Always remember: **higher IC = more specific concept = less frequent.**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Semantic Framework |
|--------|----------------------------------|
| **Clinical Decision Support (CDS)** | Suggests treatments using ontology-based drug-disease matching |
| **Drug Repurposing** | Mining semantic relations between drugs, targets, and diseases |
| **Patient Cohort Identification** | SNOMED-based queries for trial recruitment |
| **Cross-border EHR Exchange** | HL7 FHIR + SNOMED CT enables semantic interoperability |
| **Public Health Surveillance** | Real-time monitoring of disease outbreaks via NLP + ICD codes |
| **Pharmacovigilance** | Detect adverse drug events from social media + clinical notes |
| **Medical Imaging Semantics** | RadLex ontology for standardizing radiology report terms |
| **Genomics** | HPO (Human Phenotype Ontology) for phenotype-disease association |

---

## 2.5 Healthcare Ontologies — Comparative Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Comparison Table for KTU Multiple Choice / Short Answer Questions**

| Ontology | Full Form | Scope | Granularity | Example |
|----------|-----------|-------|-------------|---------|
| **SNOMED CT** | Systematized Nomenclature of Medicine — Clinical Terms | Clinical concepts | Very high (~350,000 concepts) | `22298006` = Myocardial Infarction |
| **ICD-10/11** | International Classification of Diseases | Diagnoses & causes of death | Moderate (~72,000 codes) | `I21.0` = STEMI |
| **LOINC** | Logical Observation Identifiers Names and Codes | Lab tests & clinical observations | High (~100,000 codes) | `33747-0` = General appearance |
| **MeSH** | Medical Subject Headings | Biomedical literature indexing | High (~28,000 terms) | `D009203` = Myocardial Infarction |
| **RxNorm** | — | Medications & clinical drugs | High | `197361` = Aspirin 81mg |
| **HPO** | Human Phenotype Ontology | Phenotypic abnormalities | Very high (~18,000 terms) | `HP:0001657` = Long QT syndrome |
| **UMLS** | Unified Medical Language System | Meta-thesaurus integrating others | Meta-level | `C0027051` = MI (CUI) |
| **RadLex** | Radiology Lexicon | Radiology-specific | Moderate | `RID480` = pulmonary edema |
| **GO** | Gene Ontology | Genes & proteins | Domain-specific | `GO:0006915` = apoptosis |
| **FMA** | Foundational Model of Anatomy | Human anatomy | Anatomical | `FMA:7088` = Liver |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Information Content (IC) of a Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Why derive this?** IC is the foundation of every semantic similarity measure. KTU may ask to compute IC given a corpus.

### Problem Statement
Given a medical corpus where the concept "Pneumonia" appears 200 times out of 10,000 total disease mentions, compute the **Information Content** of "Pneumonia."

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the corpus probability of the concept.**

In a hierarchical ontology (like SNOMED CT), the probability of a concept $c$ is the probability of any instance being annotated with $c$ or any of its descendants in the corpus.

$$
P(c) = \frac{\text{freq}(c)}{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

Given: $\text{freq}(\text{Pneumonia}) = 200$, $N = 10{,}000$.

$$
P(\text{Pneumonia}) = \frac{200}{10{,}000} = 0.02
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the IC formula.**

$$
IC(c) = -\log_2 P(c)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerically.**

$$
IC(\text{Pneumonia}) = -\log_2(0.02)
$$

Using $\log_2(0.02) \approx -5.6438$:

$$
IC(\text{Pneumonia}) = -(-5.6438) = 5.6438 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

A higher IC value (e.g., 5.64 bits) means the concept is **more specific** and **less frequent** in the corpus. More general concepts (like "Disease") have lower IC (closer to 0).

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** A concept at the *root* of an ontology (e.g., "SNOMED CT root") has $P = 1$, hence $IC = 0$. A highly specific leaf concept (e.g., "Acute Necrotizing Ulcerative Gingivitis") has very low $P$, hence very high $IC$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Resnik Semantic Similarity

### Problem Statement
Compute the Resnik similarity between **"Pneumonia"** ($c_1$) and **"Bronchitis"** ($c_2$) given:

- $IC(\text{Pneumonia}) = 5.64$ bits
- $IC(\text{Bronchitis}) = 5.20$ bits
- The Least Common Subsumer (LCS) in SNOMED CT is **"Lower Respiratory Tract Infection"**, with $IC = 4.10$ bits.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the Resnik formula.**

$$
sim_{Resnik}(c_1, c_2) = IC(LCS(c_1, c_2))
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the LCS.**

From the SNOMED CT hierarchy:
- Pneumonia $\rightarrow$ Lower Respiratory Tract Infection $\rightarrow$ Respiratory Disease
- Bronchitis $\rightarrow$ Lower Respiratory Tract Infection $\rightarrow$ Respiratory Disease

LCS = "Lower Respiratory Tract Infection"

**Step 3 — Apply the formula.**

$$
sim_{Resnik}(\text{Pneumonia}, \text{Bronchitis}) = IC(\text{Lower Respiratory Tract Infection})
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**

$$
sim_{Resnik} = 4.10 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

Resnik similarity is **not bounded** between 0 and 1 (it returns raw IC of LCS). Higher value = more semantically similar. Here, 4.10 bits indicates moderate-to-high similarity because both conditions affect the lower respiratory tract.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse Resnik (similarity, higher = more similar) with Jiang-Conrath (distance, lower = more similar). Don't mix them up in exams.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Lin Semantic Similarity (Normalized)

### Problem Statement
Using the values from Section 3.2, compute the **Lin similarity** between "Pneumonia" and "Bronchitis."

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the Lin formula.**

$$
sim_{Lin}(c_1, c_2) = \frac{2 \cdot IC(LCS)}{IC(c_1) + IC(c_2)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values.**

$IC(c_1) = 5.64$, $IC(c_2) = 5.20$, $IC(LCS) = 4.10$.

$$
sim_{Lin} = \frac{2 \times 4.10}{5.64 + 5.20}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator.**

$$
2 \times 4.10 = 8.20
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator.**

$$
5.64 + 5.20 = 10.84
$$

**Step 5 — Final division.**

$$
sim_{Lin} = \frac{8.20}{10.84} \approx 0.7565
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**

Lin similarity is bounded in $[0, 1]$. A value of **0.7565** indicates high semantic similarity between Pneumonia and Bronchitis, which aligns with clinical intuition — both are lower respiratory infections with overlapping symptoms (cough, dyspnea).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Wu-Palmer Structural Similarity

### Problem Statement
Given the SNOMED CT hierarchy depths:
- $depth(\text{Pneumonia}) = 8$
- $depth(\text{Bronchitis}) = 7$
- $depth(\text{LCS "Lower Respiratory Tract Infection"}) = 6$

Compute the **Wu-Palmer similarity.**

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the Wu-Palmer formula.**

$$
sim_{WP}(c_1, c_2) = \frac{2 \cdot depth(LCS)}{depth(c_1) + depth(c_2)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
sim_{WP} = \frac{2 \times 6}{8 + 7}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator.**

$$
2 \times 6 = 12
$$

**Step 4 — Denominator.**

$$
8 + 7 = 15
$$

**Step 5 — Final result.**

$$
sim_{WP} = \frac{12}{15} = 0.80
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**

A Wu-Palmer score of **0.80** confirms high structural similarity — both concepts are at similar depth levels and share a deep common ancestor. Wu-Palmer does *not* require corpus statistics, making it useful when no corpus is available.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Jaccard Similarity Between Two Patients

### Problem Statement
Two patients have the following diagnosis concept sets (using SNOMED CT IDs):

- **Patient A:** {Diabetes, Hypertension, Obesity, Hyperlipidemia} → $D_A$
- **Patient B:** {Diabetes, Hypertension, Asthma, Hyperlipidemia} → $D_B$

Compute the **Jaccard similarity** between them.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the Jaccard formula.**

$$
J(A, B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the intersection.**

$$
A \cap B = \{\text{Diabetes, Hypertension, Hyperlipidemia}\}
$$

$$
\vert A \cap B \vert = 3
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the union.**

$$
A \cup B = \{\text{Diabetes, Hypertension, Obesity, Hyperlipidemia, Asthma}\}
$$

$$
\vert A \cup B \vert = 5
$$

**Step 4 — Final calculation.**

$$
J(A, B) = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

A Jaccard similarity of **0.60** indicates substantial clinical overlap. This metric is widely used in:
- Patient cohort identification
- K-nearest-neighbor disease prediction
- Treatment recommendation systems

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Similarity Measure

| Measure | Input Required | Output Range | Best Used When |
|---------|---------------|--------------|----------------|
| **Resnik** | Corpus frequencies | $[0, \infty)$ | You have large annotated corpus |
| **Lin** | Corpus frequencies | $[0, 1]$ | You want bounded, normalized score |
| **Jiang-Conrath** | Corpus frequencies | $[0, \infty)$ | You need a *distance* metric |
| **Wu-Palmer** | Ontology structure only | $[0, 1]$ | No corpus available |
| **Path-based** | Ontology structure only | $(0, 1]$ | Quick coarse filtering |
| **Jaccard** | Concept sets | $[0, 1]$ | Set-based patient comparison |
| **Cosine** | Vector embeddings | $[-1, 1]$ | Deep learning / embeddings |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If a KTU question says "compute the semantic similarity using Resnik's measure" — always identify the **LCS** first, then look up its IC. Most marks are lost in the LCS identification step.
